cases.title: Persistent Facial Breakouts in a Teenager
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening facial breakouts despite using an over-the-counter wash for several months.
cases.history: A 16-year-old girl presents with a 14-month history of facial breakouts that have steadily worsened over the past 6 months. She has tried an over-the-counter 2.5% benzoyl peroxide wash daily for the last 4 months with minimal improvement and is increasingly self-conscious at school. The lesions are most prominent on her forehead, cheeks, and chin, and she notes a few tender, red bumps along her jawline. She denies oral lesions, joint pain, or excessive hair growth. Menarche was at age 12 and her periods are regular; she is not sexually active and uses no contraception. She takes no prescription medications and uses only the benzoyl peroxide wash and a non-comedogenic moisturizer. She has no known drug allergies. She is a high-school junior, plays soccer, does not smoke, drink, or use recreational drugs. Family history is notable for severe teenage acne in her mother. Review of systems is otherwise negative, with no weight changes, hirsutism, or menstrual irregularity.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent in no distress. Skin exam shows numerous open and closed comedones across the forehead, nose, and cheeks, with approximately 20-25 inflammatory papules and several small pustules concentrated on the cheeks and jawline. No nodules, cysts, sinus tracts, or scarring are present. There is mild post-inflammatory erythema but no hyperpigmentation. No facial hypertrichosis or hirsutism on the chin or upper lip. No acanthosis nigricans at the neck. The remainder of the skin, including the back and chest, shows only scattered comedones. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the lesion morphology and distribution, which is the most accurate characterization of her condition?
options: Mild comedonal acne with no inflammatory component
Moderate acne vulgaris with both comedonal and inflammatory lesions
Acne rosacea with papulopustular flare
Perioral dermatitis from topical steroid use
stages.explanation: She has a mix of comedones (open and closed) plus 20-25 inflammatory papules and pustules without nodules, scarring, or extensive truncal involvement, defining moderate acne vulgaris. Mild comedonal acne is wrong because a substantial inflammatory papulopustular burden is present. Rosacea is wrong because she has true comedones (absent in rosacea), is an adolescent, and lacks the central-face flushing, telangiectasias, and absence of comedones that define rosacea. Perioral dermatitis is wrong because lesions are diffuse rather than clustered around the mouth/nasolabial folds, and there is no history of topical steroid exposure. Accurate severity grading drives therapy: moderate acne warrants combination topical therapy rather than monotherapy.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which set of factors best explains the pathogenesis of her acne?
options: Demodex mite overgrowth with vascular hyperreactivity
Autoimmune destruction of sebaceous glands
Follicular hyperkeratinization, increased sebum, Cutibacterium acnes, and inflammation
IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to a contact allergen
stages.explanation: Acne vulgaris results from four interacting factors: androgen-driven sebum production, abnormal follicular hyperkeratinization producing the microcomedo, colonization by Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes, and resulting inflammation. Demodex/vascular hyperreactivity describes rosacea, not adolescent acne. There is no autoimmune destruction of sebaceous glands in acne; sebaceous activity is actually increased. IgE-mediated contact hypersensitivity describes allergic contact dermatitis, which presents with pruritic eczematous or vesicular lesions in an exposure distribution, not comedones and pustules. Understanding this pathway explains why retinoids (normalize keratinization) and antimicrobials (target C. acnes and inflammation) are used together.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management for her moderate acne after failed OTC benzoyl peroxide?
options: Topical retinoid (adapalene) plus a topical antibiotic (clindamycin), continuing benzoyl peroxide
Oral isotretinoin started immediately
Topical antibiotic monotherapy with erythromycin
Reassurance and continued OTC benzoyl peroxide alone
stages.explanation: Moderate acne with comedonal and inflammatory lesions is best treated with combination topical therapy: a topical retinoid (e.g., adapalene) to target hyperkeratinization plus a topical antibiotic (clindamycin), with benzoyl peroxide continued to reduce antibiotic resistance. Oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe nodulocystic or treatment-refractory acne or scarring disease and requires iPLEDGE monitoring; it is premature here. Topical antibiotic monotherapy promotes C. acnes resistance and is discouraged; antibiotics should always be paired with benzoyl peroxide and/or a retinoid. Continuing OTC benzoyl peroxide alone has already failed over 4 months and is inadequate for moderate disease.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important to set expectations and improve adherence to her new regimen?
options: She should expect complete clearance within one week
She should stop the retinoid permanently if mild dryness or peeling occurs
Improvement typically takes 8-12 weeks, and initial dryness/irritation is common and manageable
She must avoid all sun exposure indefinitely because acne is caused by sunlight
stages.explanation: Acne therapies act slowly; meaningful improvement generally takes 8-12 weeks, so setting realistic expectations and warning that retinoids commonly cause early dryness, peeling, and transient irritation (and sometimes a brief flare) is key to adherence. Expecting clearance in one week leads to premature discontinuation. Stopping the retinoid for mild dryness is unnecessary; irritation is managed by moisturizers, every-other-night application, and applying to dry skin. Sunlight does not cause acne, though retinoids increase photosensitivity, so daily sunscreen is advised rather than total sun avoidance. Counseling on the lag to response and tolerability is the single most impactful adherence intervention.

pearls: Always pair topical (or oral) antibiotics with benzoyl peroxide and/or a retinoid to limit C. acnes resistance; avoid antibiotic monotherapy.
Topical retinoids are the backbone of acne therapy because they normalize follicular keratinization and are both comedolytic and anti-inflammatory.
Counsel patients that acne treatments take 8-12 weeks to work and that early retinoid-related dryness/irritation is expected and manageable.
Reserve oral isotretinoin for severe nodulocystic, scarring, or refractory acne, with mandatory iPLEDGE pregnancy-prevention monitoring.

cases.title: Itchy Recurrent Rash in a Preschooler
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent itchy rash behind the knees and elbows that keeps coming back.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for a chronic, intensely itchy rash that has waxed and waned since infancy. The current flare started 2 weeks ago and involves the creases behind both knees and the inner elbows. The itching is worst at night and disrupts his sleep; his mother reports he scratches until the skin weeps. Flares are triggered by hot weather, sweating, and a wool sweater he wore last winter. As an infant he had widespread dry, scaly cheeks. He was diagnosed with asthma at age 3 and has seasonal allergic rhinitis in spring. He takes albuterol as needed and an oral antihistamine during flares; the family applies fragrance-free emollients inconsistently. He has no known drug allergies. He attends preschool and lives at home with his parents and a cat. Both his mother (eczema as a child) and father (allergic rhinitis) have atopic conditions. Immunizations are up to date. Review of systems is negative for fever, oozing pus, or systemic symptoms.
cases.exam: Active, interactive child in no distress, scratching intermittently. Skin shows symmetric, ill-defined erythematous patches with scale and excoriations in the antecubital and popliteal fossae. There are areas of lichenification (thickened skin with accentuated markings) from chronic rubbing, plus dry, rough skin (xerosis) diffusely over the trunk and extensor surfaces. There is no honey-colored crust, no fluctuant or pustular lesions, and no surrounding cellulitic erythema or warmth. Dennie-Morgan infraorbital folds are present. No lymphadenopathy. Mucous membranes are clear and the diaper area is spared.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this child's chronic, relapsing, flexural, intensely pruritic rash?
options: Atopic dermatitis
Allergic contact dermatitis to nickel
Tinea corporis
Scabies
stages.explanation: Chronic, relapsing, intensely pruritic eczematous dermatitis in a flexural distribution in a child with the atopic triad (eczema, asthma, allergic rhinitis) and a family history of atopy is classic atopic dermatitis. The early infantile facial/extensor pattern shifting to flexural involvement in childhood and Dennie-Morgan folds further support it. Nickel contact dermatitis is localized to sites of metal contact (e.g., belt buckle, jewelry), not symmetric flexures. Tinea corporis produces annular plaques with a raised scaly border and central clearing, often single or few, not symmetric flexural eczema. Scabies favors web spaces, wrists, axillae, and genitals with burrows and often affects multiple household members, which is not described here.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which underlying mechanism most directly contributes to the dryness and barrier dysfunction seen in atopic dermatitis?
options: Deficiency of melanin in the epidermis
Filaggrin gene mutations impairing the skin barrier with Th2-skewed inflammation
Complement-mediated destruction of the basement membrane
Autoantibodies against desmoglein 3
stages.explanation: Atopic dermatitis is driven by epidermal barrier dysfunction, classically associated with loss-of-function filaggrin mutations, combined with a Th2-skewed immune response (IL-4, IL-13) that promotes inflammation, pruritus, and increased transepidermal water loss. This explains the xerosis, susceptibility to irritants/allergens, and chronic itch-scratch cycle. Melanin deficiency causes pigmentary disorders, not eczema. Complement-mediated basement membrane destruction and anti-desmoglein 3 autoantibodies describe autoimmune blistering diseases (e.g., bullous pemphigoid, pemphigus vulgaris), which present with bullae or erosions, not pruritic flexural eczema. Targeting barrier repair (emollients) and Th2 inflammation (topical anti-inflammatories) follows directly from this mechanism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this acute flare plus long-term control?
options: Oral prednisone taper for 3 weeks
Topical antifungal cream twice daily
Liberal daily emollients plus a low-to-medium potency topical corticosteroid for the flare
Systemic antibiotics empirically for all flares
stages.explanation: First-line management of atopic dermatitis combines consistent skin barrier repair with liberal emollients and topical anti-inflammatory therapy; a low-to-medium potency topical corticosteroid (e.g., hydrocortisone or triamcinolone, matched to body site) treats the acute flare. Maintenance relies on daily moisturizers and trigger avoidance. Systemic prednisone causes rebound flares on withdrawal and is avoided for routine pediatric eczema. Topical antifungals treat tinea, not eczema. Empiric systemic antibiotics are unwarranted without signs of secondary bacterial infection (honey-colored crust, pustules, spreading warmth), which are absent here. Topical calcineurin inhibitors are useful steroid-sparing options for sensitive sites like the face/folds.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The mother asks what skin infection her son is most at risk for. Which is the most characteristic infectious complication of atopic dermatitis?
options: Disseminated herpes simplex causing eczema herpeticum
Necrotizing fasciitis
Cutaneous larva migrans
Erythema migrans of Lyme disease
stages.explanation: The impaired barrier in atopic dermatitis predisposes to infection. Secondary Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization (honey-colored crust) is most common, but the classic dangerous complication is eczema herpeticum: disseminated herpes simplex producing monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions, fever, and malaise, which is a dermatologic emergency requiring systemic acyclovir. Necrotizing fasciitis is a rare deep soft-tissue infection unrelated to baseline eczema risk. Cutaneous larva migrans (serpiginous tracks) follows hookworm larva exposure, typically on the feet, not a complication of eczema. Erythema migrans is the expanding target rash of Lyme disease from a tick bite, unrelated to atopic dermatitis. Recognizing rapidly worsening, clustered vesicular lesions in an eczematous child prompts urgent antiviral therapy.

pearls: The atopic triad (atopic dermatitis, asthma, allergic rhinitis) plus a flexural, intensely pruritic, relapsing rash strongly suggests atopic dermatitis.
Cornerstones of therapy are liberal daily emollients for barrier repair plus site-appropriate topical corticosteroids (or calcineurin inhibitors) for flares.
Filaggrin barrier dysfunction and Th2 inflammation explain the xerosis, pruritus, and susceptibility to irritants and infection.
Suspect eczema herpeticum when monomorphic punched-out vesicles, fever, and rapid worsening appear; treat urgently with systemic acyclovir.

cases.title: An Expanding Ring-Shaped Rash on a Wrestler
cases.chiefComplaint: A growing, itchy, ring-shaped patch on the forearm that is clearing in the center.
cases.history: A 22-year-old collegiate wrestler presents with a single itchy patch on his right forearm that appeared 10 days ago and has slowly enlarged. He first noticed a small red scaly spot that expanded outward into a ring, with the center now looking nearly normal. It is mildly itchy but not painful. He has not tried any treatment. He reports that two teammates had similar rashes recently, and he frequently practices skin-to-skin on shared mats. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no drug allergies. He showers after practice but admits he often reuses the same towel and gym clothes. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and is sexually active with one partner using condoms. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is negative for fever, joint pain, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, or systemic symptoms. He denies new soaps, detergents, plants, or jewelry contact at the site, and the lesion has not blistered or weeped.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young man in no distress. On the right volar forearm there is a single, well-demarcated annular plaque approximately 4 cm in diameter with a raised, erythematous, scaly active border and central clearing. There are no pustules, vesicles, honey-colored crust, or burrows. No satellite lesions. The scalp, nails, feet, and groin appear normal. No regional lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the skin exam is unremarkable, with no other annular lesions, no target lesions, and no mucosal involvement.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains this annular plaque with a raised scaly border and central clearing?
options: Nummular eczema
Tinea corporis (dermatophyte infection)
Granuloma annulare
Erythema migrans
stages.explanation: An expanding annular plaque with a raised, scaly, active leading edge and central clearing in a wrestler exposed to teammates with similar lesions is classic tinea corporis caused by dermatophytes spread by skin and fomite contact ("tinea gladiatorum"). Nummular eczema forms coin-shaped, uniformly scaly/crusted plaques without true central clearing and is usually multiple and very pruritic. Granuloma annulare produces an annular ring of firm dermal papules but is typically non-scaly and asymptomatic. Erythema migrans expands as an erythematous patch (often with central clearing) but is not scaly, is associated with a tick bite and Lyme exposure, and is usually not itchy. The scaly advancing border is the key discriminator pointing to a dermatophyte.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: Which bedside test best confirms the diagnosis?
options: Tzanck smear of a lesion
Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation of skin scrapings from the active border
Patch testing
Wood lamp examination expecting bright green fluorescence
stages.explanation: A KOH preparation of scrapings taken from the active scaly border dissolves keratinocytes and reveals branching, septate hyphae, confirming a dermatophyte infection quickly and inexpensively. A Tzanck smear detects multinucleated giant cells in herpes/varicella, not dermatophytes. Patch testing evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, a different mechanism, and would not identify fungus. Wood lamp is unreliable for tinea corporis: most dermatophytes (e.g., Trichophyton) do not fluoresce, so a negative lamp does not exclude infection (it is more useful in certain tinea capitis cases). Scraping the leading edge maximizes the yield of viable hyphae for the KOH prep.

stages.label: BEDSIDE TESTING
stages.content:
KOH preparation (active border scraping): numerous branching, septate hyphae crossing keratinocyte borders
No pseudohyphae or budding yeast
No multinucleated giant cells
Fungal culture: pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this single localized lesion, what is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical antifungal such as terbinafine or clotrimazole
Topical high-potency corticosteroid
Oral acyclovir
Topical mupirocin
stages.explanation: Localized tinea corporis is treated with a topical antifungal such as terbinafine, clotrimazole, or another azole applied to the lesion and a margin of surrounding skin for 2-4 weeks. Topical corticosteroids alone worsen and mask dermatophyte infection ("tinea incognito"), altering its appearance and allowing spread; they are avoided. Oral acyclovir treats herpesvirus infections, not fungi. Mupirocin is an antibacterial for impetigo or localized staph infection and has no antifungal activity. Oral antifungals (terbinafine, itraconazole) are reserved for extensive, recurrent, or follicular involvement, or tinea capitis/onychomycosis, which are not present here.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point best prevents recurrence and team transmission?
options: He may return to wrestling immediately with no precautions
Cover or treat lesions, avoid skin-to-skin contact until treated, and disinfect shared mats and gear; do not share towels
Prophylactic oral antibiotics for the whole team
Bleach baths daily for six months
stages.explanation: Tinea gladiatorum spreads via skin-to-skin contact and contaminated mats, towels, and gear, so prevention includes covering or adequately treating lesions before return to contact, withholding from skin-to-skin practice until treated per athletic guidelines, disinfecting mats and equipment, and not sharing towels or clothing. Returning immediately without precautions risks infecting teammates. Prophylactic oral antibiotics target bacteria, not dermatophytes, and are inappropriate. Daily bleach baths for six months are used to reduce staphylococcal colonization in recurrent skin infections, not to prevent dermatophyte spread, and the prolonged course is unwarranted here. Hygiene and contact precautions are the highest-yield interventions.

pearls: An annular plaque with a raised, scaly active border and central clearing is the classic morphology of tinea corporis.
Confirm with a KOH prep of scrapings from the active edge showing branching septate hyphae; Wood lamp is unreliable for tinea corporis.
Never treat suspected tinea with a topical steroid alone, which causes tinea incognito by masking and worsening the infection.
In contact-sport athletes (tinea gladiatorum), prevent spread by treating/covering lesions, disinfecting mats, and avoiding shared towels and gear.

cases.title: Flaky, Greasy Scalp and Facial Redness
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent flaking and redness of the scalp, eyebrows, and sides of the nose.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents with several months of flaking and mild itching of the scalp accompanied by redness and greasy scale along his eyebrows and the creases beside his nose. The flaking worsens in winter and during periods of stress and lack of sleep, and improves somewhat in summer. He has tried a cosmetic shampoo without lasting benefit. He denies pain, pustules, or hair loss. He has no significant medical history, is not immunocompromised, and reports no recent travel. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He works long hours as a software engineer, drinks coffee heavily, sleeps poorly, and is under deadline stress. He does not smoke or use drugs and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history includes a brother with dandruff. Review of systems is negative for fever, joint pain, nail changes, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or weight loss. He has no known HIV risk factors and no neurologic symptoms such as tremor or rigidity.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. Scalp shows diffuse fine-to-greasy white-yellow scale on a mildly erythematous base without discrete plaques or alopecia. The medial eyebrows, glabella, and nasolabial folds have erythema with greasy yellowish scale. The external ear canals and retroauricular folds show mild scaling. There is no silvery plaque on extensor elbows or knees, no nail pitting or oil spots, and no scalp boggy or pustular areas. No facial telangiectasias or comedones. No malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds. The trunk shows minimal presternal scaling. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits greasy yellow scale on the scalp, eyebrows, and nasolabial folds?
options: Plaque psoriasis
Seborrheic dermatitis
Systemic lupus erythematosus (malar rash)
Rosacea
stages.explanation: Greasy yellow-white scale with erythema in sebum-rich areas (scalp, eyebrows, glabella, nasolabial folds, ears) that fluctuates with stress and season is classic seborrheic dermatitis, linked to Malassezia yeast. Plaque psoriasis produces well-demarcated plaques with thick silvery scale on extensor surfaces and may show nail pitting; the distribution and greasy scale here favor seborrhea. Lupus malar rash characteristically spares the nasolabial folds (the opposite of this patient) and is associated with photosensitivity and systemic features. Rosacea causes central-face erythema, flushing, telangiectasias, and papulopustules without greasy scale and typically spares the scalp. The seborrheic distribution and scale quality are the discriminators.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which factor is most directly implicated in the pathogenesis of seborrheic dermatitis?
options: Overgrowth/host response to Malassezia (Pityrosporum) yeast in sebaceous areas
Dermatophyte invasion of the stratum corneum
Type IV hypersensitivity to nickel
Autoantibodies to type VII collagen
stages.explanation: Seborrheic dermatitis is associated with an inflammatory host response to commensal Malassezia (Pityrosporum) yeast and its lipase byproducts in sebum-rich skin, which is why antifungal/anti-yeast agents are effective. Dermatophyte invasion causes tinea, producing annular scaly plaques, not seborrheic distribution. Type IV hypersensitivity to nickel causes allergic contact dermatitis localized to contact sites. Autoantibodies to type VII collagen cause epidermolysis bullosa acquisita, a blistering disease. The role of Malassezia explains the response to ketoconazole and other antifungal shampoos. Note that flares can be more severe and refractory in Parkinson disease and in HIV/immunosuppression, prompting consideration of those conditions when atypical.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his scalp and facial seborrheic dermatitis?
options: Oral terbinafine for 3 months
Topical antifungal (e.g., ketoconazole shampoo/cream), with short-term low-potency topical steroid for facial inflammation
High-potency topical steroid to the face indefinitely
Oral isotretinoin
stages.explanation: First-line therapy is topical antifungals such as ketoconazole (or selenium sulfide/zinc pyrithione) shampoo for the scalp and ketoconazole cream for the face, with a brief course of low-potency topical corticosteroid (e.g., hydrocortisone) or a topical calcineurin inhibitor to calm facial inflammation during flares. Oral terbinafine treats dermatophyte/nail infections and is not indicated. High-potency steroids on the face risk atrophy, telangiectasia, and perioral dermatitis, especially with chronic use, so they are avoided. Oral isotretinoin is for severe acne, not seborrheic dermatitis. Seborrheic dermatitis is chronic and relapsing, so intermittent maintenance antifungal shampoo helps prevent recurrence.

stages.label: CLINICAL REASONING
stages.question: Which finding, if present, should prompt evaluation for an underlying systemic condition such as HIV or Parkinson disease?
options: Improvement of the rash during summer months
Mild itching of the scalp
Unusually severe, extensive, or treatment-refractory seborrheic dermatitis
A family member with simple dandruff
stages.explanation: Severe, widespread, or refractory seborrheic dermatitis can be a clue to underlying immunosuppression (notably HIV, where seborrhea is more prevalent and severe) or neurologic disease such as Parkinson disease (related to increased sebum and reduced facial movement), warranting a directed history and appropriate testing. Seasonal improvement in summer is typical of ordinary seborrheic dermatitis and is reassuring. Mild scalp itching is a common, nonspecific feature of routine disease. A relative with dandruff reflects the benign, familial tendency to seborrhea and does not signal systemic illness. Atypical severity or recalcitrance is the red flag that should broaden the differential.

pearls: Seborrheic dermatitis favors sebum-rich sites: scalp, eyebrows, glabella, nasolabial folds, ears, and presternal chest, with greasy yellow scale.
It reflects an inflammatory response to Malassezia yeast, so topical antifungals (ketoconazole, selenium sulfide, zinc pyrithione) are first-line.
Lupus malar rash spares the nasolabial folds, whereas seborrheic dermatitis characteristically involves them, an easy bedside discriminator.
Severe or refractory seborrheic dermatitis should prompt consideration of HIV or Parkinson disease.

cases.title: Thick Scaly Patches on the Elbows and Knees
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent thick, scaly patches on the elbows, knees, and scalp.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents with well-defined scaly plaques on his elbows, knees, and scalp that have been present for over a year and slowly enlarged. The plaques are mildly itchy and occasionally crack and bleed when scratched. He notes that the rash flared after a recent streptococcal sore throat and worsens with winter dryness and stress. He has tried emollients without significant benefit. He reports occasional stiffness and aching in his fingers in the morning. Past medical history includes obesity and borderline hypertension. He takes no regular medications other than occasional ibuprofen and has no known drug allergies. He is a smoker (10 pack-years) and drinks 3-4 beers on weekends. He works in construction. Family history is significant for a father with similar lifelong skin plaques. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or eye redness, though he reports pitting changes in a few fingernails.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no acute distress. Skin shows well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with thick, silvery-white scale on the extensor elbows and knees and over the scalp at the hairline. Gentle removal of scale produces pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign). Several fingernails show fine pitting and a few small "oil-drop" yellow-brown spots. There is symmetric plaque distribution and a few small plaques over the lumbosacral region. No pustules, no honey-colored crust, no annular central clearing. Mild tenderness without swelling at a few distal interphalangeal joints. No facial greasy scale, no nasolabial involvement. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains well-demarcated silvery plaques on extensor surfaces with a positive Auspitz sign?
options: Seborrheic dermatitis
Plaque psoriasis
Atopic dermatitis
Tinea corporis
stages.explanation: Well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with thick silvery-white scale on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees) and scalp, a positive Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding after scale removal), nail pitting/oil-drop spots, post-streptococcal flare, and a positive family history are hallmark plaque psoriasis. Seborrheic dermatitis has greasy yellow scale in sebaceous areas (scalp, nasolabial folds), not thick silvery extensor plaques. Atopic dermatitis is poorly demarcated, intensely pruritic, and flexural, the opposite distribution. Tinea corporis forms annular plaques with central clearing and a positive KOH, lacking the silvery scale and Auspitz sign. The combination of extensor location, scale quality, Auspitz sign, and nail changes is diagnostic of psoriasis.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which mechanism underlies the plaques of psoriasis?
options: T-cell-driven (Th17/IL-17, TNF) inflammation causing keratinocyte hyperproliferation
IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation
Dermatophyte hyphae invading the stratum corneum
Sebaceous gland atrophy from androgen deficiency
stages.explanation: Psoriasis is an immune-mediated disease in which activated T cells and cytokines (notably the IL-23/Th17/IL-17 axis and TNF-alpha) drive markedly accelerated keratinocyte proliferation and abnormal differentiation, producing thick scale and parakeratosis. This explains the efficacy of biologics targeting TNF, IL-17, and IL-23. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation causes urticaria/anaphylaxis, not psoriatic plaques. Dermatophyte invasion causes tinea, a fungal infection. Sebaceous gland atrophy is unrelated to psoriasis pathogenesis. Understanding the Th17/IL-17 pathway connects the histology (epidermal hyperproliferation) to modern targeted therapy and to the systemic inflammatory comorbidities of psoriasis.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED DISEASE
stages.question: Given his morning finger stiffness and nail changes, which associated condition must be screened for?
options: Psoriatic arthritis
Gout
Septic arthritis
Carpal tunnel syndrome
stages.explanation: Up to about 30% of psoriasis patients develop psoriatic arthritis; morning stiffness, distal interphalangeal joint involvement, and nail pitting/oil-drop changes are classic clues, so screening (history, joint exam, and referral if positive) is essential because early treatment prevents joint damage. Gout typically causes acute monoarticular attacks (often the first MTP) with hyperuricemia and crystals, not symmetric morning DIP stiffness with psoriatic nails. Septic arthritis presents acutely with a hot, swollen, exquisitely painful joint and systemic signs, inconsistent with chronic stiffness. Carpal tunnel syndrome causes median-nerve sensory symptoms (numbness/tingling), not inflammatory joint stiffness or nail changes. The DIP involvement and nail disease specifically point toward psoriatic arthritis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For his localized plaque psoriasis, what is the most appropriate first-line topical therapy?
options: Oral methotrexate as initial therapy
Topical high-potency corticosteroid, often combined with a vitamin D analog (calcipotriene)
Topical antifungal cream
Systemic antibiotics
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for limited plaque psoriasis is topical therapy with a potent corticosteroid, frequently combined with a vitamin D analog (calcipotriene), which together reduce inflammation and normalize keratinocyte proliferation; emollients and coal tar are adjuncts. Oral methotrexate and biologics are reserved for moderate-to-severe disease, extensive body-surface involvement, or psoriatic arthritis, not as first-line for limited plaques. Topical antifungals treat tinea, not psoriasis. Systemic antibiotics have no role in routine plaque psoriasis (although treating a triggering streptococcal infection is reasonable for guttate flares). Phototherapy is an option for more widespread disease. Topical steroid plus vitamin D analog is the standard initial regimen here.

pearls: Plaque psoriasis shows well-demarcated silvery-scaled plaques on extensor surfaces and scalp, with a positive Auspitz sign and nail pitting/oil-drop spots.
Psoriasis is driven by the IL-23/Th17/IL-17 and TNF axis, the basis for modern biologic therapy.
Screen every psoriasis patient for psoriatic arthritis; morning stiffness, DIP involvement, and nail disease are key clues.
First-line for limited disease is a potent topical corticosteroid plus a vitamin D analog (calcipotriene); reserve systemics/biologics for moderate-severe disease.

cases.title: An Itchy Streaky Rash After Yard Work
cases.chiefComplaint: Intensely itchy, blistering rash in streaks on the arms two days after gardening.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents with an intensely itchy rash that appeared on her forearms and lower legs about 2 days after a weekend spent clearing brush and weeds in her backyard. She describes red, swollen streaks with small blisters that have progressively worsened and now ooze clear fluid. The itching is severe and worse at night. She wore short sleeves and shorts and recalls pulling out vines with shiny three-leaflet leaves. She has had a milder version of this years ago after a camping trip. She has no chronic medical conditions, takes only an oral contraceptive pill, and has no known drug allergies. She works as a teacher, does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and has no pets. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, facial or airway swelling, difficulty breathing, wheezing, throat tightness, or lightheadedness. She has had no new soaps, detergents, jewelry, cosmetics, or medications, and reports no involvement of areas covered by clothing.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman, uncomfortable from itching but in no respiratory distress. On the exposed forearms and lower legs there are linear and geometric streaks of erythematous, edematous papulovesicles and tense vesicles, some coalescing into plaques, with areas of weeping and serous crust. The lesions are sharply confined to exposed skin in a clearly external, streaky pattern. There is no facial, lip, tongue, or periorbital swelling, no wheezing or stridor, and no urticarial wheals on covered skin. No honey-colored crust or pustules. Mucous membranes are clear. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains a linear vesicular rash limited to exposed skin after pulling out three-leaflet vines?
options: Acute urticaria
Allergic contact dermatitis to urushiol (poison ivy)
Cellulitis
Bullous impetigo
stages.explanation: Linear/streaky vesicular and papulovesicular eruptions confined to exposed skin appearing 1-3 days after contact with three-leaflet vines (Toxicodendron) are classic allergic contact dermatitis to urushiol (poison ivy/oak). The streaky pattern reflects where the plant brushed the skin. Acute urticaria produces transient migratory wheals that resolve within 24 hours without vesicles or a contact/linear pattern. Cellulitis is a warm, tender, spreading area of confluent erythema with possible fever, not pruritic linear vesicles. Bullous impetigo shows flaccid bullae and honey-colored crust, usually in children, and is not confined to a contact distribution. The linear/exposed distribution plus vesicles after plant exposure is the key.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What immunologic mechanism produces this reaction, and what is its typical timing?
options: Type I IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity within minutes
Type IV cell-mediated (delayed) hypersensitivity peaking 24-72 hours after exposure
Type II antibody-mediated cytotoxicity
Type III immune complex deposition
stages.explanation: Allergic contact dermatitis is a type IV (delayed, T-cell-mediated) hypersensitivity reaction. Urushiol acts as a hapten that binds skin proteins; previously sensitized T cells drive a delayed eczematous response peaking about 24-72 hours after re-exposure, matching her 2-day onset and prior history of sensitization. Type I IgE-mediated reactions (urticaria, anaphylaxis) occur within minutes, not days, and produce wheals rather than vesicles. Type II cytotoxic and type III immune complex reactions underlie diseases like autoimmune hemolysis and serum sickness/vasculitis, not contact dermatitis. The delayed, T-cell-mediated mechanism explains why a prior exposure is required for sensitization and why the rash emerges over days.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her rash is moderate but localized to the extremities without facial or extensive involvement. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Oral antibiotics
Mid-to-high potency topical corticosteroid plus symptomatic antipruritics; wash skin/clothing to remove residual urushiol
Immediate intramuscular epinephrine
Oral antifungal therapy
stages.explanation: Localized allergic contact dermatitis is treated with a mid-to-high potency topical corticosteroid, cool compresses, and antipruritic measures, plus thorough washing of skin, nails, clothing, and tools to remove residual urushiol and prevent further spread. Oral antibiotics are unnecessary without secondary bacterial infection. Intramuscular epinephrine treats anaphylaxis (a type I reaction with airway/cardiovascular compromise), which she does not have. Oral antifungals treat dermatophyte infection, not contact dermatitis. A short oral corticosteroid taper (about 2-3 weeks) is reserved for severe, widespread, or facial/genital involvement; her localized disease is best managed topically.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: Which statement should you give about contagiousness and spread of the rash?
options: The blister fluid is contagious and spreads the rash to others
The rash spreads because the patient is scratching and moving bacteria
New lesions appearing over days reflect variable urushiol dose/absorption, not spread of fluid; the rash is not contagious person-to-person
Cutting the vesicles open will cure it faster
stages.explanation: Patients often think the rash "spreads" from blister fluid, but the staggered appearance of new lesions over several days reflects differences in the amount of urushiol deposited and the rate of skin absorption at various sites, not contagious fluid. Blister fluid does not contain urushiol and cannot transmit the rash to others or to new skin; contact dermatitis is not person-to-person contagious. Residual urushiol on unwashed skin, clothing, pets, or tools can cause new reactions, which is why decontamination matters. Scratching does not move bacteria to cause spread, and lancing vesicles risks secondary infection rather than speeding healing. Clear counseling reduces anxiety and unnecessary isolation.

pearls: Linear/streaky vesicles confined to exposed skin after plant contact are classic for urushiol allergic contact dermatitis.
It is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction peaking 24-72 hours after re-exposure in a sensitized person.
Treat localized disease with potent topical steroids and decontaminate skin, clothing, and tools; reserve oral steroid tapers for severe/facial/widespread cases.
The rash is not contagious; staggered new lesions reflect variable urushiol dose/absorption, and blister fluid does not transmit it.

cases.title: A Rough Bump on a Child's Hand
cases.chiefComplaint: A rough, growing bump on the back of the hand that will not go away.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is brought in by his father for a rough, raised bump on the back of his right hand that appeared about 3 months ago and has slowly grown. It is not painful but occasionally catches on things, and he picks at it. He has no other skin lesions noted by the parent, though he bites his nails and has a small similar bump on a finger. There has been no bleeding except when he picks at it, no rapid growth, and no ulceration. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no drug allergies. He is in fifth grade, swims at a community pool, and shares a household with two siblings. Immunizations are up to date. There is no family history of skin cancer. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, fatigue, or signs of immunodeficiency. The lesion has not been treated. The parent denies any preceding trauma other than the boy's habitual picking and nail-biting.
cases.exam: Healthy, well-nourished boy in no distress. On the dorsum of the right hand there is a single, well-circumscribed, firm, hyperkeratotic (rough, cauliflower-like) papule about 6 mm in diameter, skin-colored to grayish, with a slightly verrucous surface. Paring the surface reveals tiny black dots (thrombosed capillaries) and disrupts the normal skin lines crossing the lesion. A smaller similar papule is present on the adjacent finger. There is no surrounding erythema, warmth, pus, ulceration, or central umbilication. No regional lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the skin exam, including soles and genitals, is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains a hyperkeratotic papule with black dots that interrupt the skin lines?
options: Molluscum contagiosum
Verruca vulgaris (common wart)
Acrochordon (skin tag)
Amelanotic melanoma
stages.explanation: A firm, rough, hyperkeratotic papule with a verrucous surface, tiny black dots (thrombosed capillaries) on paring, and disruption of the normal skin lines is classic verruca vulgaris (common wart) caused by human papillomavirus. Molluscum contagiosum produces smooth, dome-shaped, umbilicated papules with a central plug, not a rough keratotic surface or black dots. An acrochordon is a soft, pedunculated skin-colored tag, usually in skin folds of older or overweight individuals, lacking keratotic surface or thrombosed capillaries. Amelanotic melanoma is rare in children and presents as an enlarging, often ulcerated or color-variegated nodule rather than a verrucous papule with black dots. The black dots and interrupted skin lines are the key clues.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the causative organism, and how is it commonly acquired?
options: Human papillomavirus, spread by direct contact and autoinoculation
Poxvirus, spread by skin-to-skin and fomite contact
Staphylococcus aureus, from skin colonization
Dermatophyte fungus, from contaminated surfaces
stages.explanation: Verruca vulgaris is caused by human papillomavirus (HPV), commonly types 1, 2, 4, and 7, which infects keratinocytes and is spread by direct skin contact, fomites, and autoinoculation; this boy's nail-biting and picking promote spread to adjacent fingers (the additional finger lesion). Poxvirus (molluscum contagiosum virus) causes molluscum, a different morphology. Staphylococcus aureus causes pyogenic infections like impetigo and abscesses, not warts. Dermatophyte fungi cause tinea, presenting with scaly annular plaques, not verrucous papules. Recognizing HPV etiology explains the contagious, autoinoculable nature and informs counseling to discourage picking and nail-biting.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this common wart in an otherwise healthy child?
options: Surgical wide excision with margins
Topical salicylic acid (with or without cryotherapy); reassurance that many warts resolve spontaneously
Oral antiviral therapy
Systemic chemotherapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for common warts is topical salicylic acid, often combined with cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen), along with reassurance that many warts, especially in children, resolve spontaneously over months to a couple of years as immunity develops. Wide surgical excision is unnecessary, leaves scars, and warts can recur at the margins. There is no effective oral antiviral specifically for cutaneous HPV warts; antivirals like acyclovir target herpesviruses. Systemic chemotherapy is grossly inappropriate for a benign wart. Conservative, low-morbidity therapy (salicylic acid +/- cryotherapy) plus patient/parent education about spontaneous resolution and avoiding picking/biting is the standard approach.

stages.label: CLINICAL REASONING
stages.question: A useful bedside maneuver helps distinguish a wart from a callus or corn. Which finding supports the diagnosis of a wart?
options: Skin lines (dermatoglyphics) continue uninterrupted across the lesion
Paring reveals pinpoint black dots and loss of the normal skin lines over the lesion
The lesion blanches completely and is fluctuant
The lesion transilluminates with a penlight
stages.explanation: Warts interrupt the normal skin lines and, on paring the keratotic surface, reveal pinpoint black dots representing thrombosed capillaries, distinguishing them from calluses/corns. In a callus, the normal skin lines (dermatoglyphics) continue uninterrupted across the thickened skin, and paring shows no black dots. Fluctuance and complete blanching suggest a fluid-filled or vascular lesion, not a wart. Transillumination is characteristic of fluid-filled cystic lesions (e.g., a ganglion or hydrocele), not a solid keratotic wart. The black dots plus loss of skin lines is the simple, high-yield bedside discriminator for verruca.

pearls: Common warts are HPV-induced hyperkeratotic papules with thrombosed capillaries (black dots) that interrupt the normal skin lines.
They spread by contact and autoinoculation, so discourage picking and nail-biting to limit new lesions.
First-line therapy is topical salicylic acid with or without cryotherapy; many warts in children resolve spontaneously.
Paring reveals black dots and loss of skin lines in a wart, whereas a callus preserves uninterrupted skin lines without black dots.

cases.title: Small Pearly Bumps on a Young Girl
cases.chiefComplaint: A cluster of small, painless, pearly bumps on the trunk and arm.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is brought in by her mother for a 6-week history of small, painless, flesh-colored bumps on her trunk, axilla, and right upper arm. They started as one or two papules and have slowly increased in number. They are occasionally mildly itchy but not painful, and a few have become slightly red and irritated where she has scratched. She is otherwise well, with normal growth and development. She has mild atopic dermatitis treated with emollients. She takes no medications and has no drug allergies. She attends first grade and swims weekly at a shared pool; a classmate reportedly had similar bumps. Immunizations are current. She is not immunocompromised and has had no unusual or recurrent infections. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, fatigue, or systemic symptoms. There has been no treatment so far, and the bumps have not blistered, crusted with honey-colored material, or ulcerated.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful child in no distress. On the trunk, right axilla, and upper arm there are approximately 12-15 discrete, dome-shaped, firm, flesh-colored to pearly papules 2-5 mm in size, several with a central umbilication (dimple). A few papules are surrounded by mild eczematous erythema (likely related to her atopic dermatitis and scratching). There is no honey-colored crust, no fluctuance or pus, no vesicles, and no mucosal lesions. There are no lesions on the palms or soles. No lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the skin shows mild xerosis consistent with atopic dermatitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains multiple dome-shaped, umbilicated, flesh-colored papules in a child?
options: Verruca vulgaris
Molluscum contagiosum
Closed comedones (acne)
Folliculitis
stages.explanation: Multiple small, firm, dome-shaped, flesh-colored to pearly papules with central umbilication in a young child are characteristic of molluscum contagiosum, a poxvirus infection spread by skin contact, fomites, and autoinoculation, common in children and at shared pools. Verruca vulgaris is rough and hyperkeratotic with black dots, not smooth and umbilicated. Closed comedones are tiny keratin-filled papules on the face in a pilosebaceous distribution, not umbilicated trunk papules in a 6-year-old. Folliculitis presents with pustules or erythematous papules centered on hair follicles, often tender, without the central umbilication. The smooth dome shape with central dimple is the defining feature of molluscum.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What organism causes this condition?
options: A poxvirus (molluscum contagiosum virus)
Human papillomavirus
Herpes simplex virus
Candida albicans
stages.explanation: Molluscum contagiosum is caused by the molluscum contagiosum virus, a poxvirus (a large DNA virus), which infects epidermal keratinocytes and produces the characteristic umbilicated papules; histology shows Henderson-Paterson bodies (molluscum bodies). Human papillomavirus causes warts (rough, hyperkeratotic), not smooth umbilicated papules. Herpes simplex virus causes grouped painful vesicles on an erythematous base, a different morphology and symptom profile. Candida albicans causes intertriginous or mucosal yeast infections with satellite pustules, not discrete pearly umbilicated papules. Identifying the poxvirus etiology underlies the self-limited course and the autoinoculation/contact spread that guides counseling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In this healthy child with mildly symptomatic lesions, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance and observation, since lesions typically self-resolve over months; treat only if bothersome
Oral acyclovir
Wide surgical excision of all lesions
Systemic antifungal therapy
stages.explanation: Molluscum contagiosum is benign and self-limited in immunocompetent children, typically resolving spontaneously over 6-12 months (sometimes longer), so reassurance and observation are appropriate, with active treatment (cryotherapy, cantharidin, curettage) reserved for bothersome, extensive, or cosmetically/socially concerning lesions. Oral acyclovir targets herpesviruses, not poxvirus, and is ineffective. Wide surgical excision is excessive and scarring for a benign self-resolving condition. Systemic antifungals treat fungal infections and have no role. Counseling to avoid scratching, sharing towels, and to manage her coexisting atopic dermatitis (which can promote autoinoculation) helps limit spread while awaiting resolution.

stages.label: CLINICAL REASONING
stages.question: Extensive, atypical, or rapidly progressive molluscum in an older adolescent or adult should prompt consideration of which underlying condition?
options: Iron-deficiency anemia
Immunosuppression such as HIV infection
Hypothyroidism
Vitamin D deficiency
stages.explanation: Widespread, unusually large, facial, or recalcitrant molluscum, particularly in adults or adolescents, can signal underlying immunosuppression such as HIV infection, where giant or numerous lesions occur and may mimic other conditions; this warrants HIV testing and evaluation. Iron-deficiency anemia, hypothyroidism, and vitamin D deficiency are not associated with severe or atypical molluscum presentations. While this 6-year-old has a typical, limited, immunocompetent course, recognizing the red flag of atypical/extensive disease in older or systemically ill patients is an important clinical-reasoning point that links a common skin infection to underlying immune status.

pearls: Molluscum contagiosum presents as smooth, dome-shaped, umbilicated flesh-colored papules, caused by a poxvirus and spread by contact/autoinoculation.
In healthy children it is self-limited, usually resolving over 6-12 months; treat only bothersome or extensive lesions.
Coexisting atopic dermatitis and scratching promote autoinoculation and spread.
Extensive, giant, or recalcitrant molluscum in adolescents/adults should prompt evaluation for immunosuppression such as HIV.

cases.title: Crusty Sores Around a Child's Nose and Mouth
cases.chiefComplaint: Spreading crusted sores around the nose and mouth for several days.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for sores around his nose and mouth that began as small red spots 4 days ago, then developed into oozing lesions with a thick yellowish crust. The lesions have spread slightly and a few new spots appeared near his chin, possibly from scratching. They are mildly itchy but not very painful, and he has remained active and afebrile. He recently had a runny nose, and his mother notes he frequently rubs his nose. He attends kindergarten, where another child reportedly had similar sores. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no drug allergies. Immunizations are current. He has no history of recurrent skin infections or immunodeficiency. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is negative for fever, dark or cola-colored urine, facial swelling, joint pain, or decreased urination. There has been no treatment yet, and no lesions involve the eyes; he has no large flaccid blisters.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile child in no distress. Around the nares and perioral region there are multiple erythematous erosions covered by thick, golden-yellow (honey-colored) crusts, a few with surrounding mild erythema. Some satellite lesions are present on the chin. There are no large flaccid bullae, no deep ulceration, and no surrounding spreading warmth or induration to suggest cellulitis. Mucous membranes and conjunctivae are clear. There is mild submandibular lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the skin exam is unremarkable, with no widespread rash, no nikolsky sign, and no desquamation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains honey-colored crusted erosions around the nose and mouth of a child?
options: Herpes simplex (cold sore)
Non-bullous impetigo
Allergic contact dermatitis
Eczema herpeticum
stages.explanation: Superficial erosions with characteristic thick, golden honey-colored crusts in a perioral/perinasal distribution in an afebrile, well-appearing child are classic non-bullous impetigo, usually due to Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes, often seeded by minor breaks (e.g., nose rubbing). Herpes simplex produces grouped painful vesicles that erode, often recurrent at the same site, without the thick honey crust and typically with a Tzanck/PCR-confirmable cause. Allergic contact dermatitis is pruritic and eczematous/vesicular in a contact distribution, lacking honey-colored crust. Eczema herpeticum is a widespread, monomorphic, punched-out vesicular eruption in an eczematous, often ill-appearing child, which is not present here. The honey-colored crust is the hallmark.

stages.label: MICROBIOLOGY
stages.question: Which organisms are the most common causes of this condition?
options: Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli
Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep)
Candida albicans and Malassezia
Herpes simplex virus and varicella-zoster virus
stages.explanation: Impetigo is most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus, with Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep) also responsible; bullous impetigo specifically results from S. aureus exfoliative toxin. Pseudomonas and E. coli are gram-negative organisms that cause other infections (e.g., hot-tub folliculitis, urinary/GI infections), not typical impetigo. Candida and Malassezia are fungi/yeast causing candidiasis and seborrheic dermatitis/tinea versicolor, not honey-crusted erosions. Herpes simplex and varicella-zoster are viruses producing vesicular eruptions, not the bacterial honey-crusted lesions of impetigo. Knowing the staph/strep etiology directs appropriate antibacterial therapy and explains the post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis risk.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has limited, localized lesions. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical antibiotic such as mupirocin
Oral acyclovir
Topical high-potency corticosteroid
Topical antifungal cream
stages.explanation: Limited, localized non-bullous impetigo is treated with a topical antibiotic such as mupirocin (or retapamulin) applied to the lesions, along with gentle crust removal and hygiene measures. Oral antibiotics (e.g., cephalexin or dicloxacillin; clindamycin or doxycycline if MRSA is suspected) are reserved for numerous lesions, widespread involvement, bullous disease, systemic symptoms, or failure of topical therapy. Oral acyclovir treats herpesvirus infections, not bacterial impetigo. Topical corticosteroids do not treat infection and could worsen it. Topical antifungals target fungal infections and are ineffective against staph/strep. For this localized case, topical mupirocin is the appropriate first-line choice.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which serious complication is most specifically associated with streptococcal impetigo, and what should the family watch for?
options: Acute rheumatic fever, watching for new heart murmur
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, watching for dark/cola-colored urine, facial swelling, or reduced urine output
Guillain-Barre syndrome, watching for ascending weakness
Toxic megacolon, watching for abdominal distension
stages.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is the key complication associated with skin infection by nephritogenic strains of group A strep; families should watch for cola-colored or dark urine, periorbital/facial edema, hypertension, and decreased urine output, typically 1-2 weeks after infection. Importantly, treating the skin infection does not reliably prevent PSGN. Acute rheumatic fever follows streptococcal pharyngitis, not skin infection, so it is not the impetigo-specific risk. Guillain-Barre syndrome is most classically linked to Campylobacter (and other) infections, not strep impetigo. Toxic megacolon is associated with severe colitis (e.g., C. difficile, inflammatory bowel disease), unrelated to impetigo. Counsel on PSGN warning signs.

pearls: Honey-colored crusted erosions, classically perioral/perinasal in children, define non-bullous impetigo.
The usual organisms are Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes; bullous impetigo is caused by S. aureus exfoliative toxin.
Treat limited disease with topical mupirocin; use oral antibiotics for widespread, bullous, or systemic involvement.
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (dark urine, edema) can follow strep impetigo and is not prevented by treating the skin infection; rheumatic fever follows pharyngitis, not skin infection.

cases.title: Sudden Migratory Welts After a New Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, intensely itchy welts all over the body that keep moving and changing.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of sudden, intensely itchy raised welts that appeared on her trunk and then spread to her arms and thighs. She notes that individual welts come and go, with some fading within a few hours while new ones appear elsewhere. She started amoxicillin 2 days ago for a sinus infection. She has no lip, tongue, or throat swelling, no trouble breathing or swallowing, no chest tightness, and no lightheadedness. She has no prior history of similar reactions and no known drug allergies before this. Her only other medication is the amoxicillin. Past medical history is unremarkable. She is a graduate student, does not smoke, drinks socially, and has no recent new foods, insect stings, or other new exposures. Family history includes a mother with seasonal allergies. Review of systems is negative for fever, joint pain, abdominal pain, vomiting, wheezing, and syncope. She denies any individual lesion persisting beyond 24 hours or leaving bruising.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, alert, anxious woman in no respiratory distress with normal voice. Skin shows numerous well-circumscribed, raised, erythematous and edematous wheals (hives) of varying sizes scattered over the trunk and proximal extremities, some with central pallor and surrounding flare; several are coalescing into larger plaques. Lesions blanch with pressure and are intensely pruritic. There is no angioedema of the lips, tongue, or periorbital area, no stridor, wheezing, or accessory muscle use. Oropharynx is clear with no uvular swelling. Lungs are clear; heart is regular without murmur. No purpura, no residual bruising at sites of resolved wheals, and no target lesions or mucosal involvement.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains transient, migratory, intensely pruritic wheals appearing after a new antibiotic?
options: Acute urticaria
Erythema multiforme
Urticarial vasculitis
Anaphylaxis
stages.explanation: Transient, migratory, intensely pruritic, blanching wheals in which individual lesions resolve within 24 hours, arising after a new antibiotic, define acute urticaria (here likely a drug reaction to amoxicillin). Erythema multiforme produces fixed target lesions with a dusky center that persist for days and may involve mucosa, not fleeting wheals. Urticarial vasculitis features wheals that last longer than 24 hours, often burn rather than itch, and leave bruising/purpura/hyperpigmentation, none of which she has. Anaphylaxis requires systemic involvement (airway, breathing, cardiovascular, or GI compromise), which is absent, she is hemodynamically stable with no angioedema or respiratory symptoms. The fleeting (<24 h), blanching, pruritic nature confirms acute urticaria.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which mechanism most directly produces the wheals of acute urticaria?
options: Mast cell degranulation releasing histamine, causing dermal vasodilation and increased vascular permeability
Dermatophyte invasion of the epidermis
Immune complex deposition in vessel walls with leukocytoclasis
Accelerated keratinocyte proliferation
stages.explanation: Urticarial wheals result from mast cell degranulation with release of histamine and other mediators, producing localized dermal vasodilation, increased vascular permeability (edema), and pruritus; this is often IgE-mediated in acute drug/food reactions but can be non-immunologic. This mechanism explains the transient, migratory, blanching wheals and the response to antihistamines. Dermatophyte invasion causes tinea, a scaly fungal infection. Immune complex deposition with leukocytoclasis describes urticarial vasculitis/leukocytoclastic vasculitis, in which lesions persist and leave purpura. Accelerated keratinocyte proliferation underlies psoriasis. Targeting histamine (H1 antihistamines) follows directly from the mast cell/histamine mechanism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is stable with no airway, breathing, or circulatory compromise. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate intramuscular epinephrine and intubation
Stop the amoxicillin and treat with a non-sedating H1 antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine); add a short steroid course if severe
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
Topical antifungal cream
stages.explanation: For acute urticaria without anaphylaxis, the mainstays are removing the likely trigger (discontinue amoxicillin) and giving a second-generation, non-sedating H1 antihistamine such as cetirizine, with up-dosing or a short oral corticosteroid course for more severe or refractory symptoms. Intramuscular epinephrine and airway management are reserved for anaphylaxis with systemic compromise, which she does not have. IV antibiotics are inappropriate and could worsen a drug reaction. Topical antifungals treat fungal infections, not urticaria. She should also be counseled to avoid amoxicillin and related beta-lactams pending allergy evaluation and given clear return precautions for any airway or systemic symptoms.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Before discharge, which instruction is most important for her safety?
options: Continue the amoxicillin to finish the course
Avoid amoxicillin/penicillins, document the allergy, and return immediately for any lip/tongue/throat swelling or breathing difficulty
She may take the medication again if she pre-treats with antihistamine
No follow-up or precautions are needed
stages.explanation: The safest disposition is to stop and avoid the culprit drug (amoxicillin and, pending evaluation, related penicillins), clearly document the drug allergy, and give strict return precautions to seek emergency care immediately for any signs of progression to angioedema or anaphylaxis (lip/tongue/throat swelling, difficulty breathing or swallowing, wheezing, faintness). Continuing or re-challenging with the suspected drug risks a more severe reaction, including anaphylaxis, and pre-treating with antihistamine does not reliably prevent IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. Telling her no precautions are needed is unsafe because urticaria from a drug can herald progression. Documentation and avoidance protect her at future healthcare encounters; outpatient allergy referral can clarify the diagnosis.

pearls: Acute urticaria features transient, migratory, blanching, intensely pruritic wheals in which individual lesions resolve within 24 hours.
Wheals arise from mast cell degranulation and histamine release; first-line therapy is a non-sedating H1 antihistamine after removing the trigger.
Lesions persisting >24 hours, burning rather than itching, or leaving purpura/bruising suggest urticarial vasculitis, not simple urticaria.
Distinguish urticaria from anaphylaxis: airway, breathing, cardiovascular, or GI involvement mandates epinephrine, and patients need drug avoidance plus clear return precautions.

cases.title: Slow-healing shiny patches on both shins
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports gradually enlarging, shiny discolored patches on the fronts of both lower legs that bruise and ulcerate easily.
cases.history: A 43-year-old woman is referred for asymmetric plaques on her shins that began two years ago as small reddish bumps and slowly coalesced and flattened into shiny yellow-brown patches. They are largely asymptomatic but minor knocks cause persistent shallow ulcers that take weeks to heal. She has type 1 diabetes mellitus for 19 years, managed with insulin glargine 24 units nightly and lispro with meals; her last HbA1c was 8.4%. She also has background diabetic retinopathy and early diabetic nephropathy. Medications include lisinopril 10 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. She has no drug allergies. She works as a schoolteacher, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes type 2 diabetes in her mother and Hashimoto thyroiditis in a sister. She has tried over-the-counter hydrocortisone and emollients without improvement and is frustrated by the cosmetic appearance, which she hides under long pants even in summer.
cases.exam: On the bilateral pretibial surfaces are well-demarcated plaques up to 6 cm with a waxy yellow-brown atrophic center, prominent telangiectasias visible through thinned skin, and a slightly raised violaceous-brown border. The central skin is shiny and depressed, and a 1 cm shallow ulcer is present on the right shin. There is reduced sensation to light touch but intact pulses. No comedones, no scaling, no Nikolsky sign. The remainder of the skin, including the face and trunk, is clear; no oral or genital lesions and no nail pitting.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature on examination is MOST characteristic of the suspected diagnosis?
options: Silvery scale that bleeds with removal (Auspitz sign)
Yellow-brown atrophic center with telangiectasias seen through thinned epidermis
Tense subepidermal bullae on an erythematous base
Honey-colored crusting over an erosion
stages.explanation: The combination of a waxy yellow-brown atrophic plaque with visible telangiectasias through epidermal thinning, a raised brownish border, and a tendency to ulcerate is classic for necrobiosis lipoidica, which preferentially affects the pretibial skin of diabetics. Auspitz sign (option B) indicates psoriasis, which produces thick silvery scale rather than atrophy. Tense subepidermal bullae (option D) suggest bullous pemphigoid or porphyria, not atrophic plaques. Honey-colored crusting (option E) is impetigo. The atrophy with telangiectasias, plus this patient's long-standing type 1 diabetes, points strongly toward necrobiosis lipoidica.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis if there is clinical uncertainty?
options: Punch biopsy taken from the active raised border, not the central ulcer
Wood lamp examination of the plaques
Patch testing to a standard allergen series
Doppler ankle-brachial index alone
stages.explanation: Necrobiosis lipoidica is usually a clinical diagnosis, but when atypical a punch biopsy from the inflammatory raised edge (avoiding the atrophic ulcerated center, which heals poorly and is non-diagnostic) shows palisading granulomatous dermatitis with layered necrobiosis of collagen and plasma cells. Wood lamp (option B) is for pigmentary/fluorescent conditions, not granulomas. Patch testing (option C) evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, not relevant here. An ABI (option D) assesses arterial perfusion and may be reasonable adjunctively given ulceration, but it does not confirm the diagnosis; biopsy from the border is the confirmatory step.

stages.label: BIOPSY & LABS
stages.content:
Skin biopsy (raised border): palisading and interstitial granulomatous dermatitis with layered (tiered) necrobiosis of collagen, scattered plasma cells, and thickened dermal blood vessels
HbA1c: 8.4%
Fasting glucose: 168 mg/dL
CBC: within normal limits
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio: 96 mg/g (elevated)
ABI: 1.06 (normal)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for an active, non-ulcerated lesion?
options: Systemic isotretinoin
High-potency topical or intralesional corticosteroid applied to the active border
Oral terbinafine for 12 weeks
Wide local surgical excision of all plaques
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for active necrobiosis lipoidica is potent topical corticosteroids under occlusion or intralesional triamcinolone injected into the inflamed advancing border; the goal is to halt progression, as the atrophic center cannot be restored. Steroids should not be applied to the atrophic center, which would worsen atrophy. Isotretinoin (option A) is not standard. Terbinafine (option C) treats dermatophytes, irrelevant to a granulomatous process. Surgical excision (option D) is generally avoided because poor wound healing in diabetics and the Koebner phenomenon risk new lesions at the surgical site; recurrence is common. Tight glycemic control has not been proven to reverse lesions but is still recommended overall.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The patient asks what the most important long-term complication of these lesions is. What should you counsel her about?
options: Progression to systemic amyloidosis
Chronic ulceration with secondary infection and rare squamous cell carcinoma within long-standing plaques
Transformation into malignant melanoma
Spontaneous resolution within weeks in nearly all patients
stages.explanation: The major long-term concerns are chronic, poorly healing ulceration (often triggered by minor trauma, as in this patient) with secondary bacterial infection, and the rare but reported development of squamous cell carcinoma within long-standing lesions, which warrants biopsy of any non-healing or indurated area. Systemic amyloidosis (option A) is unrelated. Melanoma (option C) does not arise from necrobiosis lipoidica. While up to ~15-20% of lesions may slowly improve over years, the majority are chronic, so 'spontaneous resolution within weeks in nearly all patients' (option D) is incorrect. Ongoing surveillance of ulcerated plaques is essential.

pearls: Necrobiosis lipoidica presents as yellow-brown atrophic pretibial plaques with telangiectasias and a raised violaceous border, classically in long-standing diabetics.
Biopsy, when needed, should be taken from the active border and shows palisading granulomatous dermatitis with layered necrobiosis of collagen and plasma cells.
First-line treatment is potent topical or intralesional corticosteroid to the inflamed edge; surgery is avoided due to poor healing and Koebnerization.
Watch for chronic ulceration, secondary infection, and rare squamous cell carcinoma arising within long-standing lesions.

cases.title: Fragile, blistering skin on the backs of the hands
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports that the skin on the backs of his hands tears and blisters with minor bumps and is slow to heal, worse in summer.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with a several-month history of tense blisters and erosions on the dorsa of both hands, forearms, and the back of his neck. He notes the skin is extremely fragile, peeling away with minor friction, leaving crusts that heal into small white milia and scars. Symptoms flare after weekends working outdoors. He has hepatitis C (untreated, diagnosed 6 years ago) and drinks 6-8 beers daily. He smokes one pack per day. Medications include occasional ibuprofen; he recently started taking an iron supplement for fatigue and uses no sunscreen. He reports increased facial hair and darkening of the skin. No personal or family history of bullous disease. He has no known drug allergies. He has not had recent new medications and denies fever, oral ulcers, or genital lesions. He works in construction and has significant cumulative sun exposure. He has a remote history of a blood transfusion in his twenties.
cases.exam: On the dorsal hands and forearms are several tense vesicles and bullae, multiple superficial erosions with hemorrhagic crusts, and scattered 1-2 mm white milia within healed sites. There is mild hyperpigmentation and increased fine hair (hypertrichosis) over the temples and cheeks. Skin fragility is demonstrable with light lateral pressure. There is no scalp scarring, no mucosal involvement, and no Nikolsky sign on normal-appearing skin. Sclerodermoid plaques are absent. The liver edge is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and mildly firm; no jaundice or ascites.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given photodistributed blisters with skin fragility, milia, and hypertrichosis in a man with hepatitis C and heavy alcohol use, which condition is most likely?
options: Bullous pemphigoid
Porphyria cutanea tarda
Pemphigus vulgaris
Dermatitis herpetiformis
stages.explanation: Photodistributed tense bullae and erosions on sun-exposed skin with marked skin fragility, milia, hypertrichosis, and hyperpigmentation in the setting of hepatitis C, alcohol, iron, and smoking is classic porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT). Bullous pemphigoid (option A) causes tense, intensely pruritic bullae on flexures/trunk in the elderly, not fragility with milia on sun-exposed sites. Pemphigus vulgaris (option C) causes flaccid bullae with mucosal erosions and a positive Nikolsky sign. Dermatitis herpetiformis (option D) produces intensely pruritic grouped papulovesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks), not photodistributed fragile bullae with milia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which single test would most efficiently confirm the diagnosis?
options: Plasma or 24-hour urine porphyrin analysis showing elevated uroporphyrin
Direct immunofluorescence showing linear IgG along the basement membrane
Serum anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies
Tzanck smear of a blister base
stages.explanation: PCT is confirmed by demonstrating markedly elevated uroporphyrins (and 7-carboxylate porphyrins) in plasma or 24-hour urine; the urine often fluoresces coral-pink/red under a Wood lamp. The defect is reduced uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity. Direct immunofluorescence (option B) is used for autoimmune bullous diseases; in PCT, DIF may show nonspecific perivascular/junctional deposits but is not diagnostic. Anti-tTG (option C) screens for celiac disease/dermatitis herpetiformis. A Tzanck smear (option D) detects acantholytic cells or multinucleated giant cells (pemphigus, herpes), not porphyria.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
24-hour urine uroporphyrin: markedly elevated
Urine fluorescence under Wood lamp: coral-pink/red
Plasma porphyrin fluorescence peak: ~620 nm (consistent with PCT)
Ferritin: 720 ng/mL (elevated)
Transferrin saturation: 58%
AST: 88 U/L  ALT: 102 U/L
HCV antibody: positive; HCV RNA: detectable
HIV: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides eliminating triggers, what is the most appropriate first-line therapy for this patient's skin disease?
options: High-dose oral prednisone taper
Repeated therapeutic phlebotomy or low-dose hydroxychloroquine
Oral isotretinoin
Broad-spectrum oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of PCT is removal of triggers (alcohol, smoking, estrogen, iron) plus reducing hepatic iron with repeated therapeutic phlebotomy, or low-dose hydroxychloroquine/chloroquine, which mobilizes hepatic porphyrins. Either is effective; the choice depends on iron status and comorbidity. Strict photoprotection is essential. Systemic steroids (option A) treat autoimmune bullous disease, not PCT. Isotretinoin (option C) has no role. Antibiotics (option D) are only for secondary infection, not the underlying defect. Because hepatitis C drives PCT, treating HCV with direct-acting antivirals is also important and may improve the skin disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation, which additional evaluation is most appropriate?
options: Testing for HFE hemochromatosis gene mutations
Bone marrow biopsy
Immediate iron supplementation
Skin patch testing
stages.explanation: Iron overload aggravates PCT, and HFE hemochromatosis mutations (especially C282Y) are found in a notable proportion of PCT patients; identifying them refines phlebotomy planning and screens family members. This patient also has hepatitis C and heavy alcohol use, all of which raise hepatic iron. A bone marrow biopsy (option B) is unnecessary without cytopenias. Iron supplementation (option C) is contraindicated—he should STOP his iron supplement, as iron worsens PCT. Patch testing (option D) is irrelevant. Confirming HFE status and discontinuing exogenous iron directly target the disease mechanism.

pearls: PCT presents with photodistributed tense bullae, skin fragility, milia, hypertrichosis, and hyperpigmentation on the dorsal hands.
Confirm with elevated urine/plasma uroporphyrins; urine fluoresces coral-pink under a Wood lamp.
Common triggers are alcohol, hepatitis C, estrogen, smoking, and iron overload (check HFE mutations and stop iron supplements).
Treat with phlebotomy or low-dose hydroxychloroquine plus strict photoprotection and management of hepatitis C.
