Start prophylactic anticoagulation in all patients
Apply compression only at night while supine
stages.explanation: Compression therapy is effective for venous insufficiency but can cause ischemic injury if significant peripheral arterial disease is present, so confirming adequate arterial perfusion, by palpable pedal pulses and, when in doubt, an ankle-brachial index, is essential before applying compression. Option 1 is wrong: while DVT should be considered in acute unilateral swelling, her presentation is chronic and bilateral, and duplex is not driven solely by fever. Option 2 is wrong: routine prophylactic anticoagulation for all stasis dermatitis patients is not indicated. Option 3 is wrong: compression is most effective when worn during the day with ambulation and is typically removed at night, the opposite of the option stated. Ensuring arterial flow protects against compression-induced harm.

cases.diagnosis: Stasis dermatitis from chronic venous insufficiency
pearls: Bilateral, symmetric, chronic, afebrile lower-leg erythema favors stasis dermatitis; cellulitis is typically unilateral and acute.
Hemosiderin staining, varicosities, and dependent edema over the medial ankle (gaiter area) point to venous insufficiency.
Treat with leg elevation, compression, emollients, and topical corticosteroids for active dermatitis, not antibiotics.
Confirm adequate arterial perfusion (pulses or ABI) before applying compression to avoid ischemic injury.

cases.title: A 9-Month-Old With a Red Bottom
cases.chiefComplaint: A red rash in the diaper area for the past several days.
cases.history: A 9-month-old infant is brought to the pediatric clinic by her parents for a red rash in the diaper area that started about five days ago. The parents note the baby has had looser, more frequent stools since starting a new pureed food, and the rash seems worse after prolonged periods in a wet or soiled diaper. The infant is otherwise feeding well, afebrile, and active, with no oral lesions. The parents have been applying petroleum jelly intermittently but have not changed diapers more frequently overnight. Birth and developmental history are normal; she is up to date on immunizations. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. There is no recent antibiotic use. She lives with both parents and attends daycare two days per week. Family history is unremarkable. The parents are worried the rash is painful during diaper changes and want to know how to treat it and whether they need a prescription cream or an antifungal.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile, playful infant. Examination of the diaper region shows erythema with mild scaling over the convex surfaces of the buttocks, lower abdomen, and proximal thighs, the areas in greatest contact with the diaper. Notably, the deep inguinal and gluteal skin creases are spared. There are no satellite papules or pustules at the rash margins, no beefy-red confluent plaques in the folds, no vesicles, and no honey-colored crusting. The oral mucosa is clear, with no thrush. The remainder of the skin examination, including the trunk and extremities, is normal. There is no lymphadenopathy and the abdomen is soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most strongly supports an irritant cause rather than candidal diaper dermatitis?
options: Involvement of the convex surfaces with sparing of the skin folds
Presence of erythema in the diaper area
Mild scaling at the rash edges
Onset after a change in diet
stages.explanation: Irritant diaper dermatitis affects the convex surfaces in direct contact with the diaper (buttocks, lower abdomen, thighs) and characteristically spares the protected skin folds, because the creases avoid direct contact with urine and stool. Option 1 is wrong: erythema is present in both irritant and candidal dermatitis and does not distinguish them. Option 2 is wrong: mild scaling is nonspecific. Option 3 is wrong: dietary change leading to looser stool can predispose to irritant dermatitis, but it does not by itself differentiate irritant from candidal disease. Fold-sparing is the cardinal feature pointing to an irritant etiology.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding, if present, would instead suggest secondary candidal diaper dermatitis?
options: Sparing of the inguinal creases
Beefy-red plaques involving the folds with satellite papules and pustules
Erythema limited to the convex buttock surfaces
Improvement with frequent diaper changes alone
stages.explanation: Candidal diaper dermatitis classically involves the skin folds (rather than sparing them) and shows beefy-red confluent plaques with satellite papules and pustules at the periphery. Option 0 is wrong: sparing of the creases favors irritant, not candidal, dermatitis. Option 2 is wrong: erythema confined to convex surfaces is the pattern of irritant dermatitis. Option 3 is wrong: improvement with better diaper hygiene alone is typical of an irritant process, whereas candidal infection requires antifungal therapy. The presence of fold involvement with satellite lesions is the key signal to add an antifungal.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this infant's irritant diaper dermatitis?
options: Topical nystatin applied four times daily
Frequent diaper changes, gentle cleansing, air exposure, and a thick barrier paste (zinc oxide or petrolatum)
A one-week course of oral fluconazole
A high-potency topical corticosteroid twice daily for two weeks
stages.explanation: First-line care for irritant diaper dermatitis is to minimize skin contact with moisture and stool: change diapers frequently, cleanse gently, allow diaper-free air time, and apply a thick barrier ointment or paste (zinc oxide or petrolatum) at each change. Option 0 is wrong: nystatin is reserved for candidal infection, which is not present given the fold-sparing pattern without satellite lesions. Option 2 is wrong: oral fluconazole is unnecessary and inappropriate for an irritant, noninfectious dermatitis. Option 3 is wrong: high-potency topical steroids are not first-line and risk atrophy and absorption under the occlusive diaper; only a low-potency steroid is used briefly if significant inflammation persists. Barrier protection is the cornerstone.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If the rash persists despite optimal barrier care and now shows fold involvement with satellite pustules, what is the best next step?
options: Add a topical antifungal such as nystatin or clotrimazole
Start oral antibiotics for bacterial superinfection
Switch to a high-potency topical steroid alone
Stop all topical treatment and observe for two more weeks
stages.explanation: Development of fold involvement with satellite papules and pustules indicates secondary candidal infection, so adding a topical antifungal (nystatin or an azole such as clotrimazole) while continuing barrier measures is the appropriate next step. Option 1 is wrong: there is no honey-colored crusting or other sign of bacterial superinfection to justify antibiotics. Option 2 is wrong: a high-potency steroid alone would not treat the yeast and risks harm under occlusion. Option 3 is wrong: stopping treatment and observing would allow the candidal infection to progress. Recognizing the morphologic shift to a candidal pattern guides the addition of antifungal therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Irritant diaper dermatitis (with awareness of secondary candidal infection)
pearls: Irritant diaper dermatitis affects convex diaper-contact surfaces and spares the skin folds.
Candidal diaper dermatitis involves the folds with beefy-red plaques and satellite papulopustules.
First-line treatment is frequent changes, gentle cleansing, air exposure, and a thick barrier paste (zinc oxide or petrolatum).
Add a topical antifungal (nystatin/clotrimazole) only when a candidal pattern develops; avoid potent steroids under the occlusive diaper.

cases.title: A 34-Year-Old With a Painful, Swollen Fingertip
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, red swelling alongside the nail of her index finger.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents to urgent care with three days of increasing pain, redness, and swelling along the side of her right index fingernail. It started after she pulled off a hangnail two days before symptoms began. The area is now throbbing and exquisitely tender, and she thinks she sees a small pocket of pus near the nail fold. She denies fever, spreading redness up the finger, or numbness. She has no chronic wet-work exposure or repeated hand immersion. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. She is a graphic designer, does not smoke, and is up to date on tetanus vaccination. Family history is noncontributory. She has full motion of the finger but avoids using it because of pain. She is concerned the finger is getting worse despite warm soaks at home and asks whether it needs to be drained and whether she requires antibiotics.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile woman in no distress. The right index finger shows erythema, warmth, and swelling localized to the lateral proximal nail fold, with a visible, tense, fluctuant collection of pus adjacent to the nail. Light pressure produces a fluctuant wave and reproduces tenderness; a small amount of pus is expressible. The distal pulp (fingertip) is soft and nontender, with no tense swelling to suggest felon. There is no fluctuance over the volar finger, no pain with passive finger extension, no fusiform swelling, and no flexor tendon sheath tenderness to suggest flexor tenosynovitis. There is no proximal lymphangitic streaking and no epitrochlear or axillary lymphadenopathy. Capillary refill and sensation are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature on examination most reliably indicates that a drainable abscess has formed?
options: Erythema of the nail fold
A localized fluctuant collection of pus
Tenderness to palpation
Warmth of the surrounding skin
stages.explanation: A localized, fluctuant pus collection signifies an organized abscess that will benefit from drainage; fluctuance is the key sign that distinguishes a drainable collection from early cellulitic inflammation. Option 0 is wrong: erythema indicates inflammation but does not confirm a drainable abscess (it is present in early, non-suppurative paronychia too). Option 2 is wrong: tenderness is nonspecific and present in both early inflammation and abscess. Option 3 is wrong: warmth signals inflammation but, like erythema, does not establish that pus has collected. Identifying fluctuance directs management toward incision and drainage rather than antibiotics alone.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding helps exclude a felon or flexor tenosynovitis, conditions that require different, more urgent management?
options: A soft, nontender distal pulp and no pain with passive finger extension
Pus visible at the nail fold
Localized swelling at the proximal nail fold
Tenderness when pressing on the nail fold
stages.explanation: A felon is a closed-space infection of the distal pulp (causing a tense, exquisitely tender fingertip), and flexor tenosynovitis presents with Kanavel signs, including pain on passive extension and fusiform swelling. A soft, nontender pulp and absence of pain on passive extension argue against both, confirming the process is limited to the nail fold (paronychia). Option 1 is wrong: pus at the nail fold confirms paronychia but does not exclude deeper infection. Option 2 is wrong: proximal nail-fold swelling is the location of paronychia and says nothing about felon or tenosynovitis. Option 3 is wrong: nail-fold tenderness is expected in paronychia and is not discriminating. Checking the pulp and passive extension is essential to exclude dangerous deep infections.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the fluctuant periungual abscess, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Oral antibiotics alone without drainage
Incision and drainage of the abscess
Removal of the entire fingernail
Continued warm soaks alone for another week
stages.explanation: An acute paronychia that has progressed to a fluctuant abscess is managed with incision and drainage to evacuate the pus, which is the definitive immediate treatment. Option 0 is wrong: antibiotics alone will not adequately resolve an established fluctuant abscess; drainage is required. Option 2 is wrong: complete nail removal is not needed for a simple lateral nail-fold abscess and is overly aggressive. Option 3 is wrong: warm soaks help only early, non-suppurative paronychia; once an abscess has formed, continued soaks alone will delay appropriate care. Antibiotics may be added in select cases (extensive surrounding cellulitis, immunocompromise), but I&D is the cornerstone here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful incision and drainage with no surrounding cellulitis, what is the most appropriate follow-up plan?
options: Prescribe a 2-week course of oral antibiotics for everyone
Warm soaks, wound care, and return precautions; antibiotics generally not required for a simple drained abscess
Urgent hand surgery referral for tendon exploration
Start intravenous antibiotics and admit for observation
stages.explanation: For a simple acute paronychia that has been adequately drained with no surrounding cellulitis, immunocompromise, or systemic signs, post-procedure warm soaks, basic wound care, and clear return precautions are appropriate, and routine systemic antibiotics are generally unnecessary after effective drainage. Option 0 is wrong: blanket antibiotics for all patients is not evidence-based and promotes resistance. Option 2 is wrong: there are no signs of flexor tenosynovitis (negative Kanavel signs), so tendon exploration is not indicated. Option 3 is wrong: IV antibiotics and admission are excessive for a localized, drained abscess in a well-appearing patient. Confirming tetanus status (which is current) and ensuring follow-up completes appropriate care.

pearls: Acute paronychia often follows minor trauma (e.g., a hangnail) and is usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
Fluctuance signals an organized abscess that requires incision and drainage rather than antibiotics alone.
Exclude felon (tense, tender pulp) and flexor tenosynovitis (Kanavel signs, pain on passive extension) before treating as simple paronychia.
After adequate drainage of a simple abscess without cellulitis, routine systemic antibiotics are generally not required.

cases.title: A 41-Year-Old With a Firm Brown Bump on Her Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: A small, firm brown bump on her lower leg that she wants checked.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic to have a small bump on her left lower leg evaluated. She first noticed it more than a year ago and thinks it may have appeared after an insect bite or minor shaving nick, though she is not certain. It is asymptomatic, occasionally mildly itchy, and has not grown noticeably, bled, or ulcerated. She has no other changing moles. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive pill and has no known drug allergies. She is a teacher, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol occasionally. She has a moderate number of nevi and uses sunscreen inconsistently. Family history is negative for melanoma or other skin cancers. She reports no constitutional symptoms. She is mainly seeking reassurance that the bump is not skin cancer, but also asks whether it can be removed because it sometimes catches on her razor and is cosmetically bothersome.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. On the left lower leg is a solitary, firm, well-circumscribed, 6 mm hyperpigmented (red-brown) papulonodule that is fixed within the dermis but freely mobile over deeper tissue. Lateral compression of the surrounding skin with thumb and index finger produces a central dimpling (positive dimple/Fitzpatrick sign). The surface is smooth without ulceration, crust, or bleeding. Dermoscopy shows a central white scar-like patch with a delicate peripheral pigment network and no atypical pigment network, blue-white veil, or irregular vascular pattern. There is no surrounding satellite lesion. No regional inguinal lymphadenopathy is palpable. The remainder of the full skin examination reveals only banal-appearing nevi.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside finding is most characteristic of a dermatofibroma?
options: A positive dimple sign on lateral compression
Soft, easily compressible consistency
Rapid recent growth
Spontaneous bleeding with minor trauma
stages.explanation: The dimple (Fitzpatrick) sign, central dimpling of the lesion when the surrounding skin is squeezed between two fingers, is a classic, highly characteristic feature of dermatofibroma, reflecting tethering of the overlying epidermis to the fibrous dermal nodule. Option 1 is wrong: dermatofibromas are firm, not soft and compressible. Option 2 is wrong: rapid growth is atypical and would raise concern for a different, possibly malignant, lesion. Option 3 is wrong: spontaneous bleeding suggests a vascular lesion (e.g., pyogenic granuloma) or malignancy, not a dermatofibroma. A firm dermal nodule with a positive dimple sign strongly supports the diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Considering the history and examination, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Nodular melanoma
Dermatofibroma
Pyogenic granuloma
Basal cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: A long-standing, stable, firm, hyperpigmented dermal papulonodule on the lower leg with a positive dimple sign and benign dermoscopic features (central white patch, delicate peripheral network) is classic for a dermatofibroma. Option 0 is wrong: nodular melanoma typically shows growth, color/border irregularity, and atypical dermoscopic features such as a blue-white veil, none of which are present. Option 2 is wrong: pyogenic granuloma is a rapidly growing, friable, often bleeding red papule, not a firm stable brown nodule. Option 3 is wrong: basal cell carcinoma usually appears as a pearly papule with telangiectasias, often on sun-exposed skin, and lacks the dimple sign. The clinical-dermoscopic pattern confirms a benign dermatofibroma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a confident clinical diagnosis of a benign, asymptomatic dermatofibroma, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance and observation, with excision only if symptomatic or diagnostically uncertain
Wide local excision with 1 cm margins urgently
Mohs micrographic surgery
Cryotherapy and topical 5-fluorouracil
stages.explanation: Dermatofibromas are benign; when the diagnosis is clinically confident and the lesion is asymptomatic, reassurance and observation are appropriate, with elective simple excision reserved for symptomatic, repeatedly traumatized, or diagnostically uncertain lesions. Option 1 is wrong: wide 1 cm margins are used for melanoma, not a benign dermatofibroma, and would be unnecessarily disfiguring. Option 2 is wrong: Mohs surgery is for certain skin cancers in cosmetically sensitive or high-risk sites, not benign dermatofibromas. Option 3 is wrong: cryotherapy with 5-fluorouracil targets actinic keratoses/superficial keratinocyte lesions, not dermatofibromas. The patient can be reassured, with the option of simple excision for her cosmetic and razor-trauma concerns.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which scenario would most appropriately prompt biopsy or excision for histologic confirmation rather than observation?
options: The lesion remains stable and asymptomatic over years
The lesion shows recent rapid growth, irregular pigmentation, or ulceration
The patient simply prefers to keep monitoring it
The dimple sign is positive
stages.explanation: Any change suggesting malignancy, recent rapid growth, color/border irregularity, ulceration, or bleeding, warrants biopsy/excision with histopathology to exclude melanoma or other malignant lesions, because such features are atypical for a benign dermatofibroma. Option 0 is wrong: a stable, asymptomatic lesion supports a benign process and ongoing observation. Option 2 is wrong: a patient's preference to monitor a clearly benign lesion does not by itself necessitate biopsy. Option 3 is wrong: a positive dimple sign actually supports the benign diagnosis of dermatofibroma and is reassuring rather than an indication for biopsy. Vigilance for atypical change is the key safety principle.

pearls: A firm dermal nodule with a positive dimple (Fitzpatrick) sign on lateral compression is classic for dermatofibroma.
Dermatofibromas are benign and commonly occur on the lower legs, often after minor trauma or insect bites.
Dermoscopy typically shows a central white scar-like patch with a delicate peripheral pigment network.
Manage with reassurance/observation; excise if symptomatic, repeatedly traumatized, or if atypical features (rapid growth, ulceration) raise concern for malignancy.

cases.title: Enlarging Crusted Bump on the Lower Lip
cases.chiefComplaint: A growing, scaly bump on the lower lip that has not healed over three months.
cases.history: A 75-year-old retired farmer presents with a firm, enlarging nodule on his lower lip that began as a rough patch three months ago and has progressively thickened and crusted. He reports occasional bleeding when he picks at the crust and mild tenderness, but no numbness. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years and has extensive outdoor sun exposure without lip protection. He drinks two beers most evenings. PMH includes hypertension and several actinic keratoses treated with cryotherapy. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily and lisinopril 20 mg daily. No immunosuppression and no organ transplant. He has no drug allergies. Family history is notable for a brother with a 'skin cancer cut off his ear.' He denies weight loss, dysphagia, or hoarseness. Review of systems is otherwise negative. He has noticed no new lymph node swelling but admits he has not examined his neck.
cases.exam: On the vermilion of the left lower lip there is a 1.2 cm firm, indurated, hyperkeratotic nodule with a central adherent crust and surrounding erythema; it bleeds easily on gentle manipulation. The adjacent lower lip shows diffuse pallor, scaling, and blurring of the vermilion border consistent with actinic cheilitis. Scattered actinic keratoses are present on the bald scalp and dorsal hands. There is no perineural tingling and sensation is intact. Submental and cervical lymph nodes are not enlarged on palpation. Oral mucosa and tongue are unremarkable. The remainder of the skin exam shows diffuse photodamage without other suspicious lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Reassure the patient and re-examine in 6 months
Prescribe a topical antibiotic for presumed impetigo
Perform a biopsy of the lesion
Treat empirically with cryotherapy as for actinic keratosis
stages.explanation: A non-healing, enlarging, bleeding hyperkeratotic nodule on a chronically sun-damaged, smoking lip is highly concerning for squamous cell carcinoma, and tissue diagnosis is mandatory before treatment. Biopsy (shave or punch through the thickest/most indurated area) confirms the diagnosis and provides depth and differentiation. Reassurance and 6-month follow-up risk progression and metastasis of a potentially aggressive tumor. Topical antibiotics are inappropriate because the lesion is not infectious. Empiric cryotherapy is reserved for confirmed actinic keratoses; freezing an invasive carcinoma without histology obscures margins, delays definitive care, and is substandard for a lesion this suspicious.

stages.label: BIOPSY RESULTS
stages.content:
Punch biopsy: Invasive well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma
Nests of atypical keratinocytes with keratin pearls invading the dermis
Depth of invasion: 3.5 mm
No perineural invasion identified
Peripheral margin involved at biopsy edge

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which feature of this tumor most increases its risk for recurrence and metastasis?
options: Location on the lip
Well-differentiated histology
Absence of perineural invasion
Patient age of 75 years
stages.explanation: Cutaneous SCC of the lip (and ear) is a high-risk anatomic site with substantially greater metastatic potential than SCC on the trunk or extremities, in part due to rich lymphatics. Tumor depth >2 mm and >6 mm, poor differentiation, and perineural invasion are additional high-risk features. Well-differentiated histology is actually a favorable, not adverse, feature. Absence of perineural invasion is reassuring, not a risk factor. Advanced age alone is not a major independent risk determinant for SCC recurrence; the high-risk location, depth, and histologic grade drive prognosis here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive treatment for this high-risk lip SCC?
options: Topical 5-fluorouracil cream
Radiation therapy alone as first-line curative treatment
Mohs micrographic surgery
Continued observation with serial photography
stages.explanation: Mohs micrographic surgery is the treatment of choice for high-risk SCC at cosmetically and functionally critical, high-recurrence sites such as the lip, providing complete margin assessment with maximal tissue conservation and the highest cure rates. Topical 5-fluorouracil treats superficial actinic keratoses and SCC in situ, not invasive disease. Primary radiation is reserved for non-surgical candidates or as adjuvant therapy; it is not first-line for a resectable lip SCC. Observation is unacceptable for a biopsy-proven invasive carcinoma with positive margins and a high-risk location.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After successful excision, what is the most important component of this patient's long-term care?
options: Lifelong systemic retinoid prophylaxis for all patients
Regular total-body skin exams, nodal checks, and strict photoprotection plus smoking cessation
Annual chest CT regardless of symptoms
No further follow-up once margins are clear
stages.explanation: Patients with a high-risk SCC need regular full-skin and regional lymph node examinations (typically every 3-6 months initially), because they are at elevated risk for recurrence, nodal metastasis, and new keratinocyte cancers; counseling on photoprotection and smoking cessation addresses ongoing carcinogenic exposure. Systemic retinoids are reserved for selected patients with very numerous tumors (e.g., transplant recipients), not all patients. Routine surveillance CT is not indicated absent symptoms or palpable nodes. Discharging the patient with no follow-up ignores the high recurrence and second-primary risk inherent to his sun-damaged, tobacco-exposed skin.

cases.diagnosis: Invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma of the lower lip
pearls: Non-healing, bleeding, hyperkeratotic lip nodules in sun-damaged smokers are SCC until proven otherwise — biopsy promptly.
Lip and ear are high-risk SCC sites with greater metastatic potential than trunk/extremity tumors.
Mohs micrographic surgery offers the highest cure rate for high-risk SCC at functionally critical sites.
Depth >2 mm, poor differentiation, and perineural invasion are key adverse prognostic features.

cases.title: A Mole That Has Changed Shape and Color
cases.chiefComplaint: A long-standing mole on the back that has darkened and grown irregular over several months.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents because a mole on her upper back, present for years, has changed over the past five months — it now looks larger, has uneven borders, and contains shades of brown and black. Her husband noticed it after a beach vacation. She reports no bleeding or itching. She has fair skin that burns easily, used tanning beds in her twenties, and recalls several blistering sunburns as a teenager. PMH is unremarkable. She takes only a daily multivitamin and has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks socially. Family history is significant: her mother had melanoma diagnosed at age 60. She has more than 50 nevi overall and several 'funny-looking' moles. She has never had a full skin examination. Review of systems is negative for headaches, weight loss, bone pain, or visual changes. She is anxious because of her family history and wants to know whether this mole is dangerous.
cases.exam: On the left upper back there is a 9 mm pigmented macule-plaque that is asymmetric, with notched irregular borders and color variegation ranging from tan to dark brown to blue-black; one edge is slightly raised. Dermoscopy shows an atypical pigment network, irregular streaks, and a blue-white veil. The lesion is the largest and most atypical of numerous scattered nevi over the trunk and extremities ('ugly duckling'). There is no ulceration or surrounding satellite lesions. Axillary and cervical lymph nodes are not palpable. Mucous membranes, nails, and palms/soles show no pigmented lesions. The remainder of the skin exam reveals multiple benign-appearing nevi.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which biopsy technique is most appropriate for this suspicious pigmented lesion?
options: Superficial shave biopsy of the most pigmented area
Excisional (full-thickness) biopsy with narrow margins
Punch biopsy of the center only
Curettage and electrodesiccation
stages.explanation: Suspected melanoma should be sampled by excisional biopsy with 1-3 mm margins removing the entire lesion to full depth, because accurate Breslow thickness — the most important prognostic factor — requires the deepest part of the tumor. A superficial shave can transect the base and underestimate depth, compromising staging. A central punch of a large heterogeneous lesion may miss the most invasive focus and is subject to sampling error. Curettage and electrodesiccation destroys tissue, precluding any histologic assessment, and is never appropriate for a possible melanoma.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Excisional biopsy: Malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type
Breslow thickness: 1.4 mm
Clark level IV
Mitotic rate: 3 /mm²
Ulceration: absent
Deep and peripheral margins: clear

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Given a 1.4 mm non-ulcerated melanoma, what is the most appropriate next staging step?
options: PET-CT and brain MRI for all patients
Offer sentinel lymph node biopsy
Routine LDH and chest x-ray only
No further staging is needed
stages.explanation: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is recommended for clinically node-negative melanomas >0.8 mm or those <0.8 mm with high-risk features (e.g., ulceration); a 1.4 mm tumor clearly qualifies, and SLNB provides important prognostic and staging information. Routine PET-CT and brain MRI are not indicated for asymptomatic intermediate-thickness disease with a negative exam and would generate false positives. LDH and chest x-ray alone are insufficient and not the standard staging approach. Doing no further staging would miss occult nodal disease that changes prognosis and adjuvant decisions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What surgical margin is recommended for wide local excision of a 1.4 mm-thick melanoma?
options: 0.5 cm margin
1 cm margin
2 cm margin
4 cm margin
stages.explanation: For melanomas 1.01-2.0 mm thick, a wide local excision margin of 1-2 cm is recommended; 1 cm is appropriate and adequate for a 1.4 mm tumor when wider margins are not feasible. A 0.5 cm margin is reserved for melanoma in situ. A 2 cm margin is recommended for tumors >2 mm thick; while acceptable here, 1 cm is the guideline-concordant minimum for this thickness and is the best single answer offered. A 4 cm margin is excessive, increases morbidity, and has not been shown to improve survival.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the most important counseling point for this patient and her family going forward?
options: Melanoma is not hereditary, so family screening is unnecessary
Tanning beds are safe if used briefly
Lifelong sun protection, monthly self-skin-exams, regular dermatologic surveillance, and screening of first-degree relatives
Vitamin D supplements eliminate melanoma risk
stages.explanation: A personal melanoma history, numerous atypical nevi, and a first-degree relative with melanoma place this patient and her family at elevated risk; lifelong photoprotection, regular self and professional skin exams, and screening of first-degree relatives are the cornerstones of secondary prevention and early detection. Melanoma does have heritable and familial components, so screening relatives is warranted. Tanning beds are a class I carcinogen and never safe. Vitamin D supplementation does not prevent melanoma and is unrelated to UV-driven mutagenesis; relying on it is dangerous misinformation.

pearls: Use the ABCDE rule and the 'ugly duckling' sign to flag suspicious nevi.
Excisional biopsy to full depth is essential — Breslow thickness drives staging and prognosis.
Offer sentinel lymph node biopsy for melanomas >0.8 mm or thinner lesions with ulceration.
WLE margins scale with thickness: 0.5 cm (in situ), 1 cm (≤1 mm and 1-2 mm), 2 cm (>2 mm).

cases.title: Painful Mouth Sores and a Spreading Rash After a New Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful mouth and eye sores with a spreading targetoid rash and fever for two days.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman is admitted with two days of fever, painful oral erosions, gritty red eyes, and a rash that began on the trunk and spread to the limbs. She started lamotrigine three weeks ago for bipolar disorder; the dose was recently escalated. She reports malaise and a sore throat preceded the rash. She has dysuria and pain with swallowing. PMH includes bipolar II disorder and migraine. Medications: lamotrigine (recently up-titrated), valproate, and occasional ibuprofen. She has no known drug allergies, though she had a 'rash' to an antibiotic years ago that she cannot name. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. She denies recent travel or sick contacts. On review of systems she notes burning when she urinates and that her lips have crusted with blood. She is frightened and in significant pain, rating it 8/10, worse with eating or blinking.
cases.exam: She appears ill and uncomfortable. There are dusky red macules with central blistering and scattered atypical targetoid lesions on the trunk and proximal limbs; lateral pressure on perilesional skin shears the epidermis (positive Nikolsky sign). Detachment involves roughly 6% of body surface area. The lips are crusted and hemorrhagic with painful erosions of the buccal mucosa and palate. There is conjunctival injection with purulent discharge and erosions. Vulvar mucosa shows erosions. There is no facial edema or palpable lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is benign. No nail or scalp involvement is noted.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits mucosal erosions at two or more sites, atypical targetoid lesions, positive Nikolsky, and <10% epidermal detachment after a new drug?
options: Erythema multiforme major
Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
Bullous pemphigoid
stages.explanation: Severe mucosal involvement at multiple sites, atypical (two-zone) targets, a positive Nikolsky sign, and epidermal detachment under 10% BSA after a high-risk drug (lamotrigine, especially rapidly up-titrated) define Stevens-Johnson syndrome. Erythema multiforme major features typical three-zone targets, is usually infection-triggered (HSV), and lacks the dusky confluent detachment of SJS. SSSS spares mucosa, occurs mainly in young children, and splits the skin in the superficial granular layer from a toxin, not at the dermoepidermal junction. Bullous pemphigoid causes tense subepidermal bullae with a negative Nikolsky in elderly patients and is not drug-triggered in this acute fashion.

stages.label: IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Start systemic corticosteroids and continue lamotrigine
Immediately discontinue the lamotrigine
Begin empiric IV vancomycin for cellulitis
Apply topical antifungal cream to the erosions
stages.explanation: The most critical first action in any SJS/TEN is immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug; earlier cessation improves survival. Lamotrigine — particularly when titrated too quickly or combined with valproate, which raises lamotrigine levels — is a classic trigger and must be stopped at once. Continuing it while adding steroids is dangerous. Empiric vancomycin for 'cellulitis' misdiagnoses the process; antibiotics are given only for documented infection and many antibiotics themselves can trigger SJS/TEN. Topical antifungals do not address an immune-mediated drug reaction and waste critical time.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL
Na 134 mEq/L
HCO3 22 mEq/L
BUN 24 mg/dL
Glucose 138 mg/dL
AST 58 U/L
Bicarbonate 22 mEq/L
Skin biopsy: full-thickness epidermal necrosis with subepidermal split

stages.label: SUPPORTIVE CARE
stages.question: Which multidisciplinary measure is essential in managing this patient?
options: Routine prophylactic systemic antibiotics for all patients
Urgent ophthalmology consultation and meticulous wound, fluid, and mucosal care
Aggressive surgical debridement of all detached skin
Immediate plasmapheresis as standard of care
stages.explanation: SJS/TEN demands supportive care analogous to burns: fluid and electrolyte management, temperature control, nutritional support, gentle non-adherent wound care, and early ophthalmology involvement to prevent permanent ocular scarring, symblepharon, and blindness. Prophylactic systemic antibiotics are not routinely given and select for resistance; antibiotics are reserved for documented infection with cultures and clinical signs. Aggressive debridement is avoided — detached epidermis can act as a biologic dressing and overly aggressive removal worsens fluid loss. Plasmapheresis is not standard; adjuncts like cyclosporine or IVIG are debated, but supportive care is the proven backbone.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient?
options: Discharge home with oral analgesics and outpatient follow-up
Admit to a general medical floor for observation only
Transfer to a specialized burn or intensive care unit
Admit to psychiatry to restart mood stabilization
stages.explanation: SJS/TEN carries significant mortality and is best managed in a burn unit or ICU with expertise in extensive epidermal loss, fluid shifts, and infection prevention; early transfer improves outcomes even at <10% detachment given risk of progression. Discharge home is unsafe for a febrile patient with mucosal erosions and active skin sloughing. A general floor without burn/critical-care expertise is inadequate for wound, fluid, and ophthalmologic needs. Psychiatric admission ignores the life-threatening dermatologic emergency; mood stabilization is addressed later and lamotrigine must never be rechallenged.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important at discharge?
options: Lamotrigine may be cautiously restarted at a lower dose later
Lifelong avoidance of lamotrigine and documentation of the reaction; caution with cross-reactive aromatic anticonvulsants
She can safely take carbamazepine instead without precaution
The reaction was likely viral and unrelated to any drug
stages.explanation: After SJS/TEN the culprit drug must be avoided for life — rechallenge can cause rapid, fatal recurrence — and the allergy must be clearly documented in the record and communicated to the patient. Aromatic anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) can cross-react, so they are used only with great caution, making 'safely take carbamazepine' wrong. Restarting lamotrigine at any dose is contraindicated. Attributing the reaction to a virus is incorrect given the clear temporal link to a high-risk drug and biopsy showing full-thickness epidermal necrosis.

pearls: SJS = <10% BSA detachment; SJS/TEN overlap 10-30%; TEN >30%.
Lamotrigine risk rises sharply with rapid up-titration and co-administration of valproate.
Immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug is the single most survival-improving action.
Early ophthalmology and burn-unit/ICU-level supportive care prevent ocular scarring and reduce mortality.

cases.title: Widespread Skin Sloughing After an Antibiotic
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly spreading painful rash with large sheets of skin peeling off, four days after starting an antibiotic.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man is transferred to the ICU with progressive, painful skin sloughing. Ten days ago he was prescribed trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a urinary tract infection. Four days ago he developed fever, malaise, and a burning, dusky rash that began on the face and trunk and rapidly coalesced; today large sheets of epidermis are detaching. He has severe pain, photophobia, and inability to eat due to mouth pain. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, HIV on antiretroviral therapy (CD4 280), and gout. Medications: metformin, dolutegravir/lamivudine/abacavir, allopurinol (started two months ago), and the recent sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim. He reports a vague 'sulfa reaction' he never took seriously. He has no other allergies. He does not smoke; he drinks occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems reveals decreased urine output, painful swallowing, and red, weeping eyes. He is hemodynamically borderline and increasingly tachycardic, and reports the skin feels like it is 'on fire.'
cases.exam: The patient is acutely ill with diffuse dusky erythema and large flaccid bullae. Confluent epidermal detachment involves an estimated 40% of body surface area, with sheets of skin peeling to reveal raw, weeping dermis; gentle lateral pressure extends the detachment (positive Nikolsky and Asboe-Hansen signs). Severe hemorrhagic crusting of the lips and erosions of the oral and conjunctival mucosa are present, with purulent conjunctivitis. There is urethral mucosal involvement. Lungs have scattered crackles. He is dry with delayed capillary refill. There is no focal neurologic deficit. The detached areas resemble a second-degree burn.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given >30% epidermal detachment after sulfamethoxazole?
options: Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Toxic epidermal necrolysis
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis
stages.explanation: Epidermal detachment exceeding 30% BSA with mucosal involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and a clear culprit drug (sulfonamide) defines toxic epidermal necrolysis, the most severe form on the SJS/TEN spectrum. SJS by definition involves <10% BSA; 10-30% is overlap. SSSS spares mucosa, splits superficially in the granular layer, and occurs chiefly in young children — not a sulfa-exposed adult with mucositis. AGEP produces numerous sterile non-follicular pustules on edematous erythema, typically without full-thickness detachment or severe mucosal erosions. The extensive full-thickness sloughing and severe mucositis here are characteristic of TEN.

stages.label: LABS & SCORTEN VARIABLES
stages.content:
Age 57 years
Heart rate 124 bpm
Serum glucose 268 mg/dL
BUN 38 mg/dL
Bicarbonate 18 mEq/L
Body surface area detached >30%
Malignancy: absent
WBC 16.0 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The SCORTEN score is used in TEN primarily to do what?
options: Determine the exact culprit drug
Guide the choice of antibiotic
Estimate mortality risk based on prognostic variables
Decide the dose of systemic corticosteroids
stages.explanation: SCORTEN is a validated severity-of-illness score that estimates TEN mortality using seven variables: age >40, heart rate >120, malignancy, BSA detached >10%, BUN >28 mg/dL (>10 mmol/L), bicarbonate <20 mEq/L, and glucose >252 mg/dL (>14 mmol/L). This patient meets at least six, predicting high mortality. SCORTEN does not identify the culprit drug — that is done clinically by temporal correlation. It does not select antibiotics and is not a dosing tool for corticosteroids; its purpose is risk stratification and care planning.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management strategy?
options: Continue the sulfonamide and add prophylactic antibiotics
Stop the culprit drug, transfer to a burn unit, and provide aggressive supportive care
Start high-dose corticosteroids alone as definitive therapy
Begin broad surgical debridement of all detached skin
stages.explanation: Management of TEN centers on immediate cessation of the culprit drug (sulfamethoxazole here; allopurinol is also a notable trigger and should be reviewed), transfer to a burn unit or ICU, and meticulous supportive care: fluid and electrolyte resuscitation, temperature regulation, nutrition, non-adherent wound care, and infection surveillance. Continuing the offending sulfa is lethal, and prophylactic antibiotics are not routine. High-dose corticosteroids are not proven definitive therapy and may increase infection risk; immunomodulators (cyclosporine) are debated. Wide surgical debridement is avoided because detached epidermis can serve as a biologic dressing.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the leading cause of death in TEN?
options: Sepsis from extensive skin barrier loss
Acute myocardial infarction
Intracranial hemorrhage
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Sepsis arising from massive loss of the epidermal barrier — frequently with Staphylococcus aureus or Pseudomonas — together with fluid loss and multiorgan failure is the leading cause of death in TEN, which is why infection surveillance and barrier protection are paramount. Myocardial infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, and pulmonary embolism can occur in critically ill patients but are not the characteristic or leading killers in TEN. The denuded skin behaves like an extensive burn, making overwhelming infection and the resulting septic shock the dominant lethal pathway.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond acute survival, which long-term issue most requires proactive follow-up?
options: Routine echocardiography for cardiomyopathy
Chronic ocular sequelae such as scarring, dry eye, and vision loss
Permanent loss of taste only
No long-term sequelae are expected after recovery
stages.explanation: Survivors of TEN frequently develop chronic ocular complications — symblepharon, entropion, severe dry eye, corneal neovascularization, and vision loss — making early and ongoing ophthalmology follow-up essential; other sequelae include cutaneous dyspigmentation, nail dystrophy, and mucosal strictures. Routine echocardiography is not a standard TEN concern. Isolated permanent taste loss is not the dominant long-term morbidity. Expecting no sequelae is incorrect: the severe mucosal injury commonly leaves lasting ocular and other damage, so structured multidisciplinary follow-up and strict lifelong avoidance of the culprit drug class are required.

pearls: TEN = >30% epidermal detachment; immediate culprit-drug withdrawal is the key intervention.
SCORTEN estimates mortality from 7 variables (age, HR, malignancy, BSA, BUN, HCO3, glucose).
Manage in a burn unit with burn-style supportive care; avoid aggressive debridement.
Sepsis from barrier loss is the leading cause of death; ocular scarring is a key long-term sequela.

cases.title: Feverish Toddler With Red, Peeling Skin
cases.chiefComplaint: Diffuse red, tender skin that is peeling in sheets in a febrile, irritable toddler.
cases.history: A 2-year-old previously healthy boy is admitted with two days of fever and worsening red, tender skin that is now peeling. His parents first noticed redness around the mouth and in the diaper area, which spread to the trunk and flexures. He is irritable, refuses to be held because touching hurts, and has decreased oral intake. He had a runny nose and crusting near one nostril last week. Immunizations are up to date. He takes no regular medications and has had no recent new medications. No known drug allergies. He attends daycare, where other children have had 'colds.' Birth history is unremarkable and he has no chronic illness. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is notable for fever, fussiness, and reduced wet diapers; there is no cough, no diarrhea, and no rash inside the mouth. Parents deny any caustic or chemical exposure. He has been drinking less and seems uncomfortable when his skin is touched.
cases.exam: The child is irritable and in pain. There is diffuse tender erythema most marked in the flexures, neck, and perioral region, with superficial flaccid blisters and sheet-like desquamation; gentle rubbing of erythematous skin shears the superficial epidermis (positive Nikolsky sign). There is perioral radial crusting and fissuring. Importantly, the oral, conjunctival, and genital mucosae are spared and moist. There is honey-colored crusting near the left nostril. Capillary refill is brisk. No nuchal rigidity. Lungs are clear; the abdomen is soft. The denuded areas are superficial and weep clear fluid.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes this child's condition from Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN?
options: Presence of fever
Positive Nikolsky sign
Sparing of the mucous membranes
Skin tenderness
stages.explanation: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome characteristically spares the mucous membranes, whereas SJS/TEN prominently involve oral, ocular, and genital mucosa — this is the most useful clinical discriminator at the bedside, alongside the patient's young age and superficial split. Fever, a positive Nikolsky sign, and skin tenderness occur in both SSSS and TEN, so none of those distinguishes them. In SSSS the exfoliative toxins cleave the superficial granular layer (desmoglein-1), producing very superficial, mucosa-sparing peeling, in contrast to the full-thickness, mucosa-involving necrosis of TEN.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying mechanism of the skin findings?
options: IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein-3
Staphylococcal exfoliative toxins cleaving desmoglein-1
Drug-induced full-thickness epidermal necrosis
Subepidermal IgA deposition at dermal papillae
stages.explanation: SSSS is caused by hematogenous spread of staphylococcal exfoliative (epidermolytic) toxins A and B, which are serine proteases that cleave desmoglein-1, a desmosomal protein in the superficial granular layer, producing superficial intraepidermal splitting and sheet-like peeling. IgG against desmoglein-3 causes pemphigus vulgaris (suprabasal, mucosa-prominent). Drug-induced full-thickness epidermal necrosis describes TEN. Subepidermal IgA deposition at the dermal papillae is the mechanism of dermatitis herpetiformis. The toxin-mediated, desmoglein-1-targeted, superficial split is what gives SSSS its scalded appearance with intact deeper skin.

stages.label: LABS & CULTURES
stages.content:
WBC 17.5 ×10³/µL with neutrophilia
CRP 6.2 mg/dL
Nasal swab culture: Staphylococcus aureus
Blood culture: pending
Nikolsky sign: positive
Biopsy (if performed): intraepidermal split through granular layer

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Systemic corticosteroids
Anti-staphylococcal antibiotics plus supportive care
IV immunoglobulin as first-line therapy
Topical antifungal therapy alone
stages.explanation: SSSS is treated with systemic anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (e.g., a penicillinase-resistant penicillin such as nafcillin, or vancomycin/clindamycin where MRSA is a concern) directed at the toxin-producing focus, together with supportive care: gentle skin care, analgesia, and fluid management. Corticosteroids are not indicated and can worsen an infectious process. IVIG is not first-line for SSSS. Topical antifungals do not treat a staphylococcal toxin-mediated disease. Because the split is superficial, most children recover well with prompt antibiotics and supportive measures, often without scarring.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Compared with TEN, the prognosis of SSSS in children is generally:
options: Much worse, with very high mortality
Favorable, with low mortality and healing without scarring
Identical to TEN
Universally fatal without skin grafting
stages.explanation: SSSS in otherwise healthy children generally carries a favorable prognosis with low mortality and heals without scarring, because the epidermal split is superficial (granular layer) and the deeper skin and adnexa are preserved once the toxin source is treated. TEN, with full-thickness necrosis and mucosal involvement, has substantially higher mortality. SSSS is not identical to TEN in severity or outcome, and skin grafting is not required given the superficial split. Mortality is higher in neonates and immunocompromised or systemically septic patients, but typical childhood SSSS responds well to antibiotics and supportive care.

pearls: Mucosal SPARING distinguishes SSSS from SJS/TEN, which prominently involve mucosa.
Exfoliative toxins A/B cleave desmoglein-1 in the superficial granular layer.
Treat with systemic anti-staphylococcal antibiotics plus supportive skin care.
Superficial split means healing without scarring and a generally favorable prognosis in children.

cases.title: Ring-Shaped Spots on the Hands After a Cold Sore
cases.chiefComplaint: Symmetric ring-shaped spots on the hands and forearms appearing two weeks after a cold sore.
cases.history: A 23-year-old graduate student presents with a three-day history of symmetric, ring-like skin lesions on the backs of his hands, forearms, and a few on the feet. He reports a painful cluster of blisters on his lip about two weeks ago that crusted and resolved — consistent with a recurrent cold sore he gets a few times a year. The current lesions are mildly itchy but not painful and have not blistered significantly. He has no fever, no mouth pain, and feels well overall. He started no new medications. PMH is notable only for recurrent herpes labialis. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He does not smoke, drinks socially, and is sexually active with one partner. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for joint pain, eye symptoms, dysuria, and weight loss. He is concerned because new lesions keep appearing on his extremities in a symmetric pattern, but he otherwise feels fine and is able to eat and drink normally.
cases.exam: On the dorsal hands, extensor forearms, palms, and dorsa of the feet there are numerous symmetric, well-demarcated round lesions with three concentric zones: a dusky or blistered center, a surrounding pale edematous ring, and an erythematous outer rim — classic target lesions. Lesions are acrally distributed and symmetric. There is mild residual crusting at the left vermilion lip but no active oral erosions. Conjunctivae are clear and ocular and genital mucosae are uninvolved. Nikolsky sign is negative. There is no facial edema or lymphadenopathy. The trunk is relatively spared. He is well-appearing and in no distress.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given symmetric three-zone target lesions on the acral skin after an HSV outbreak?
options: Urticaria
Erythema multiforme
Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Bullous pemphigoid
stages.explanation: Classic typical target ('iris') lesions with three concentric zones, an acral and symmetric distribution, and a preceding herpes simplex outbreak are characteristic of erythema multiforme, which is most often triggered by HSV. Urticaria produces transient migratory wheals that resolve within 24 hours and lack a fixed dusky center. SJS shows atypical (two-zone) targets, prominent multisite mucosal erosions, systemic toxicity, and skin detachment — features absent here. Bullous pemphigoid causes tense bullae on an urticarial base in the elderly, not symmetric acral targets in a young adult following HSV.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the most common precipitating cause of erythema multiforme?
options: Herpes simplex virus infection
Sulfonamide antibiotics
Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection
Underlying malignancy
stages.explanation: Herpes simplex virus is by far the most common trigger of erythema multiforme, particularly recurrent EM, with lesions typically appearing 1-2 weeks after an HSV outbreak — exactly this patient's course. Mycoplasma pneumoniae is an important cause, especially in children and in mucosa-predominant disease, but is less common overall than HSV. Drugs such as sulfonamides more often cause SJS/TEN than classic EM. Malignancy is not a typical EM trigger. Recognizing the HSV association guides both diagnosis and prevention of recurrences.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this patient's current episode?
options: Hospital admission and IVIG
Symptomatic care with antihistamines and topical steroids; consider antivirals
High-dose systemic corticosteroids for all patients
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
stages.explanation: Most EM minor episodes are self-limited and managed symptomatically with oral antihistamines and topical corticosteroids for itch; antivirals (acyclovir/valacyclovir) are useful when HSV is the trigger and, importantly, suppressive antiviral therapy prevents recurrent HSV-associated EM. Hospitalization and IVIG are reserved for severe SJS/TEN, not this well-appearing patient with EM minor. Routine high-dose systemic steroids are not standard for uncomplicated EM and may prolong disease. IV antibiotics are inappropriate without a bacterial infection; this is a post-viral immune reaction.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: He reports several similar episodes over the past year, each after a cold sore. What is the best long-term strategy?
options: Lifelong daily systemic corticosteroids
Continuous suppressive antiviral therapy
Reassurance with no intervention regardless of frequency
Prophylactic antibiotics during each episode
stages.explanation: For recurrent HSV-associated erythema multiforme, continuous suppressive antiviral therapy (e.g., daily valacyclovir or acyclovir for 6-12 months) is the most effective strategy to reduce or prevent recurrences by suppressing the triggering HSV reactivations. Chronic systemic corticosteroids carry substantial cumulative toxicity and are not first-line maintenance. Reassurance alone is reasonable for a single mild episode but inadequate for frequent, recurrent disease that impairs the patient. Prophylactic antibiotics do not address the underlying viral trigger and are not indicated.

pearls: Typical EM lesions are three-zone targets, acral and symmetric, often 1-2 weeks after HSV.
HSV is the most common trigger; Mycoplasma is a leading cause in mucosa-predominant pediatric cases.
EM minor is self-limited — treat symptomatically; antivirals help when HSV-driven.
Recurrent HSV-associated EM is best prevented with continuous suppressive antiviral therapy.

cases.title: Itchy Tense Blisters in an Elderly Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Intensely itchy, large tense blisters on the trunk and flexures for several weeks.
cases.history: A 78-year-old woman presents with several weeks of intensely itchy skin that initially showed only red, hive-like patches, followed by the appearance of large, tense fluid-filled blisters on the inner thighs, lower abdomen, axillae, and flexor forearms. The blisters are tense and do not rupture easily; some have healed without scarring. She denies mouth sores and eats normally. PMH includes hypertension, osteoarthritis, and mild dementia. Medications: amlodipine, acetaminophen, and a recently started gliptin (DPP-4 inhibitor) for newly diagnosed diabetes. No known drug allergies. She lives in an assisted-living facility, does not smoke, and rarely drinks. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, dysphagia, and eye symptoms. The itching disturbs her sleep. There has been no recent travel and no new topical products. Caregivers note she has been scratching frequently, and several blisters keep recurring in the same flexural areas despite emollients.
cases.exam: There are numerous large (1-3 cm) tense bullae filled with clear fluid arising on both normal-appearing and erythematous, urticarial skin, distributed over the lower abdomen, inner thighs, axillae, and flexural arms. Several intact bullae resist rupture; lateral pressure on perilesional skin does not extend the blister (negative Nikolsky sign). There are excoriations and a few collapsed, crusted erosions that are healing without scarring. The oral, conjunctival, and genital mucosae are intact. There is no facial involvement or lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable for the patient's age.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical feature best supports bullous pemphigoid over pemphigus vulgaris?
options: Flaccid easily ruptured bullae
Prominent painful oral erosions
Tense bullae with a negative Nikolsky sign and spared mucosa
Positive Nikolsky sign
stages.explanation: Bullous pemphigoid produces tense subepidermal bullae (the full epidermis forms the blister roof) with a negative Nikolsky sign and usually spared or minimal mucosal involvement, typically in elderly patients with intense pruritus — matching this presentation. Flaccid, easily ruptured bullae and a positive Nikolsky sign indicate an intraepidermal split, as in pemphigus vulgaris. Prominent painful oral erosions are the hallmark of pemphigus vulgaris, which is mucosa-predominant. The combination of tense bullae, negative Nikolsky, and intact mucosa reliably points to bullous pemphigoid.

stages.label: BIOPSY & IMMUNOFLUORESCENCE
stages.content:
H&E (lesional): subepidermal blister with eosinophil-rich infiltrate
Direct immunofluorescence (perilesional): linear IgG and C3 along the basement membrane zone
Indirect IF salt-split skin: IgG binds the epidermal (roof) side
Serum ELISA: anti-BP180 antibodies elevated
Eosinophil count: 720 /µL

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: On direct immunofluorescence, which pattern is diagnostic of bullous pemphigoid?
options: Intercellular ('chicken-wire') IgG within the epidermis
Linear IgG and C3 along the basement membrane zone
Granular IgA in the dermal papillae
No immunoreactant deposition
stages.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence in bullous pemphigoid shows a linear band of IgG and C3 along the basement membrane zone, reflecting autoantibodies (anti-BP180/BP230) targeting hemidesmosomal proteins and producing a subepidermal split. Intercellular 'chicken-wire' (fishnet) IgG within the epidermis is the pattern of pemphigus vulgaris, where desmosomal adhesion is disrupted. Granular IgA in the dermal papillae is diagnostic of dermatitis herpetiformis. Absence of deposition would argue against an autoimmune blistering disease. The linear BMZ pattern, plus salt-split skin binding to the roof and anti-BP180, confirms BP.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for moderate-to-extensive bullous pemphigoid?
options: High-potency topical corticosteroids and/or systemic corticosteroids
Lifelong high-dose oral corticosteroids as the only option
Topical antifungal therapy
Plasmapheresis as initial therapy for all patients
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for bullous pemphigoid is potent corticosteroids: high-potency topical clobetasol is highly effective and is preferred for localized or even widespread disease in frail elderly patients to limit systemic toxicity, with systemic corticosteroids and steroid-sparing agents (doxycycline/nicotinamide, azathioprine, mycophenolate, or rituximab) used as needed. Indefinite high-dose oral steroids as the 'only option' is incorrect and harmful given side effects. Topical antifungals do not treat an autoimmune blistering disease. Plasmapheresis is reserved for severe refractory cases, not initial therapy. A possible DPP-4 inhibitor trigger should also be reviewed and discontinued.

stages.label: TRIGGER
stages.question: Which of this patient's medications is a recognized potential trigger of bullous pemphigoid and should be reviewed?
options: Acetaminophen
Amlodipine
The DPP-4 inhibitor (gliptin)
None of her medications are associated with BP
stages.explanation: DPP-4 inhibitors (gliptins) such as vildagliptin and others are an increasingly recognized drug trigger for bullous pemphigoid; the temporal association with her recently started gliptin warrants review and consideration of discontinuation in consultation with her diabetes team, which may improve disease control. Acetaminophen and amlodipine are not characteristic BP triggers. Stating that none of her medications are associated is incorrect given the well-described gliptin link. Identifying and stopping a culprit drug can be an important adjunct to corticosteroid therapy in drug-associated BP.

pearls: BP: tense subepidermal bullae, intense pruritus, negative Nikolsky, mucosa usually spared, elderly.
DIF shows linear IgG and C3 at the basement membrane zone; salt-split binds the roof; anti-BP180.
High-potency topical clobetasol is effective first-line, sparing frail patients systemic steroid toxicity.
DPP-4 inhibitors (gliptins) are a recognized drug trigger — review and consider stopping.

cases.title: Painful Mouth Erosions and Soft Blisters
cases.chiefComplaint: Weeks of painful mouth erosions followed by soft, easily rupturing skin blisters.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man is admitted with several weeks of progressively painful mouth sores that have made eating and drinking difficult, followed more recently by flaccid blisters and raw erosions on the chest, back, and scalp. The skin lesions rupture almost as soon as they form, leaving painful denuded areas that have been slow to heal. He has lost weight because eating is so painful. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and started nothing new. No known drug allergies. He is of Mediterranean descent. He does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems reveals odynophagia, hoarseness, and sore, raw gums; he denies eye pain and visual change. He has had low-grade fevers and feels fatigued. He reports the skin 'sloughs off' when he rubs it. There is no clear infectious prodrome, and no one at home is ill. His oral intake has dropped significantly because of pain.
cases.exam: The oral mucosa shows extensive painful erosions of the buccal mucosa, gingiva, and palate, with crusted, fissured lips. On the trunk, scalp, and back there are flaccid, thin-walled bullae and numerous raw, denuded erosions; gentle lateral pressure on normal-appearing perilesional skin produces epidermal shearing (positive Nikolsky sign), and pressure on an intact bulla extends it laterally (positive Asboe-Hansen sign). The blisters rupture easily, and intact bullae are few. Conjunctivae are clear. There is no target morphology. The erosions are painful and weeping. There is no lymphadenopathy; the lungs are clear and the abdomen is benign.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits painful oral erosions, flaccid bullae, and a positive Nikolsky sign in a middle-aged adult?
options: Bullous pemphigoid
Pemphigus vulgaris
Dermatitis herpetiformis
Erythema multiforme
stages.explanation: Pemphigus vulgaris classically presents with painful mucosal erosions (often the first sign) plus flaccid, easily ruptured bullae and a positive Nikolsky sign from loss of intraepidermal (suprabasal) keratinocyte adhesion. Bullous pemphigoid produces tense bullae with a negative Nikolsky and usually spares mucosa. Dermatitis herpetiformis causes intensely pruritic grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces, not flaccid bullae with mucosal erosions. Erythema multiforme shows target lesions and is typically post-infectious. The mucosa-first course with flaccid bullae and positive Nikolsky strongly indicates pemphigus vulgaris.

stages.label: BIOPSY & SEROLOGY
stages.content:
H&E (lesional): suprabasal acantholysis with 'row of tombstones' basal layer
Direct immunofluorescence: intercellular ('fishnet/chicken-wire') IgG within the epidermis
Serum ELISA: anti-desmoglein-3 antibodies elevated; anti-desmoglein-1 mildly elevated
Nikolsky sign: positive
Albumin 3.0 g/dL

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: The autoantibodies in pemphigus vulgaris primarily target which protein, and where is the split?
options: BP180 at the basement membrane (subepidermal split)
Desmoglein-3 in desmosomes (intraepidermal, suprabasal split)
Tissue transglutaminase in dermal papillae
Type VII collagen in the sublamina densa
stages.explanation: Pemphigus vulgaris is caused by IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein-3 (and desmoglein-1 when skin is involved), desmosomal cadherins that hold keratinocytes together; their disruption causes acantholysis with an intraepidermal, suprabasal split, leaving the characteristic 'row of tombstones.' BP180 at the basement membrane with a subepidermal split defines bullous pemphigoid. Tissue transglutaminase relates to dermatitis herpetiformis. Type VII collagen targeting causes epidermolysis bullosa acquisita. The desmoglein-3/suprabasal mechanism explains the flaccid bullae, mucosal predominance, and positive Nikolsky sign.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe pemphigus vulgaris?
options: High-potency topical corticosteroids alone
Systemic corticosteroids, often combined with rituximab
Suppressive antiviral therapy
A strict gluten-free diet
stages.explanation: Pemphigus vulgaris requires systemic immunosuppression; first-line therapy for moderate-to-severe disease is systemic corticosteroids combined with rituximab, which improves remission rates and is now favored as a steroid-sparing anchor; other adjuncts include azathioprine or mycophenolate. Topical steroids alone are inadequate for this potentially life-threatening, widespread autoimmune disease. Suppressive antivirals treat recurrent HSV-associated erythema multiforme, not pemphigus. A gluten-free diet is the treatment for dermatitis herpetiformis and has no role in pemphigus vulgaris. Aggressive immunosuppression with supportive wound and nutritional care is essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before recognition of effective therapy, the leading cause of death in pemphigus vulgaris was:
options: Malignant transformation of skin lesions
Sepsis and fluid/electrolyte loss from widespread erosions
Renal failure from autoantibody deposition
Pulmonary fibrosis
stages.explanation: Untreated pemphigus vulgaris is potentially fatal, historically chiefly from sepsis and fluid/protein/electrolyte loss through extensive denuded skin and mucosa, compounded by poor oral intake and malnutrition — underscoring the need for wound care, infection surveillance, and nutritional support alongside immunosuppression. The lesions do not undergo malignant transformation as a cause of death. The autoantibodies target desmosomal cadherins in skin, not the kidney, so renal failure is not characteristic. Pulmonary fibrosis is unrelated. Effective immunosuppression has markedly reduced mortality, but barrier loss and secondary infection remain the central threats.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Which test is most useful for monitoring pemphigus vulgaris disease activity over time?
options: Serial anti-desmoglein antibody titers (ELISA)
Repeated full-thickness skin biopsies weekly
Serum BP180 titers
Tissue transglutaminase IgA levels
stages.explanation: Serial anti-desmoglein (especially anti-desmoglein-3) antibody titers by ELISA correlate with disease activity and are useful for monitoring response to therapy and anticipating relapse in pemphigus vulgaris. Repeated weekly full-thickness biopsies are unnecessary, morbid, and not how activity is tracked. BP180 titers monitor bullous pemphigoid, not pemphigus. Tissue transglutaminase IgA tracks celiac disease/dermatitis herpetiformis. Combining clinical assessment with serial desmoglein titers allows clinicians to taper immunosuppression appropriately and detect early relapse.

pearls: PV: mucosa-first painful erosions, flaccid bullae, positive Nikolsky; suprabasal acantholysis.
DIF shows intercellular 'fishnet' IgG; serology shows anti-desmoglein-3 (±dsg-1) antibodies.
First-line for moderate-severe disease: systemic corticosteroids plus rituximab.
Serial anti-desmoglein titers track disease activity and guide therapy tapering.

cases.title: Maddeningly Itchy Bumps on the Elbows and Knees
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of intensely itchy, grouped bumps and blisters on the elbows, knees, and buttocks.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents with several months of severely itchy, grouped small bumps and tiny blisters symmetrically on his elbows, knees, buttocks, and scalp. The itch is so intense he often scratches the vesicles away before he can show them, leaving crusts and excoriations. He has tried over-the-counter hydrocortisone without relief. He reports intermittent bloating, loose stools, and some unintended weight loss over the past year, which he attributed to stress. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history is notable for a sister with 'thyroid problems' and a cousin with celiac disease. Review of systems reveals fatigue and the gastrointestinal symptoms above; he denies fever, joint pain, or mouth sores. He follows a typical Western diet that includes bread and pasta. The itching is worst at night and disrupts his sleep, and the rash keeps recurring in the same symmetric locations despite topical treatments.
cases.exam: On the extensor elbows, knees, buttocks, and posterior scalp there are symmetric, grouped (herpetiform) erythematous papulovesicles, many excoriated with crusting; intact vesicles are small and few because of scratching. The distribution is strikingly symmetric over extensor surfaces. There is no mucosal involvement and no nail changes. There is mild conjunctival pallor suggesting anemia. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. Nikolsky sign is negative. There is no lymphadenopathy. The lesions are intensely pruritic, and the picture is dominated by excoriations rather than intact blisters.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given intensely pruritic grouped vesicles symmetrically on extensor surfaces with GI symptoms?
options: Scabies
Dermatitis herpetiformis
Bullous pemphigoid
Atopic dermatitis
stages.explanation: Dermatitis herpetiformis presents with intensely pruritic, grouped (herpetiform) papulovesicles symmetrically distributed over extensor surfaces — elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp — and is the cutaneous manifestation of gluten sensitivity, explaining his GI symptoms and family history of celiac disease. Scabies favors finger webs, wrists, and genitals with burrows and household spread, not symmetric extensor grouping. Bullous pemphigoid produces tense bullae in the elderly. Atopic dermatitis favors flexural surfaces with lichenification and a personal atopic history. The extensor, symmetric, herpetiform pattern with celiac features strongly suggests DH.

stages.label: BIOPSY & SEROLOGY
stages.content:
DIF (perilesional, normal skin): granular IgA deposits in the dermal papillae
H&E: subepidermal vesicle with neutrophilic microabscesses in dermal papillae
Serum: anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA elevated; anti-endomysial antibody positive
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (microcytic)
Ferritin 14 ng/mL

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which finding on direct immunofluorescence confirms dermatitis herpetiformis?
options: Linear IgG and C3 at the basement membrane zone
Granular IgA deposits in the dermal papillae
Intercellular 'fishnet' IgG within the epidermis
Absence of any immune deposits
stages.explanation: The diagnostic DIF finding in dermatitis herpetiformis is granular IgA deposition in the dermal papillae of perilesional normal-appearing skin — the gold-standard confirmatory test. Linear IgG and C3 at the basement membrane zone indicate bullous pemphigoid. Intercellular 'fishnet' IgG within the epidermis indicates pemphigus vulgaris. Absence of immune deposits would argue against an immunobullous disease. The granular IgA pattern reflects the immune complexes that drive the neutrophilic dermal papillary microabscesses seen on H&E and ties DH directly to gluten-sensitive enteropathy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important long-term treatment for this condition?
options: Lifelong systemic corticosteroids
A strict, lifelong gluten-free diet
Topical antifungal therapy
Continuous antihistamines alone
stages.explanation: A strict, lifelong gluten-free diet is the cornerstone of dermatitis herpetiformis management; it treats both the skin disease and the underlying celiac enteropathy, reduces medication needs over time, and lowers the long-term risk of complications including small-bowel lymphoma. Systemic corticosteroids are not the mainstay and carry cumulative toxicity. Topical antifungals are irrelevant to this autoimmune, gluten-driven disease. Antihistamines alone do not control the disease. Dapsone provides rapid relief of itch and skin lesions and is commonly used initially, but it does not treat the enteropathy and the gluten-free diet remains the essential long-term therapy.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: Dapsone gives rapid symptom relief in DH. Which test should be checked before starting it?
options: Serum IgE level
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) activity
Antinuclear antibody (ANA)
Serum copper level
stages.explanation: Dapsone can precipitate severe hemolysis in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, so G6PD activity must be checked before initiating therapy; methemoglobinemia and agranulocytosis are additional risks requiring monitoring of the CBC. Serum IgE is irrelevant to dapsone safety. ANA is used to evaluate connective tissue disease, not dapsone toxicity. Serum copper is unrelated. Screening for G6PD deficiency and monitoring blood counts make dapsone safe and effective for rapidly controlling the intense pruritus while the gluten-free diet takes effect.

pearls: DH = intensely pruritic grouped vesicles, symmetric on extensor surfaces; the cutaneous sign of celiac disease.
DIF shows granular IgA in dermal papillae (gold standard); check tTG-IgA and endomysial antibodies.
Lifelong gluten-free diet is the essential treatment and lowers lymphoma risk; dapsone gives rapid relief.
Always check G6PD before starting dapsone to avoid severe hemolysis.

cases.title: Tender Red Lumps on Both Shins
cases.chiefComplaint: Several painful, tender red lumps on both shins over the past two weeks.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman presents with two weeks of painful, tender, red-to-purple nodules on the front of both lower legs. The lumps are warm and exquisitely tender but have not ulcerated. She also reports low-grade fevers, fatigue, achy ankles and knees, and a recent dry cough. About three weeks ago she had a sore throat that resolved.
