Slit-lamp exam reveals several small, dome-shaped, tan-brown elevations on both irides. No proptosis. Spine straight with no obvious scoliosis. Neuro exam grossly normal; no focal deficits. No hypopigmented ash-leaf macules and no facial papules. Cardiac, pulmonary, and abdominal exams unremarkable. No palpable masses.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings is most specific for the suspected diagnosis?
options: A single large café-au-lait macule with overlying coarse hair
Six or more café-au-lait macules plus axillary/inguinal freckling
Hypopigmented macules plus a shagreen patch
A solitary port-wine stain plus seizures
stages.explanation: Six or more café-au-lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal) combined with axillary or inguinal (Crowe sign) freckling are two of the diagnostic criteria for neurofibromatosis type 1 and are highly characteristic — option B is correct. A is wrong: a single CALM with coarse overlying hair suggests a congenital nevus or McCune-Albright-type lesion, not NF1. C is wrong: hypopigmented macules and a shagreen patch are features of tuberous sclerosis. D is wrong: a port-wine stain with seizures suggests Sturge-Weber, a different neurocutaneous disorder. The number and pattern of pigmented lesions here point clearly to NF1.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The tan-brown dome-shaped iris lesions seen on slit-lamp exam are best described as which finding?
options: Brushfield spots
Kayser-Fleischer rings
Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas)
Cherry-red spots
stages.explanation: Lisch nodules are pigmented iris hamartomas that appear as dome-shaped tan-brown elevations and are a hallmark of NF1, found in the majority of children by school age — option C is correct. A is wrong: Brushfield spots are speckled white/gray peripheral iris spots associated with Down syndrome. B is wrong: Kayser-Fleischer rings are copper deposits at the corneal limbus seen in Wilson disease. D is wrong: a cherry-red macular spot is a retinal (not iris) finding in lysosomal storage diseases. Lisch nodules are asymptomatic but add a major diagnostic criterion for NF1.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which screening evaluation is most important to perform given this diagnosis and his school difficulties?
options: Routine annual blood pressure and developmental/learning assessment, with ophthalmologic monitoring
Immediate whole-body PET-CT to find malignancy
Echocardiogram to screen for cardiac rhabdomyomas
Renal ultrasound to screen for angiomyolipomas
stages.explanation: NF1 management centers on regular surveillance: annual blood pressure (risk of pheochromocytoma and renal artery stenosis), monitoring for learning disabilities/ADHD (very common and consistent with his school struggles), and ophthalmologic evaluation for optic pathway gliomas. Option A is correct. B is wrong: routine whole-body PET-CT is not standard screening and carries radiation risk without indication. C is wrong: cardiac rhabdomyomas are associated with tuberous sclerosis, not NF1. D is wrong: renal angiomyolipomas are a tuberous sclerosis feature; while NF1 has its own renovascular concerns, routine renal ultrasound screening is not the priority over BP, learning, and eye surveillance.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which tumor is a recognized cause of vision loss in young children with this condition and should prompt ophthalmologic surveillance?
options: Optic pathway (optic nerve) glioma
Retinoblastoma
Choroidal melanoma
Vestibular schwannoma
stages.explanation: Optic pathway gliomas (typically low-grade pilocytic astrocytomas) occur in roughly 15% of NF1 children, usually before age 6, and can cause proptosis, decreased visual acuity, or precocious puberty if the hypothalamus is involved — hence routine eye exams. Option A is correct. B is wrong: retinoblastoma is a sporadic/RB1-related malignancy not characteristic of NF1. C is wrong: choroidal melanoma is an adult uveal malignancy, not an NF1 childhood concern. D is wrong: bilateral vestibular schwannomas are the hallmark of neurofibromatosis type 2, a genetically distinct disorder, not NF1.

pearls: NF1 diagnostic criteria include ≥6 café-au-lait macules, axillary/inguinal freckling (Crowe sign), Lisch nodules, neurofibromas, optic glioma, a distinctive bony lesion, and an affected first-degree relative.
Lisch nodules are pigmented iris hamartomas and are highly specific for NF1.
Learning disabilities and ADHD are the most common neurologic manifestations of NF1 in children.
Surveillance includes annual blood pressure (pheochromocytoma, renal artery stenosis), ophthalmologic exams (optic pathway glioma), and monitoring for plexiform neurofibroma and MPNST transformation.

cases.title: A Girl With Seizures and Pale Skin Patches
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents bring their daughter for worsening seizures and several light spots and facial bumps on her skin.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl is referred for difficult-to-control seizures that began in infancy as brief flexor spasms in clusters and have evolved into focal seizures with impaired awareness. She has mild developmental delay and behavioral concerns including features suggestive of autism spectrum disorder. Parents have noticed light-colored oval patches on her trunk since infancy and, more recently, small reddish bumps appearing across her cheeks and nose that they initially thought was acne. There is also a rough, slightly raised, skin-colored plaque on her lower back. PMH: infantile spasms treated previously; no cardiac symptoms. Medications: levetiracetam 30 mg/kg/day divided BID. No known drug allergies. Family history: no known seizures or similar skin findings in parents, though neither parent has had formal evaluation. Social history: attends a special-needs preschool; lives with both parents. Review of systems negative for hematuria, flank pain, and shortness of breath. She has not had renal or cardiac imaging.
cases.exam: Cooperative but with limited expressive language. Skin: four hypopigmented oval macules (ash-leaf shaped) on the trunk, better seen under Wood lamp; multiple firm, red-pink dome-shaped papules in a butterfly distribution over the malar cheeks and nasolabial folds; a 4-cm flesh-colored, pebbly, slightly elevated plaque over the lumbar spine (shagreen patch); and a small fibrous papule at the lateral nail fold of one toe. Neuro: nonfocal motor exam; developmentally delayed. Fundoscopic exam shows a few subtle whitish retinal lesions. No café-au-lait macules and no port-wine stain. Cardiac exam normal with no murmur. Abdomen soft, no masses.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of hypopigmented ash-leaf macules, facial angiofibromas, and a shagreen patch most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Neurofibromatosis type 1
Tuberous sclerosis complex
Sturge-Weber syndrome
Incontinentia pigmenti
stages.explanation: Ash-leaf (hypomelanotic) macules, facial angiofibromas (often mistaken for acne), and a shagreen patch are three classic cutaneous criteria for tuberous sclerosis complex, especially with infantile spasms and developmental delay — option B is correct. A is wrong: NF1 produces hyperpigmented café-au-lait macules and neurofibromas, not hypopigmented macules or angiofibromas. C is wrong: Sturge-Weber causes a port-wine capillary malformation and leptomeningeal angiomatosis, not these lesions. D is wrong: incontinentia pigmenti follows Blaschko lines with vesicular, verrucous, and swirled hyperpigmented stages, an entirely different pattern.

stages.label: IMAGING & GENETICS
stages.content:
MRI brain:
Multiple cortical tubers in frontal and parietal lobes
Subependymal nodules along the lateral ventricles
A 9-mm enhancing lesion near the foramen of Monro concerning for subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA)
Renal ultrasound:
Bilateral small angiomyolipomas
Echocardiogram:
Small regressing cardiac rhabdomyoma, no outflow obstruction
Genetic testing:
Pathogenic TSC2 mutation identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which intracranial finding near the foramen of Monro is the characteristic tumor of this condition and can cause obstructive hydrocephalus?
options: Medulloblastoma
Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA)
Craniopharyngioma
Pilocytic astrocytoma of the optic nerve
stages.explanation: A SEGA classically arises from subependymal nodules near the foramen of Monro in tuberous sclerosis and can obstruct CSF flow, causing hydrocephalus — option B is correct and matches the enhancing lesion on MRI. A is wrong: medulloblastoma is a posterior fossa malignancy not associated with TSC. C is wrong: craniopharyngioma arises in the sellar/suprasellar region from Rathke pouch remnants, unrelated to TSC. D is wrong: optic nerve pilocytic astrocytoma (optic pathway glioma) is the NF1-associated tumor. SEGAs may be treated with mTOR inhibitors or surgery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication class targets the underlying molecular pathway and can treat both the SEGA and renal angiomyolipomas in this disease?
options: Tumor necrosis factor (TNF) inhibitors
mTOR inhibitors (e.g., everolimus, sirolimus)
Calcineurin inhibitors
Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) inhibitors
stages.explanation: TSC is caused by loss of TSC1/TSC2, which normally inhibit the mTOR pathway; loss leads to mTOR overactivation. mTOR inhibitors such as everolimus shrink SEGAs and renal angiomyolipomas and can reduce seizure burden — option B is correct. A is wrong: TNF inhibitors target inflammatory cytokines (psoriasis, IBD), not the mTOR pathway. C is wrong: calcineurin inhibitors are immunosuppressants used in transplant and atopic dermatitis, not targeted TSC therapy. D is wrong: VEGF inhibitors are antiangiogenic agents for various cancers but are not the pathway-specific therapy for TSC.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond brain and renal imaging, which additional first-line antiseizure agent is specifically preferred for infantile spasms when they occur in tuberous sclerosis?
options: Vigabatrin
Phenytoin
Ethosuximide
Carbamazepine
stages.explanation: Vigabatrin is the preferred first-line therapy for infantile spasms specifically in the setting of tuberous sclerosis and is often dramatically effective — option A is correct (it is monitored for peripheral visual field loss). B is wrong: phenytoin is a sodium-channel blocker for focal/generalized tonic-clonic seizures, not first-line for spasms. C is wrong: ethosuximide treats absence seizures only. D is wrong: carbamazepine can worsen or is not indicated for infantile spasms and may aggravate certain generalized epilepsies. The TSC association makes vigabatrin the standout choice here.

pearls: Cutaneous clues to tuberous sclerosis: hypomelanotic ash-leaf macules (Wood lamp), facial angiofibromas, shagreen patch, and periungual fibromas.
Neurologic features include cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, SEGA (foramen of Monro), infantile spasms, and developmental delay.
Vigabatrin is the preferred agent for infantile spasms in TSC; mTOR inhibitors (everolimus) treat SEGA and renal angiomyolipomas.
Multisystem surveillance is essential: brain MRI, renal imaging (angiomyolipomas), echocardiography (rhabdomyomas), and ophthalmologic exam (retinal hamartomas).

cases.title: A Man Turned Red All Over and Shivering
cases.chiefComplaint: A man is admitted with diffuse red, scaly skin covering nearly his entire body and uncontrollable shivering.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man is admitted after two weeks of progressively worsening generalized redness and scaling that began on his trunk and spread to nearly his entire body. He reports intense itching, skin tightness, malaise, and feeling cold despite blankets. He has a 20-year history of plaque psoriasis previously controlled on topicals, but he ran out of his medications a month ago and was recently started on a course of oral prednisone by an urgent-care clinician for 'a flare,' which was then abruptly stopped. He also takes lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: retired, lives alone, drinks alcohol occasionally, former smoker. Family history: psoriasis in a brother. Review of systems: poor oral intake, lightheadedness on standing, and shedding large amounts of skin scale onto his bedsheets. No fever at home but episodic chills and rigors.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, shivering man. Confluent erythema involving an estimated 90% of body surface area with diffuse fine scaling and skin warmth that is paradoxically lost peripherally; skin feels tight and edematous. Diffuse non-tender lymphadenopathy in cervical and inguinal regions (likely dermatopathic). Palms and soles thickened. Nails show pitting and onycholysis. Lower-extremity pitting edema is present. Mucous membranes spared. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur; jugular venous pressure mildly elevated. Lungs clear. No target lesions, no Nikolsky sign, no bullae. He is mildly tachypneic and uncomfortable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This patient meets the definition of erythroderma. Which physiologic complication explains his low body temperature and tachycardia?
options: Vasoconstriction of skin vessels reducing heat loss
Massive cutaneous vasodilation causing heat loss, fluid shifts, and high-output cardiac demand
Central hypothalamic failure from psoriasis
Hypothyroidism induced by topical steroids
stages.explanation: Erythroderma (>90% BSA erythema) causes massive cutaneous vasodilation, leading to uncontrolled transepidermal heat and water loss (hypothermia), insensible fluid loss with hypovolemia, and a high-output state straining the heart. Option B is correct and explains hypothermia, tachycardia, and edema. A is wrong: vasoconstriction would retain heat — the opposite of what occurs in erythroderma. C is wrong: there is no hypothalamic/central thermoregulatory failure; the loss is peripheral. D is wrong: while hypothyroidism causes cold intolerance, it does not explain the acute erythroderma; the mechanism here is cutaneous, not endocrine.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL
Na 132 mEq/L
K 3.4 mEq/L
Albumin 2.6 g/dL
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
BUN 32 mg/dL
Lactate 1.8 mmol/L
High-output state on bedside echo: hyperdynamic LV, no effusion
Blood cultures: pending
Skin biopsy: psoriasiform hyperplasia with parakeratosis, consistent with psoriasis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his history, which factor is the most likely precipitant of this erythrodermic flare?
options: Abrupt withdrawal of systemic corticosteroids in a psoriasis patient
Atorvastatin therapy
Chronic lisinopril use
Occasional alcohol consumption
stages.explanation: Abrupt withdrawal of systemic corticosteroids is a classic trigger for erythrodermic (and pustular) psoriasis — a key reason systemic steroids are generally avoided in plaque psoriasis. He was given prednisone then stopped it abruptly, fitting the timeline. Option A is correct. B is wrong: atorvastatin is not a recognized trigger of erythrodermic psoriasis. C is wrong: while ACE inhibitors can rarely aggravate psoriasis, abrupt steroid withdrawal is the far more likely and classic precipitant here. D is wrong: occasional alcohol can worsen psoriasis modestly but does not explain this acute, severe flare like steroid withdrawal does.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of this erythrodermic patient?
options: Discharge with topical corticosteroids and outpatient follow-up
Restart high-dose oral prednisone immediately
Admit for supportive care—warming, IV fluids/electrolyte repletion, emollients—and initiate appropriate psoriasis-directed therapy
Start broad empiric chemotherapy for presumed cutaneous lymphoma
stages.explanation: Erythroderma is a dermatologic emergency. Initial care is supportive: thermoregulation/warming, IV fluid and electrolyte repletion, nutritional support, bland emollients and wet wraps, and treating the underlying cause — here, restarting effective psoriasis therapy (e.g., cyclosporine, acitretin, or biologics rather than systemic steroids). Option C is correct. A is wrong: he is hemodynamically unstable and hypothermic — outpatient management is unsafe. B is wrong: reintroducing systemic steroids risks rebound and is the very class implicated in the flare. D is wrong: biopsy shows psoriasis, not lymphoma; empiric chemotherapy is inappropriate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which additional life-threatening complication should be actively monitored for in severe erythroderma?
options: Secondary infection/sepsis and high-output heart failure
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Pancreatitis
Spontaneous pneumothorax
stages.explanation: The compromised skin barrier in erythroderma predisposes to bacterial superinfection (notably Staphylococcus aureus) and sepsis, while the high-output vasodilated state can precipitate heart failure — especially in older patients. Both are leading causes of death and are correctly chosen in option A; his elevated JVP, edema, and tachycardia warrant cardiac monitoring, and chills/rigors warrant infection surveillance. B, C, and D are not characteristic complications of erythroderma — angle-closure glaucoma, pancreatitis, and pneumothorax have no mechanistic link to the diffuse skin failure and hemodynamic derangement seen here.

cases.diagnosis: Erythroderma (erythrodermic psoriasis, precipitated by systemic corticosteroid withdrawal)
pearls: Erythroderma = erythema/scaling over ≥90% BSA; it is a dermatologic emergency causing hypothermia, fluid loss, and high-output cardiac strain.
Abrupt systemic corticosteroid withdrawal is a classic trigger of erythrodermic and pustular psoriasis—avoid systemic steroids in plaque psoriasis.
Common causes: psoriasis, atopic/contact dermatitis, drug reactions, and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome)—always consider malignancy.
Management is supportive (warming, fluids, electrolytes, emollients) plus treating the underlying cause; watch for sepsis and heart failure.

cases.title: A Teen With a Sudden Shower of Small Scaly Spots
cases.chiefComplaint: A teenager presents with the rapid appearance of many small, scaly red spots across her trunk.
cases.history: A 17-year-old girl presents with a 4-day history of numerous small, red, scaly spots that appeared suddenly and spread rapidly across her trunk, upper arms, and thighs. They are mildly itchy but not painful. About two weeks ago she had a sore throat with fever that resolved without treatment; she did not see a clinician at the time. She has no prior history of skin disease. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none; she has not started any new drugs (no lithium, no antimalarials, no beta-blockers). No known drug allergies. Social history: high-school student, sexually active with one partner, uses condoms inconsistently; no tobacco or drug use. Family history: a maternal uncle has psoriasis. Review of systems: denies genital lesions, denies recent rash on palms or soles, denies joint pain, denies fevers currently. She is anxious about the appearance before an upcoming event.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent. Skin shows numerous discrete, 2-10 mm, salmon-pink, drop-shaped (guttate) papules and small plaques with fine silvery scale, concentrated on the trunk and proximal extremities, sparing the palms and soles. Gentle scale removal reveals pinpoint bleeding points. Scalp has a few small scaly plaques at the hairline. No lesions on palms, soles, or genitalia; no mucous-membrane involvement; no lymphadenopathy. Oropharynx is no longer erythematous. Nails are normal without pitting. No annular scaling plaques with central clearing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The pinpoint bleeding seen after removing the silvery scale is best described as which sign?
options: Nikolsky sign
Auspitz sign
Darier sign
Koebner phenomenon
stages.explanation: The Auspitz sign is pinpoint bleeding produced when the scale of a psoriatic plaque is removed, reflecting dilated dermal capillaries beneath thinned suprapapillary epidermis — option B is correct. A is wrong: the Nikolsky sign is sloughing of skin with lateral pressure, seen in pemphigus and SJS/TEN. C is wrong: Darier sign is wheal formation when a mastocytosis lesion is stroked. D is wrong: the Koebner phenomenon is the appearance of lesions at sites of trauma (which does occur in psoriasis) but is not what bleeding-on-scale-removal describes. The Auspitz sign supports a psoriasiform process.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the abrupt drop-like eruption two weeks after a sore throat, which precipitating infection is most likely?
options: Group A streptococcal (Streptococcus pyogenes) pharyngitis
Primary HIV infection
Secondary syphilis
Epstein-Barr virus mononucleosis
stages.explanation: Guttate psoriasis classically erupts 1-3 weeks after group A streptococcal pharyngitis, particularly in children and young adults, due to immune cross-reactivity (superantigen-driven T-cell activation). Option A is correct and fits the preceding sore throat. B is wrong: acute HIV causes a morbilliform rash with mononucleosis-like illness, not silvery-scaled guttate papules with the Auspitz sign. C is wrong: secondary syphilis characteristically involves the palms and soles, which are spared here. D is wrong: EBV mononucleosis can cause a rash (especially after aminopenicillins) but not this guttate, Auspitz-positive eruption tied to strep.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which test is most appropriate to confirm the recent streptococcal trigger?
options: Skin biopsy of a lesion for direct immunofluorescence
Throat culture or anti-streptolysin O (ASO)/anti-DNase B titers
RPR and treponemal antibody testing only
Patch testing for contact allergens
stages.explanation: A throat culture (if still symptomatic) or, more usefully given timing, elevated anti-streptococcal antibody titers (ASO, anti-DNase B) help confirm the recent strep infection that precipitated guttate psoriasis. Option B is correct. A is wrong: direct immunofluorescence is used for autoimmune blistering disease, not psoriasis confirmation. C is wrong: while sexually active, her presentation (silvery scale, Auspitz sign, palm/sole sparing, strep prodrome) is classic guttate psoriasis, not syphilis—though STI screening is reasonable separately. D is wrong: patch testing evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, which does not fit this acute post-strep guttate eruption.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this patient's eruption?
options: Systemic methotrexate
Oral systemic corticosteroids
Topical corticosteroids ± vitamin D analogues, with phototherapy (narrowband UVB) for widespread disease
Oral isotretinoin
stages.explanation: Guttate psoriasis is often self-limited but is treated with topical corticosteroids and vitamin D analogues (calcipotriene); narrowband UVB phototherapy is first-line for more widespread cutaneous involvement. Option C is correct. A is wrong: methotrexate is reserved for severe or refractory psoriasis, not first-line for a first guttate episode. B is wrong: systemic steroids can trigger rebound/pustular flares and are avoided in psoriasis. D is wrong: isotretinoin is used for acne and some keratinization disorders; it is not standard psoriasis therapy (acitretin, a different retinoid, has a role in severe psoriasis but not as first-line here).

pearls: Guttate psoriasis presents as a sudden shower of small, drop-shaped, scaly salmon-pink papules, classically 1-3 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis in young patients.
The Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal) and Koebner phenomenon support a psoriatic process.
Palms, soles, and mucous membranes are spared—an important distinction from secondary syphilis.
First-line therapy is topical steroids plus vitamin D analogues, with narrowband UVB phototherapy for widespread disease; many cases resolve over weeks to months.

cases.title: A Man With Swollen Fingers and Pitted Nails
cases.chiefComplaint: A man presents with a painful, uniformly swollen finger and several stiff, aching joints.
cases.history: A 46-year-old man with a 10-year history of plaque psoriasis presents with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, pain in the small joints of his hands and feet, and one entirely swollen, sausage-like finger that has been tender for weeks. He also has low-back stiffness that improves with activity. His psoriasis is currently mild, managed with topical agents. PMH: hypertension, obesity. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, topical calcipotriene/betamethasone, occasional ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. Social history: works in construction, drinks 4-5 beers per week, does not smoke. Family history: father with psoriasis; no rheumatoid arthritis or gout in the family. Review of systems: denies fevers, denies eye redness/pain currently, denies urethral discharge or diarrhea, denies tophi. He notes that the affected nail has small pits and is lifting from the nail bed.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, overweight man. Skin: scattered scaly erythematous plaques on the elbows and scalp; gluteal cleft erythema. The right third finger is diffusely swollen from base to tip (dactylitis, 'sausage digit') and tender. There is tenderness at the right Achilles insertion (enthesitis). Several distal interphalangeal joints are tender and mildly swollen. Nails show multiple pits, onycholysis, and an oil-drop discoloration. Reduced lumbar flexion. No tophi, no rheumatoid nodules, no synovitis of the MCPs in a symmetric rheumatoid pattern. No conjunctival injection. Peripheral pulses intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The diffusely swollen 'sausage' finger and tenderness at the Achilles insertion represent which two characteristic features of this disease?
options: Synovitis and tophus formation
Dactylitis and enthesitis
Tenosynovitis and bursitis only
Osteophyte formation and crepitus
stages.explanation: Dactylitis (uniform 'sausage digit' swelling from combined joint and tendon-sheath inflammation) and enthesitis (inflammation at tendon/ligament insertions such as the Achilles) are hallmark features of psoriatic arthritis and the seronegative spondyloarthropathies — option B is correct. A is wrong: tophi are urate deposits seen in gout, not PsA. C is wrong: while tenosynovitis contributes, 'tenosynovitis and bursitis only' omits the defining enthesitis and dactylitis terminology. D is wrong: osteophytes and crepitus describe osteoarthritis, a non-inflammatory degenerative process, inconsistent with the inflammatory morning stiffness and dactylitis here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 38 mm/hr
CRP 2.4 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP antibody: negative
Serum uric acid 6.2 mg/dL
HLA-B27: positive
Hand/foot radiographs: erosive changes at a DIP joint with adjacent bony proliferation; early 'pencil-in-cup' deformity
Sacroiliac imaging: asymmetric sacroiliitis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The radiographic 'pencil-in-cup' deformity together with DIP involvement, negative RF/anti-CCP, and nail changes best supports which diagnosis?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis
Gouty arthritis
Psoriatic arthritis
Osteoarthritis
stages.explanation: Psoriatic arthritis is a seronegative (RF/anti-CCP negative) spondyloarthropathy; the 'pencil-in-cup' deformity, DIP-joint involvement, dactylitis, enthesitis, nail pitting/onycholysis, and psoriasis history are classic — option C is correct. A is wrong: rheumatoid arthritis is typically symmetric, spares DIPs, and is RF/anti-CCP positive with marginal erosions (no bony proliferation). B is wrong: gout would show negatively birefringent crystals and tophi with a normal-to-high but variable urate; his uric acid is not diagnostic and the pattern fits PsA. D is wrong: osteoarthritis is non-inflammatory with osteophytes and Heberden nodes, not erosive 'pencil-in-cup' change with elevated inflammatory markers.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this patient with active peripheral arthritis, dactylitis, and erosive disease, which is the most appropriate disease-modifying treatment strategy?
options: NSAIDs alone indefinitely
Start a conventional DMARD (e.g., methotrexate); escalate to a TNF inhibitor or other biologic if inadequate response or erosive/axial disease
Long-term oral prednisone monotherapy
Allopurinol to lower uric acid
stages.explanation: Active psoriatic arthritis with erosions warrants disease-modifying therapy. Methotrexate is commonly used for peripheral arthritis and skin disease, with escalation to TNF inhibitors (or IL-17/IL-23 inhibitors) for inadequate response, erosive disease, or axial involvement — option B is correct. A is wrong: NSAIDs relieve symptoms but do not prevent erosive joint damage. C is wrong: chronic systemic steroids are avoided in psoriatic disease (rebound/erythrodermic flare risk on tapering) and do not adequately control erosive arthritis. D is wrong: allopurinol treats gout; his presentation is PsA, so urate-lowering therapy is not indicated.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which extra-articular manifestation should this HLA-B27-positive patient be counseled to report urgently?
options: Acute anterior uveitis (painful red eye with photophobia)
Painless gradual hearing loss
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Raynaud phenomenon
stages.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis is a recognized extra-articular feature of HLA-B27-associated spondyloarthropathies, including psoriatic arthritis; it presents with a painful red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision and requires urgent ophthalmologic treatment to prevent complications — option A is correct. B is wrong: progressive painless hearing loss is not a feature of PsA. C is wrong: carpal tunnel syndrome is a compressive neuropathy not specific to PsA. D is wrong: Raynaud phenomenon is associated with connective-tissue diseases like scleroderma and lupus, not characteristically with PsA/HLA-B27 spondyloarthropathies.

pearls: Psoriatic arthritis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy: RF and anti-CCP are typically negative and HLA-B27 may be positive (especially with axial disease).
Hallmark features include dactylitis ('sausage digit'), enthesitis, DIP-joint involvement, nail pitting/onycholysis, and the 'pencil-in-cup' radiographic deformity.
Treat-to-target therapy uses methotrexate for peripheral/skin disease and biologics (TNF, IL-17, IL-23 inhibitors) for refractory, erosive, or axial disease.
Screen for and counsel about HLA-B27-associated acute anterior uveitis—a painful red eye that needs urgent care.

cases.title: A Man With Itchy Coin-Shaped Patches on His Shins
cases.chiefComplaint: A man presents with several round, itchy, scaly patches on his lower legs that worsen in winter.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of multiple round, coin-shaped, intensely itchy patches on both shins and forearms. He notices they worsen in the dry winter months and after long hot showers. He has a history of 'sensitive, dry skin' and used to have eczema as a child. The patches are sometimes weepy and crusted from scratching. He denies a single expanding ring with central clearing. PMH: hypothyroidism, well controlled. Medications: levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, occasional over-the-counter antihistamine. No known drug allergies. Social history: works outdoors part of the year, bathes daily with hot water and deodorant soap, lives in a cold dry climate. Family history: atopic dermatitis and asthma in two siblings. Review of systems: denies pets at home recently, denies similar lesions in household contacts, denies fever, denies new medications. He has tried an OTC antifungal cream for two weeks without improvement.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man with diffusely dry, xerotic skin. On the anterior shins and extensor forearms are multiple sharply marginated, round-to-oval, coin-shaped (nummular) erythematous plaques 2-5 cm in diameter with scale, crust, and some pinpoint oozing; several are excoriated. The plaques lack central clearing and lack a raised advancing scaly border. No interdigital scaling between the toes; no nail changes. No id reactions on the trunk. KOH preparation performed in clinic shows no hyphae. No lymphadenopathy. Remainder of skin exam shows generalized xerosis with fine scaling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes these coin-shaped plaques from tinea corporis?
options: Presence of a single expanding annular plaque with central clearing and an active scaly border
Uniform scaling and crusting across the entire round plaque without central clearing, and a negative KOH
Location on the lower legs
Associated intense pruritus
stages.explanation: Nummular (discoid) eczema produces coin-shaped plaques that are uniformly scaly/crusted across the lesion without the central clearing typical of tinea, and KOH is negative for hyphae — option B is correct. A actually describes tinea corporis (annular, central clearing, active advancing border), so it is wrong as a distinguishing feature of eczema. C is wrong: location alone does not distinguish them—both can occur on the legs. D is wrong: pruritus occurs in both conditions and is nonspecific. A negative KOH plus uniform plaque morphology points to nummular eczema over tinea.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given an OTC antifungal failed and KOH is negative, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step before treating?
options: Order a punch biopsy of every lesion immediately
Confirm the negative KOH (and consider fungal culture if doubt remains), supporting a clinical diagnosis of nummular eczema
Start oral terbinafine empirically for presumed tinea
Order serum IgE and food allergy panel
stages.explanation: With a negative KOH, characteristic morphology, an atopic background, and winter xerosis, nummular eczema is a clinical diagnosis; confirming the negative KOH (and using fungal culture only if genuine doubt remains) is the appropriate, low-cost next step. Option B is correct. A is wrong: biopsy is reserved for atypical or refractory cases, not routine. C is wrong: empiric terbinafine is illogical after a failed antifungal and negative KOH—it would not treat eczema. D is wrong: serum IgE and food panels are not useful for diagnosing or managing nummular eczema and do not change management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment regimen?
options: Mid- to high-potency topical corticosteroid plus liberal emollients and gentle skin care (avoid hot water/harsh soaps)
Oral acyclovir
Topical antifungal cream continued for 6 more weeks
Systemic corticosteroids for a 3-week taper
stages.explanation: Nummular eczema is treated with mid- to high-potency topical corticosteroids for the inflamed plaques, generous emollients to repair the dry skin barrier, and trigger avoidance (limit hot showers and harsh/deodorant soaps) — option A is correct. B is wrong: acyclovir treats herpetic infections, not eczema. C is wrong: antifungals already failed and KOH is negative—continuing them is futile. D is wrong: systemic steroids are not first-line for localized nummular eczema and risk rebound and systemic adverse effects; topical therapy with barrier repair is preferred.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If a plaque becomes increasingly weepy with honey-colored crust and surrounding warmth despite topical steroids, what is the most likely complication?
options: Secondary Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization
Malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma
Development of tinea over the eczema
Contact urticaria
stages.explanation: Excoriated, weepy eczema commonly becomes secondarily infected with Staphylococcus aureus, producing honey-colored crusting, increased oozing, and surrounding erythema/warmth; this warrants topical or oral antibiotics in addition to eczema care — option A is correct. B is wrong: nummular eczema does not undergo malignant transformation, and SCC would present as a non-healing indurated lesion over months to years. C is wrong: superimposed tinea is uncommon and would not classically produce honey-colored crust. D is wrong: contact urticaria causes transient wheals on exposure, not honey-colored crusted, warm, oozing plaques.

pearls: Nummular eczema presents as intensely pruritic, coin-shaped, uniformly scaly/crusted plaques, often on the legs, worsening with winter xerosis and hot showers.
Unlike tinea corporis, nummular eczema lacks central clearing and has a negative KOH—always confirm before treating.
First-line therapy is mid/high-potency topical corticosteroids plus aggressive emollient use and avoidance of hot water and harsh soaps.
Weepy plaques with honey-colored crust suggest secondary Staphylococcus aureus infection requiring antibiotics.

cases.title: A Woman With Itchy Blisters on Her Palms
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents with recurrent, intensely itchy small blisters along the sides of her fingers and palms.
cases.history: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of intensely itchy, small, deep-seated blisters on her palms and the sides of her fingers. She reports similar episodes 2-3 times per year, often flaring in spring and during periods of stress, and when her hands sweat. The blisters eventually dry up and the skin peels. She is a hairdresser and frequently has her hands in water and handles hair products and nickel-containing tools. PMH: seasonal allergic rhinitis, childhood eczema. Medications: cetirizine as needed. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, social alcohol use, reports high stress recently. Family history: atopic dermatitis in her mother. Review of systems: denies fever, denies oral or genital lesions, denies systemic symptoms, denies new oral medications. She notes a pierced-ear rash years ago after wearing cheap earrings. She has tried OTC moisturizer with minimal relief.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman. On the lateral aspects of the fingers, the palms, and a few on the soles are numerous 1-3 mm deep-seated, clear, tense vesicles resembling tapioca pearls, without surrounding erythema in early lesions; some older areas show desquamation and fissuring. No vesicles on the dorsal hands, trunk, or mucous membranes. No honey-colored crust or pustules. No nail pitting. No interdigital toe scaling and KOH of a scraping is negative for hyphae. No target lesions. Remainder of the skin exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The deep-seated, tapioca-like vesicles on the lateral fingers and palms are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Dyshidrotic eczema (pompholyx)
Bullous pemphigoid
Palmar tinea (tinea manuum)
Herpetic whitlow
stages.explanation: Dyshidrotic eczema (pompholyx) classically produces crops of intensely pruritic, deep-seated 'tapioca' vesicles on the palms, lateral fingers, and soles, often triggered by stress, sweating, and contact irritants/allergens — option A is correct. B is wrong: bullous pemphigoid causes large tense bullae on an urticarial base in older adults, typically on flexures/trunk, not tapioca palmar vesicles. C is wrong: tinea manuum shows scaling (often one-hand-two-feet) with positive KOH; her KOH is negative. D is wrong: herpetic whitlow is a painful, grouped vesicular infection usually on a single digit, not symmetric pruritic palmar vesicles.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given her occupation and history of an earring rash, which evaluation may help identify a modifiable trigger?
options: Patch testing for contact allergens such as nickel
Skin biopsy for direct immunofluorescence
Tzanck smear
Serum protein electrophoresis
stages.explanation: Pompholyx can be exacerbated by contact allergens (notably nickel) and irritants; her hairdressing exposure and prior nickel earring reaction make patch testing a useful step to identify and avoid a modifiable trigger — option A is correct. B is wrong: direct immunofluorescence evaluates autoimmune blistering diseases (pemphigus/pemphigoid), which this is not. C is wrong: a Tzanck smear detects multinucleated giant cells in herpetic infections, not relevant to eczematous vesicles. D is wrong: serum protein electrophoresis screens for paraproteinemias and has no role in evaluating dyshidrotic eczema.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for her current flare?
options: Oral terbinafine for 6 weeks
High-potency topical corticosteroids, cool compresses/soaks, emollients, and avoidance of irritants/allergens (gloves at work)
Oral acyclovir
Long-term daily systemic corticosteroids
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of a dyshidrotic eczema flare is high-potency topical corticosteroids, supportive measures (cool soaks/compresses, emollients), and reducing exposure to irritants and allergens—using protective gloves at work and managing sweating/stress. Option B is correct. A is wrong: terbinafine treats dermatophyte infection, but KOH is negative. C is wrong: acyclovir is for herpes, not eczema. D is wrong: chronic systemic steroids are inappropriate for recurrent localized hand eczema given adverse effects; short bursts or steroid-sparing agents (e.g., topical calcineurin inhibitors, phototherapy) are reserved for refractory disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which long-term counseling point is most important to reduce recurrences for this patient?
options: Avoid all hand washing entirely
Implement hand protection (gloves), gentle skin care, emollient use, and identify/avoid contact triggers and excess moisture
Take prophylactic oral antibiotics daily
Switch to a low-iodine diet
stages.explanation: Chronic/relapsing pompholyx is best managed long-term with barrier protection (wearing gloves for wet/chemical work), frequent emollients, gentle non-irritating skin care, and avoidance of identified contact allergens (e.g., nickel) and excessive moisture/sweating — option B is correct. A is wrong: avoiding all hand washing is impractical and unhygienic; the goal is gentle washing and immediate moisturizing. C is wrong: prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated without recurrent infection. D is wrong: while systemic nickel ingestion is debated, a 'low-iodine diet' is not an evidence-based intervention for dyshidrotic eczema.

pearls: Dyshidrotic eczema presents as recurrent crops of deep-seated, intensely pruritic 'tapioca' vesicles on the palms, lateral fingers, and soles.
Triggers include stress, sweating (hyperhidrosis), warm weather, and contact irritants/allergens—nickel sensitivity is common.
Patch testing can identify modifiable contact triggers in atopic or occupationally exposed patients.
Management combines high-potency topical steroids, emollients, irritant/allergen avoidance with glove protection, and phototherapy or steroid-sparing agents for refractory disease.

cases.title: A Woman With a Thick Itchy Patch on the Back of Her Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents with a chronically itchy, thickened patch on the back of her neck that she cannot stop scratching.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents with a single, well-demarcated, intensely itchy thickened patch on the nape of her neck that has been present for over a year. She admits to rubbing and scratching it constantly, especially in the evenings and during stressful periods at work; the itch is relieved temporarily by scratching, which then perpetuates the cycle. The area becomes worse when she is anxious. PMH: generalized anxiety disorder, well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: office manager, high job stress, nonsmoker. Family history: atopic dermatitis in a daughter. Review of systems: denies similar lesions elsewhere, denies systemic symptoms, denies weight change, denies generalized pruritus. She reports the patch is the only itchy area and that scratching has become almost a habit. She has used OTC hydrocortisone intermittently with partial, temporary relief.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman. On the posterior neck/nape is a solitary, well-demarcated plaque approximately 5 x 4 cm that is thickened (lichenified) with exaggerated, accentuated skin lines, mild hyperpigmentation, scale, and several linear excoriations. No vesicles, pustules, or honey-colored crust. No annular border, and KOH is not indicated by morphology. No nail pitting or scalp plaques to suggest psoriasis. No other lichenified areas, no widespread xerosis, and no signs of systemic illness. Regional lymph nodes are not enlarged.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The accentuated skin markings (lichenification) in a single chronically scratched plaque are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Lichen planus
Lichen simplex chronicus
Plaque psoriasis
Tinea corporis
stages.explanation: Lichen simplex chronicus is a localized area of lichenification (thickened skin with exaggerated skin lines) resulting from a chronic itch-scratch cycle, classically on accessible sites such as the nape, ankles, and genitals — option B is correct and fits the solitary, accentuated plaque. A is wrong: lichen planus shows pruritic, polygonal, planar violaceous papules with Wickham striae, often on the wrists, not a single lichenified scratch plaque. C is wrong: psoriasis has well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale at typical extensor sites, usually multiple, with nail/scalp findings. D is wrong: tinea is annular with central clearing and a positive KOH.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor is central to the pathophysiology of this condition?
options: An autoimmune attack on basal keratinocytes
A self-perpetuating itch-scratch cycle, often exacerbated by stress/anxiety
Dermatophyte invasion of the stratum corneum
IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein
stages.explanation: Lichen simplex chronicus is driven by a self-perpetuating itch-scratch cycle: repeated rubbing/scratching thickens the skin, which itself itches more, often worsened by psychological stress and anxiety (as in this patient). Option B is correct. A is wrong: an autoimmune attack on basal keratinocytes describes lichen planus. C is wrong: dermatophyte invasion describes tinea, which has different morphology and a positive KOH. D is wrong: anti-desmoglein IgG autoantibodies cause pemphigus vulgaris, a blistering disease, not a lichenified scratch plaque.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral antifungal therapy
Mid- to high-potency topical corticosteroid (often under occlusion) plus measures to interrupt scratching, and address stress
Wide surgical excision of the plaque
Systemic chemotherapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy is a mid- to high-potency topical corticosteroid—often under occlusion to enhance penetration and act as a physical barrier against scratching—combined with behavioral strategies to break the itch-scratch cycle and address contributing stress/anxiety; antihistamines or topical calcineurin inhibitors can help. Option B is correct. A is wrong: there is no fungal infection (morphology and history fit LSC). C is wrong: surgical excision is unnecessary and would not address the underlying scratching behavior. D is wrong: this is a benign condition; chemotherapy has no role.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If the plaque fails to respond to potent topical steroids and good adherence, what is a reasonable next step?
options: Intralesional corticosteroid injection and/or evaluate for an alternative or coexisting diagnosis
Begin systemic corticosteroids for life
Start broad-spectrum oral antibiotics
Recommend daily exfoliation with abrasive scrubs
stages.explanation: For refractory lichen simplex chronicus, intralesional corticosteroid injection can deliver potent anti-inflammatory effect directly to the thickened plaque, and persistent non-response should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis (e.g., biopsy to exclude psoriasis, mycosis fungoides, or other entities) — option A is correct. B is wrong: lifelong systemic steroids are inappropriate for a localized benign condition. C is wrong: antibiotics are unnecessary without secondary infection. D is wrong: abrasive scrubbing is a form of mechanical trauma that perpetuates the itch-scratch cycle and would worsen the condition.

pearls: Lichen simplex chronicus is localized lichenification (thickened skin with accentuated lines) from a chronic itch-scratch cycle, classically on the nape, ankles, scalp, and genitals.
Stress and anxiety frequently drive the scratching behavior—addressing psychological factors is part of treatment.
First-line therapy is potent topical corticosteroids (often under occlusion) plus interrupting the scratch habit; intralesional steroids help refractory plaques.
Non-response should prompt biopsy to exclude psoriasis, lichen planus, or cutaneous T-cell lymphoma.

cases.title: A Woman With Crusted Itchy Bumps She Keeps Picking
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents with multiple intensely itchy, firm bumps that she repeatedly scratches and picks open.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with several months of multiple intensely itchy, firm bumps on her extensor arms, shins, and upper back. The itch is relentless and worse at night, and she admits to scratching and picking until they bleed and crust. She has lost some weight unintentionally and feels fatigued. PMH: type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease (stage 3), and depression. Medications: metformin 1000 mg BID, lisinopril 10 mg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, no alcohol, retired teacher. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: reports occasional night sweats, no fevers, no jaundice, no recent travel, no new pets, no sick contacts with itching (no scabies exposure reported). She is distressed by the appearance and the unrelenting itch, which interferes with sleep. She has tried multiple OTC antihistamines and moisturizers with little relief.
cases.exam: Thin, anxious-appearing woman. Skin shows numerous discrete, firm, dome-shaped, hyperpigmented and erythematous nodules 1-2 cm in diameter, many with central excoriation, crust, and scarring, symmetrically distributed on the extensor extremities and upper back in reachable areas; the mid-upper back center is relatively spared ('butterfly sign'). No burrows in finger webs, no genital lesions. No jaundice or scleral icterus. No lymphadenopathy palpated. Mild bilateral lower-extremity edema. No thyromegaly. No primary blisters or pustules. The lesions are intensely pruritic on history.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Multiple firm, intensely pruritic, excoriated nodules in reachable areas with sparing of the mid-upper back are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Prurigo nodularis
Nodular melanoma
Bullous pemphigoid
Scabies
stages.explanation: Prurigo nodularis presents as multiple firm, hyperkeratotic, intensely pruritic nodules from chronic scratching/picking, typically on extensor surfaces in reachable areas, with relative sparing of the central upper back ('butterfly sign') — option A is correct. B is wrong: nodular melanoma is usually a single pigmented growing lesion, not crops of symmetric pruritic nodules. C is wrong: bullous pemphigoid causes tense bullae on an urticarial base, not excoriated nodules. D is wrong: scabies causes burrows in finger webs/wrists and intense pruritus, but the morphology of large excoriated nodules with the butterfly-sign distribution and no burrows favors prurigo nodularis (though scabies should be excluded).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL with eosinophils 8%
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (eGFR 38 mL/min)
HbA1c 8.6%
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL (normal)
LDH mildly elevated
LFTs normal; bilirubin normal
HIV antibody: negative
Ferritin normal; iron studies normal
Further workup ordered given weight loss and night sweats

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because prurigo nodularis is frequently associated with an underlying cause of chronic itch, which workup is most appropriate given her systemic symptoms?
options: No further workup; treat the skin only
Evaluate for systemic causes of pruritus—renal/hepatic function, thyroid, glucose, HIV, iron studies, and screen for malignancy/lymphoma given weight loss and night sweats
Immediate Mohs surgery on each nodule
Empiric permethrin and declare scabies regardless of findings
stages.explanation: Prurigo nodularis is often secondary to chronic pruritus from systemic disease, so a targeted workup for underlying causes is essential: renal failure (she has CKD), cholestatic liver disease, thyroid dysfunction, diabetes, HIV, iron deficiency, and—given red flags like weight loss and night sweats—screening for malignancy/lymphoma. Option B is correct. A is wrong: ignoring an underlying systemic itch driver risks missing treatable or serious disease. C is wrong: Mohs surgery treats skin cancers, not benign prurigo nodules. D is wrong: while scabies should be excluded, reflexively declaring scabies and treating empirically without evaluation is inappropriate given her morphology and systemic features.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial dermatologic management while the systemic workup proceeds?
options: High-potency or intralesional corticosteroids, emollients, antipruritic measures, and interrupting the scratch cycle; treat any identified underlying cause
Oral isotretinoin
Daily systemic high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely
Topical antifungal monotherapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of prurigo nodularis targets the itch-scratch cycle: high-potency topical or intralesional corticosteroids for individual nodules, emollients, and antipruritic strategies (e.g., menthol, antihistamines, gabapentinoids, or topical calcineurin inhibitors), plus treating any identified underlying systemic cause. Option A is correct. B is wrong: isotretinoin is for acne/keratinization disorders, not prurigo nodularis. C is wrong: indefinite high-dose systemic steroids carry unacceptable risks, especially in a diabetic with CKD. D is wrong: there is no fungal infection; antifungal monotherapy would not address the pruritic nodules.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For severe, refractory prurigo nodularis unresponsive to topical therapy, which targeted systemic agent is now approved/used to control the underlying type 2 inflammation and itch?
options: Dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 receptor blocker)
Allopurinol
Oral metronidazole
Topical benzoyl peroxide
stages.explanation: Dupilumab, an IL-4Rα antagonist blocking IL-4/IL-13 signaling, is approved for prurigo nodularis and effectively reduces nodules and pruritus in refractory disease; other options include gabapentinoids, phototherapy, and immunosuppressants. Option A is correct. B is wrong: allopurinol lowers uric acid for gout and has no role here. C is wrong: metronidazole is an antimicrobial/antiprotozoal without indication in prurigo nodularis. D is wrong: topical benzoyl peroxide treats acne and would not control the type-2-driven pruritus and nodules of prurigo nodularis.

pearls: Prurigo nodularis features multiple firm, intensely pruritic, excoriated nodules on extensor surfaces in reachable areas, with central-back sparing ('butterfly sign').
It is often secondary to chronic pruritus—always evaluate for renal, hepatic, thyroid, diabetic, HIV, iron-deficiency, and malignancy/lymphoma causes, especially with red-flag systemic symptoms.
Initial treatment targets the itch-scratch cycle: potent/intralesional steroids, emollients, antipruritics (gabapentinoids), and treating the underlying cause.
Dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 blockade) is approved for moderate-to-severe refractory prurigo nodularis.

cases.title: Fragile Blisters and Peeling Skin in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother brings him in for blisters and raw patches on his trunk that started two days ago.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for a 2-day history of blisters on his lower abdomen and inguinal folds. The lesions began as small clear bumps that quickly enlarged into floppy fluid-filled blisters; several have ruptured, leaving moist red areas. He is otherwise active, eating and drinking well, with no fever at home. There is no history of trauma, burns, or new topical products. He attends daycare, where another child reportedly had 'crusty sores.' His vaccinations are up to date. Past medical history includes mild atopic dermatitis controlled with emollients. He takes no daily medications. No known drug allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sister; no sick contacts at home aside from the daycare exposure. Family history is notable for atopic dermatitis in the mother. Review of systems is negative for cough, diarrhea, irritability, or refusal to bear weight. His mother is worried the rash is spreading and asks whether he can return to daycare.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful toddler in no distress. Skin exam shows several flaccid bullae 1-2 cm in diameter on the lower abdomen and inguinal folds, filled with slightly turbid fluid. Several have ruptured, leaving shallow, glistening erosions rimmed by a thin collarette of scale; a few have a varnish-like honey crust. Surrounding skin is minimally erythematous without deep induration. No facial or perioral involvement, no mucosal lesions, and no target lesions. Nikolsky sign is negative on uninvolved skin. No regional lymphadenopathy. Remainder of the skin shows dry patches consistent with mild eczema. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports a diagnosis of bullous impetigo over other blistering conditions?
options: Widespread positive Nikolsky sign with sloughing of normal-appearing skin
Flaccid bullae that rupture to leave a collarette of scale, localized and in a well-appearing child
Symmetric target lesions with dusky centers on the palms and soles
Tense bullae on an urticarial base along the extremities
stages.explanation: Bullous impetigo presents with flaccid, thin-roofed bullae that rupture easily and leave a characteristic collarette of scale, typically localized to intertriginous areas in a well-appearing child. A widespread positive Nikolsky sign suggests staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) or a more severe exfoliative process, not localized impetigo. Symmetric target lesions with dusky centers describe erythema multiforme. Tense bullae on an urticarial base suggest bullous pemphigoid (rare in children) or a fixed bullous reaction. The combination of flaccid bullae, collarette of scale, and an otherwise healthy child is classic for bullous impetigo caused by exfoliative-toxin-producing Staphylococcus aureus.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the molecular target responsible for the superficial blister formation in this condition?
options: BP180 (collagen XVII) in the hemidesmosome
Type VII collagen anchoring fibrils
Desmoglein-1 in the superficial epidermis
Desmoglein-3 in the deep epidermis
stages.explanation: Bullous impetigo is caused by Staphylococcus aureus exfoliative toxins A and B, which are serine proteases that cleave desmoglein-1, a desmosomal adhesion protein in the granular layer of the superficial epidermis. Loss of desmoglein-1 causes intraepidermal separation high in the epidermis, producing flaccid, easily ruptured bullae. The same mechanism underlies SSSS, where toxin spreads hematogenously. BP180/collagen XVII is targeted in bullous pemphigoid. Type VII collagen is targeted in epidermolysis bullosa acquisita and is mutated in dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa. Desmoglein-3 is targeted in pemphigus vulgaris, which produces deeper, mucosal-predominant erosions, not the superficial blistering seen here.

stages.label: WOUND CULTURE
stages.content:
Gram stain of bulla fluid: Gram-positive cocci in clusters
Bacterial culture: Staphylococcus aureus, moderate growth
Methicillin susceptibility: methicillin-susceptible (MSSA)
Clindamycin: susceptible
No group A streptococci isolated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has roughly six lesions confined to the lower abdomen and groin and is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical mupirocin three times daily to affected areas
Oral cephalexin for 7 days
Intravenous vancomycin and admission for observation
Topical high-potency corticosteroid twice daily
stages.explanation: For limited, localized impetigo (including bullous impetigo) in a well child, topical antibiotics such as mupirocin or retapamulin are first-line and as effective as oral agents with fewer side effects. Oral cephalexin (or another antistaphylococcal agent) is reserved for numerous lesions, widespread disease, or failure of topical therapy; here disease is limited, so oral therapy is unnecessary. IV vancomycin and admission are overtreatment for localized impetigo in a well-appearing child without systemic illness or SSSS. Topical corticosteroids treat inflammatory dermatoses, not bacterial infection, and could worsen the infection. Good wound hygiene and covering lesions reduce spread.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: His mother asks when he can return to daycare. What is the most appropriate guidance?
options: He may return immediately because impetigo is not contagious
He must stay home until all lesions have completely healed, usually 2-3 weeks
He may return 24 hours after starting effective antibiotic therapy, with lesions covered
He should be excluded for 10 days regardless of treatment
stages.explanation: Impetigo is contagious by direct contact, but children may return to daycare or school 24 hours after starting effective antibiotic therapy, provided draining lesions are kept covered and hand hygiene is emphasized. Saying it is not contagious is incorrect and risks spread to classmates. Requiring complete healing (2-3 weeks) is excessive and not supported by guidelines. A blanket 10-day exclusion ignores the rapid reduction in transmissibility once treatment begins. Counseling should also include keeping the child's nails trimmed, avoiding sharing towels, and watching for new lesions in household contacts.

pearls: Bullous impetigo is caused by exfoliative-toxin-producing S. aureus that cleaves desmoglein-1, producing flaccid superficial bullae with a collarette of scale.
Localized impetigo is best treated with topical mupirocin; reserve oral antistaphylococcal antibiotics for widespread or numerous lesions.
A well-appearing child with localized bullae differs from SSSS, where toxin spreads systemically and Nikolsky sign is widely positive.
Children can return to daycare 24 hours after starting effective antibiotics with lesions kept covered.

cases.title: An Enlarging Bald Patch on a Boy's Scalp
cases.chiefComplaint: His parents bring him in for a scaly bald spot on the back of his head that has been growing for several weeks.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 4-week history of a gradually enlarging scaly patch of hair loss on the occipital scalp. His mother first noticed flaking that she mistook for dandruff; the area has since become more clearly bald with stubble. He reports mild itching but no pain. There has been no fever or systemic symptoms. He plays on a youth wrestling team and shares a household with a younger sibling and a pet cat. No prior similar episodes. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications. No known drug allergies. He is fully immunized. Social history: attends second grade; family has limited financial resources but reliable follow-up. Family history is noncontributory. There has been no recent travel. His mother tried an over-the-counter antifungal cream for two weeks with no improvement, and a friend suggested an anti-dandruff shampoo, which also did not help. She is frustrated and asks whether a cream or pill is needed to clear it.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing child. Scalp shows a 4 cm circular patch of alopecia over the occiput with fine adherent gray scale. Within the patch, broken hairs at the follicular openings appear as small black dots; remaining hairs extract easily and painlessly. There is no boggy swelling, no pustules, and no honey-colored crust. Tender, mobile posterior cervical and occipital lymph nodes are palpable, approximately 1 cm. No erythematous nodules or fluctuance. The rest of the scalp, eyebrows, and eyelashes are normal. Nails are normal. Skin elsewhere shows no annular plaques. No facial lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical finding best distinguishes tinea capitis from alopecia areata in this child?
options: Smooth patch of complete hair loss with no scale and exclamation-point hairs
Scaly patch with broken hairs (black dots) and occipital lymphadenopathy
Diffuse thinning with a positive hair-pull test of telogen hairs
Well-demarcated patch with overlying silvery plaques and pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed
stages.explanation: Tinea capitis classically produces a scaly patch of alopecia with broken-off hairs appearing as black dots at the follicular orifice, and regional (occipital/posterior cervical) lymphadenopathy is a strong supporting clue pointing to infection. Alopecia areata produces smooth, non-scaly patches of complete hair loss with exclamation-point hairs and no lymphadenopathy. Diffuse thinning with a positive pull test of telogen hairs describes telogen effluvium. Silvery plaques with pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign) describe psoriasis. The presence of scale, black dots, and tender occipital nodes makes a dermatophyte infection by far the most likely diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Wood's lamp examination alone, which will fluoresce green if positive
Scalp biopsy with direct immunofluorescence
KOH preparation and fungal culture of plucked hairs and scale
Empiric oral antibiotics and reassessment in 2 weeks
stages.explanation: KOH microscopy of plucked hairs and scale (looking for endothrix arthroconidia) plus fungal culture is the standard confirmatory approach and identifies the organism to guide therapy and duration. Wood's lamp is unreliable because the most common cause in the U.S., Trichophyton tonsurans, is an endothrix organism that does NOT fluoresce (only Microsporum species fluoresce green); a negative lamp does not exclude tinea capitis. Scalp biopsy is invasive and rarely needed. Empiric oral antibiotics treat bacterial infection, not a dermatophyte, and would delay appropriate antifungal therapy. KOH plus culture confirms the diagnosis while culture can also speciate the organism.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES
stages.content:
KOH prep of plucked hair: numerous arthroconidia within the hair shaft (endothrix pattern)
Wood's lamp: no fluorescence
Fungal culture: pending (preliminary growth at 1 week suggestive of Trichophyton tonsurans)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for confirmed tinea capitis?
options: Topical ketoconazole cream applied to the patch twice daily
Oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for several weeks, with an adjunctive antifungal shampoo
Selenium sulfide shampoo alone three times weekly
Oral cephalexin for 10 days
stages.explanation: Tinea capitis requires systemic antifungal therapy because the fungus invades the hair shaft and follicle, where topical agents cannot penetrate adequately. Oral griseofulvin (longer course, better for Microsporum) or terbinafine (shorter course, effective against Trichophyton) is first-line; an adjunctive sporicidal shampoo such as selenium sulfide or ketoconazole reduces shedding and transmission. Topical antifungal cream alone fails because it cannot reach the intrafollicular fungus. Selenium sulfide shampoo alone is only adjunctive, not curative. Oral cephalexin treats bacteria, not a dermatophyte. Treating household contacts' fomites and using the shampoo helps limit spread, especially given his wrestling exposure.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If left untreated or inadequately treated, which complication is this child most at risk of developing?
options: Permanent scarring alopecia from a kerion or untreated inflammatory reaction
Systemic candidemia
Squamous cell carcinoma of the scalp
Bullous pemphigoid
stages.explanation: Inadequately treated tinea capitis can progress to a kerion, a boggy inflammatory mass representing a vigorous host immune response; severe or delayed inflammation can destroy follicles and cause permanent scarring alopecia. Prompt systemic therapy reduces this risk. Systemic candidemia is unrelated to dermatophyte scalp infection. Squamous cell carcinoma is not a recognized complication of pediatric tinea capitis. Bullous pemphigoid is an autoimmune blistering disease unrelated to dermatophyte infection. Recognizing the risk of scarring underscores why oral antifungals (not topical alone) and timely treatment are essential, and why an inflammatory kerion warrants urgent attention.

pearls: Tinea capitis presents as a scaly alopecic patch with black-dot broken hairs and tender occipital/posterior cervical lymphadenopathy.
Trichophyton tonsurans (endothrix) is the most common U.S. cause and does NOT fluoresce under Wood's lamp; only Microsporum fluoresces.
Treatment requires oral antifungals (griseofulvin or terbinafine) because topicals cannot reach the intrafollicular fungus; add a sporicidal shampoo to reduce spread.
Untreated inflammatory tinea capitis can evolve into a kerion and cause permanent scarring alopecia.

cases.title: A Painful, Swollen Mass on a Boy's Scalp
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother brings him in for a tender, swollen, oozing lump on his scalp that has grown over the past 10 days.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 10-day history of a progressively enlarging, tender lump on the vertex of his scalp with overlying hair loss and yellow drainage. It began as a small scaly patch with itching, then became swollen, boggy, and painful. He has had low-grade fevers at home. A primary care visit a week ago diagnosed a 'scalp abscess,' and he was started on oral cephalexin with no improvement; the lesion continued to enlarge. There is no history of trauma or insect bite. He has a pet dog and recently visited a relative's farm with cattle. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: cephalexin, now on day 7. No known drug allergies. He is fully immunized. Social history: fourth grade; lives with parents and two siblings. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for headache, neck stiffness, visual changes, or weight loss. His mother is concerned the antibiotic is not working and worries about whether the lump needs to be drained surgically.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, mildly uncomfortable child. On the vertex there is a 5 cm boggy, erythematous, exquisitely tender mass studded with multiple pustules and draining purulent material from several follicular openings, with overlying alopecia and broken hairs. Gentle pressure expresses pus from numerous discrete follicular orifices rather than a single point. Hair within the lesion epilates easily and painlessly at the margins. Tender, enlarged occipital and posterior cervical lymph nodes (~1.5 cm) are present. No fluctuant single cavity, no overlying necrosis, no central punctum. Neurologic exam is normal; no meningismus. Remaining scalp and skin are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Despite the purulent appearance and prior 'abscess' label, which feature most argues against a simple bacterial abscess?
options: The presence of tender regional lymphadenopathy
Failure to improve on cephalexin with pus draining from multiple follicular openings, plus overlying alopecia and easily epilated hairs
Low-grade fever and mild tenderness
Location on the vertex of the scalp
stages.explanation: A kerion is an intensely inflammatory dermatophyte infection that mimics a bacterial abscess but is distinguished by pus draining from MULTIPLE follicular orifices (not a single fluctuant cavity), associated alopecia with easily epilated hairs, and—critically—failure to respond to antibacterial antibiotics like cephalexin. Tender lymphadenopathy occurs in both bacterial infection and kerion, so it does not discriminate. Low-grade fever and mild tenderness are nonspecific. Vertex location is common for both. The boggy mass with follicular pustules, hair loss, and antibiotic non-response in a child points to an inflammatory tinea (kerion) rather than a pyogenic abscess.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Incision and drainage with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
KOH preparation and fungal culture of plucked hairs and pus
CT of the head with contrast
Punch biopsy for direct immunofluorescence
stages.explanation: KOH microscopy and fungal culture of plucked hairs and exudate confirm the dermatophyte etiology of a kerion and guide antifungal selection and duration. Incision and drainage is generally avoided in kerion: it does not treat the fungal infection, can worsen scarring, and the 'pus' is largely inflammatory rather than a drainable bacterial collection. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics target bacteria, but the underlying problem is fungal. CT of the head is unnecessary without signs of deep extension, osteomyelitis, or intracranial involvement. Punch biopsy with immunofluorescence is used for autoimmune blistering or connective-tissue disease, not dermatophyte infection. Bacterial culture may be sent if superinfection is suspected, but fungal studies are the priority.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES
stages.content:
KOH prep of plucked hair: arthroconidia within the hair shaft (endothrix)
Fungal culture: Trichophyton tonsurans (final at 3 weeks)
Bacterial culture of exudate: mixed skin flora, no significant pathogen
Wood's lamp: no fluorescence
CBC: WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL, otherwise normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment regimen for this kerion?
options: Topical terbinafine cream and warm compresses only
Oral systemic antifungal (e.g., griseofulvin or terbinafine) for several weeks, often with a short course of oral corticosteroids and an adjunctive antifungal shampoo
Surgical excision of the mass
Continue cephalexin and add topical mupirocin
stages.explanation: A kerion requires systemic oral antifungal therapy (griseofulvin or terbinafine) for several weeks because the fungus invades hair follicles. A short course of oral corticosteroids is commonly added to reduce the intense inflammation and lower the risk of scarring alopecia, and an adjunctive sporicidal shampoo limits spread. Topical antifungal cream alone cannot reach intrafollicular fungus and is inadequate. Surgical excision is not indicated and risks unnecessary scarring. Continuing cephalexin and adding mupirocin targets bacteria, which are not the cause; the prior antibiotic failure already demonstrated this. Early systemic antifungal therapy improves outcomes and minimizes permanent hair loss.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Beyond treating the patient, what additional public-health measure is most appropriate?
options: Isolate the child at home for 6 weeks
Examine and screen close contacts and household members, and look for an animal source given the farm/dog exposure
Administer antifungal prophylaxis to the entire class
Report the case to the health department as a notifiable disease
stages.explanation: Dermatophytes causing tinea capitis and kerion are transmissible person-to-person and sometimes from animals (zoophilic species). The appropriate measure is to examine and screen close/household contacts for asymptomatic carriage or active disease and consider an animal reservoir given the dog and cattle exposure. Prolonged 6-week home isolation is excessive; children may return once treatment is underway and lesions are managed. Empiric antifungal prophylaxis for an entire class is not standard. Tinea capitis is not a routinely reportable notifiable disease in most jurisdictions. Identifying and treating contacts and any animal source helps prevent reinfection and ongoing transmission.

pearls: A kerion is an intensely inflammatory tinea capitis that mimics a bacterial abscess but drains pus from multiple follicular openings and fails antibacterial antibiotics.
Avoid incision and drainage; confirm with KOH and fungal culture rather than treating it as a pyogenic abscess.
Treat with systemic oral antifungals for weeks, often plus a short corticosteroid course to reduce inflammation and prevent scarring alopecia.
Screen close contacts and consider zoophilic animal sources to prevent reinfection.

cases.title: Itchy, Scaling, Cracked Feet in a Recurrent Leg-Infection Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: He comes in for itchy, peeling skin between his toes and along the soles of his feet for several months.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with several months of itching, scaling, and occasional cracking between his toes, particularly the fourth web space, along with chronic dry scaling along the soles and sides of both feet. The web spaces sometimes become white, soggy, and macerated, with a faint odor. He works long shifts in steel-toed boots and exercises at a gym, where he uses communal showers. Notably, he has been hospitalized twice in the past year for cellulitis of the lower leg, each requiring IV antibiotics. Past medical history: those two episodes of lower-extremity cellulitis; otherwise healthy. Medications: none currently. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, no IV drug use. Family history is noncontributory. He has tried an over-the-counter foot powder with partial relief. He asks whether his foot rash could be connected to the repeated leg infections that keep landing him in the hospital.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. Feet show fissuring and white, macerated, malodorous skin in the lateral toe web spaces, most prominent in the fourth interdigital space. The plantar surfaces and lateral feet have diffuse fine scaling in a 'moccasin' distribution extending to the sides of the feet but sparing the dorsa. Mild background erythema; no vesicles, no honey crust, no warmth or spreading erythema currently. Toenails show mild distal yellowing and subungual debris on two nails. No lymphangitic streaking and no calf tenderness today. The contralateral leg shows faint residual postinflammatory hyperpigmentation from prior cellulitis. Distal pulses intact; sensation normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical pattern best characterizes this patient's foot findings?
options: Vesiculobullous eruption confined to the dorsal feet
Interdigital maceration plus moccasin-distribution scaling sparing the dorsa
Well-demarcated erythema with sharp borders and systemic fever
Symmetric pitting and oil-drop changes of the nails with plantar pustules
