stages.explanation: Tinea pedis commonly presents as interdigital maceration (especially the lateral web spaces) combined with a chronic 'moccasin' pattern of fine scaling over the soles and sides of the feet that characteristically spares the dorsa. A vesiculobullous eruption confined to the dorsa is atypical; the inflammatory/bullous variant of tinea pedis favors the instep but is less common. Well-demarcated erythema with sharp borders and fever describes erysipelas/cellulitis, not tinea. Symmetric nail pitting with oil-drop changes and plantar pustules suggests psoriasis. The maceration-plus-moccasin combination, with web-space predominance, is classic for dermatophyte infection of the feet.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial confirmatory test?
options: KOH preparation of skin scrapings from the active scaling border
Punch biopsy of the sole
Patch testing for allergic contact dermatitis
Doppler ultrasound of the lower extremity
stages.explanation: A KOH preparation of skin scrapings from the leading scaly edge is the quick, inexpensive bedside test for tinea pedis, revealing branching septate hyphae. Punch biopsy is unnecessary for a classic clinical picture and is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty. Patch testing evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, which can mimic foot dermatitis but is not the most likely diagnosis given maceration, moccasin scaling, and nail involvement. Doppler ultrasound evaluates for deep vein thrombosis, relevant for acute unilateral leg swelling, not chronic bilateral foot scaling. KOH confirms the dermatophyte and guides topical versus oral therapy; fungal culture can be added if the diagnosis is unclear.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His tinea pedis is confirmed. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral terbinafine for 12 weeks immediately
Topical antifungal (e.g., terbinafine or clotrimazole) with foot-drying measures
Topical high-potency corticosteroid twice daily
Oral antibiotics to prevent cellulitis
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated tinea pedis is treated with a topical antifungal such as terbinafine or an azole, applied for the recommended course, along with keeping the feet dry, changing socks, and using moisture-wicking footwear. Oral terbinafine is reserved for extensive, refractory, or moccasin-type disease that fails topical therapy, or when onychomycosis requires systemic treatment—not as routine first-line for skin-limited disease. Topical corticosteroids alone do not treat the fungus and can worsen it. Oral antibiotics do not treat a fungal infection; addressing the tinea itself is the key to reducing future cellulitis. Drying measures and treating web-space maceration are essential to prevent recurrence.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: How does treating his tinea pedis relate to his recurrent lower-leg cellulitis?
options: There is no relationship; the two are coincidental
Interdigital tinea creates fissures that serve as a portal of entry for bacteria, so treating it reduces recurrent cellulitis
Tinea pedis directly causes cellulitis through fungal bloodstream invasion
Cellulitis causes tinea pedis, so antibiotics will cure the foot scaling
stages.explanation: Interdigital tinea pedis macerates and fissures the web-space skin, disrupting the cutaneous barrier and providing a portal of entry for streptococci and staphylococci that cause lower-extremity cellulitis. Eradicating the tinea and maintaining dry, intact web spaces is an evidence-based strategy to reduce recurrent cellulitis. The relationship is not coincidental. Tinea does not invade the bloodstream to cause cellulitis directly; rather, bacteria enter through the fungal-damaged skin. Cellulitis does not cause tinea, and antibiotics will not cure a dermatophyte infection. Recognizing the foot as the entry portal is high-yield and changes management toward antifungal therapy and barrier protection.

pearls: Tinea pedis classically combines interdigital web-space maceration with moccasin-distribution scaling that spares the dorsal foot.
KOH prep of scrapings from the active scaling edge shows septate branching hyphae and confirms the diagnosis.
Skin-limited tinea pedis is treated topically; reserve oral terbinafine for extensive, refractory, or nail-associated disease.
Interdigital tinea is a portal of entry for recurrent lower-extremity cellulitis—treating it helps prevent further infections.

cases.title: Sore, Red Glans with Increased Thirst and Urination
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents with redness, soreness, and itching of the head of his penis for about two weeks.
cases.history: A 54-year-old uncircumcised man presents with two weeks of erythema, soreness, and itching of the glans penis, with a white discharge under the foreskin and discomfort with retraction. He denies dysuria, urethral discharge, ulcers, or new sexual partners; he is in a long-term monogamous relationship. On further questioning, he reports several weeks of increased thirst, frequent urination including nocturia, fatigue, and an unintentional 5 kg weight loss. Past medical history: hypertension and obesity; he has not seen a physician in 3 years. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Family history: type 2 diabetes in both parents. Social history: drinks 2-3 beers nightly, former smoker, sedentary office job. He has tried over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream with no improvement and is embarrassed by the symptoms. He asks whether this could be a sexually transmitted infection.
cases.exam: Overweight man in no acute distress. Genital exam: uncircumcised penis with diffuse erythema and mild edema of the glans and inner prepuce. There is a curd-like white exudate beneath the foreskin and several small superficial pustules and erosions at the periphery of the erythema (satellite lesions). No deep ulcers, no vesicles, no indurated chancre, and no urethral discharge on milking. Mild tenderness; foreskin retracts with discomfort but no phimosis. Inguinal nodes are not enlarged. Skin elsewhere is unremarkable; no oral thrush noted. No intertriginous candidal patches identified on a quick survey of skin folds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exam finding most specifically points to candidal balanitis rather than other causes of balanitis?
options: A single painless indurated ulcer (chancre) on the glans
Glans erythema with a curd-like exudate and peripheral satellite pustules
Grouped painful vesicles on an erythematous base
A purulent urethral discharge with dysuria
stages.explanation: Candidal balanitis classically shows glans/prepuce erythema with a curd-like white exudate and characteristic satellite pustules or papules at the edges of the main plaque—the satellite lesions are a strong clue to Candida. A single painless indurated ulcer is the chancre of primary syphilis. Grouped painful vesicles on an erythematous base suggest herpes simplex. A purulent urethral discharge with dysuria points to urethritis (e.g., gonococcal/chlamydial), not balanitis. The combination of erythema, curd-like exudate under the foreskin, and satellite pustules in an uncircumcised man is typical of candidal balanitis, especially when a predisposing factor like diabetes is present.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and weight loss, what is the most important initial workup beyond confirming the skin diagnosis?
options: Screen for diabetes mellitus with a random/fasting glucose and HbA1c
Order HIV and syphilis serologies only
Obtain a scrotal ultrasound
Refer for circumcision before any laboratory testing
stages.explanation: The classic triad of polyuria, polydipsia, and unintentional weight loss with candidal balanitis strongly suggests undiagnosed diabetes mellitus, which predisposes to candidal infection through glucosuria and immune effects. Checking a random or fasting glucose and HbA1c is the key initial step and can be diagnostic and life-changing. HIV and syphilis testing may be reasonable based on risk but do not explain the metabolic symptoms and are not the priority here. Scrotal ultrasound evaluates testicular/scrotal pathology, not balanitis or hyperglycemia. Circumcision is a later consideration for recurrent disease and should not precede identifying and treating the underlying diabetes. Recurrent or treatment-resistant candidal balanitis should always prompt diabetes screening.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Random plasma glucose: 312 mg/dL
HbA1c: 10.4%
Urinalysis: glucose 4+, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, trace ketones
KOH prep of subpreputial exudate: budding yeasts with pseudohyphae
Basic metabolic panel: Na 134 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Topical antibiotic and reassurance
Topical antifungal (e.g., clotrimazole) to the glans plus initiation of diabetes management and education
Oral acyclovir and abstinence counseling
High-potency topical corticosteroid alone
stages.explanation: Candidal balanitis is treated with a topical antifungal such as clotrimazole (an oral azole like fluconazole is an option for severe or refractory cases), combined with hygiene measures. Critically, the newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 10.4%, glucose 312) must be addressed—lifestyle counseling and pharmacologic therapy (e.g., metformin)—because controlling hyperglycemia treats the predisposing cause and prevents recurrence. Topical antibiotics target bacteria, not yeast. Oral acyclovir treats herpes, which is not the diagnosis (KOH shows budding yeast with pseudohyphae). High-potency corticosteroid alone would not treat the infection and could worsen it. Treating both the infection and the underlying diabetes is essential.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: He asks how to prevent this from happening again. What is the best advice?
options: Use prophylactic oral antibiotics weekly
Achieve good glycemic control and practice careful genital hygiene with gentle drying under the foreskin
Avoid all dairy products
Apply hydrocortisone daily as maintenance
stages.explanation: Recurrence of candidal balanitis is best prevented by optimizing glycemic control—lowering glucose reduces glucosuria and the moist, sugar-rich environment that favors Candida—plus good genital hygiene including gentle cleansing and thorough drying under the foreskin. Prophylactic oral antibiotics do not prevent fungal infection and promote resistance. Dietary dairy avoidance has no role. Daily hydrocortisone is not maintenance therapy and can predispose to recurrent candidiasis by local immunosuppression. For men with troublesome recurrent candidal balanitis despite good control and hygiene, circumcision may be considered, but optimizing diabetes and hygiene is the foundation of prevention.

pearls: Candidal balanitis shows glans erythema with a curd-like exudate and peripheral satellite pustules in uncircumcised men.
Recurrent or treatment-resistant candidal balanitis is a classic presenting clue to new-onset diabetes mellitus—always check glucose and HbA1c.
Treat with a topical antifungal (or oral fluconazole if severe) and, crucially, manage the underlying hyperglycemia.
Prevention hinges on glycemic control and careful genital hygiene; consider circumcision only for refractory recurrent disease.

cases.title: Mouth Sores and Spots on the Hands and Feet in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Her father brings her in for mouth sores, fussiness, and a rash on her hands and feet for two days.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is brought in by her father for 2 days of irritability, low-grade fever, and refusal to eat or drink well. He noticed painful-looking sores in her mouth and small spots appearing on her hands and feet. She had a runny nose and mild fever about a day before the sores appeared. She attends daycare, where several children have reportedly had similar mouth sores and rashes recently. She is producing slightly fewer wet diapers but is still making tears. Past medical history is unremarkable; she is fully immunized for age. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: lives with both parents; attends daycare full time. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for vomiting, diarrhea, cough, difficulty breathing, neck stiffness, or rash on the trunk. Her father is worried she is not drinking enough because her mouth hurts, and he asks whether she needs antibiotics.
cases.exam: Fussy but consolable toddler, mildly uncomfortable. Oral exam reveals several shallow, yellow-gray ulcers with erythematous rims on the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate; the anterior mouth and lips are involved. Skin shows scattered gray-roofed oval vesicles and red macules with a few thin-walled vesicles on the palms, lateral fingers, soles, and a few on the buttocks. Lesions are oriented along skin lines; no honey crust, no umbilication. Mucous membranes are slightly tacky but she has tears and moist conjunctivae. No meningismus, no respiratory distress, lungs clear. No truncal rash. Capillary refill < 2 seconds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings is most characteristic of hand-foot-and-mouth disease?
options: Vesicles in different stages over the trunk with a dewdrop-on-a-rose-petal appearance
Oral ulcers plus acral vesicles on the palms, soles, and buttocks following a brief prodrome
Diffuse sandpaper-textured rash with circumoral pallor and a strawberry tongue
Koplik spots followed by a cephalocaudal morbilliform rash
stages.explanation: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease (HFMD) presents with painful oral ulcers (enanthem) together with vesicles on the palms, soles, and often the buttocks (exanthem) after a short prodrome of fever and malaise. Vesicles in different stages over the trunk ('dewdrop on a rose petal') describe varicella. A sandpaper rash with circumoral pallor and strawberry tongue describes scarlet fever. Koplik spots followed by a cephalocaudal morbilliform rash describe measles. The acral (hands/feet) distribution combined with oral ulcers, in a daycare setting, is classic for HFMD, most commonly caused by coxsackievirus A16 (and enterovirus A71).

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the most likely causative organism in this outbreak setting?
options: Herpes simplex virus type 1
Coxsackievirus A16
Group A Streptococcus
Varicella-zoster virus
stages.explanation: Coxsackievirus A16, an enterovirus, is the most common cause of classic hand-foot-and-mouth disease, especially in daycare outbreaks; enterovirus A71 can cause more severe disease with neurologic complications. Herpes simplex virus type 1 causes herpetic gingivostomatitis, which produces clustered oral vesicles/ulcers (often with prominent gingival involvement) but not the palmar/plantar exanthem. Group A Streptococcus causes pharyngitis and scarlet fever, not vesicular acral lesions with oral ulcers. Varicella-zoster virus causes chickenpox, with a generalized truncal vesicular rash in different stages rather than an acral distribution. The clinical pattern and outbreak context point to coxsackievirus A16.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this child?
options: Oral acyclovir for 7 days
Supportive care with hydration, analgesia/antipyretics, and counseling on oral intake
Oral amoxicillin for 10 days
Topical corticosteroid to the acral lesions
stages.explanation: HFMD is a self-limited viral illness; management is supportive—encouraging fluids, using acetaminophen or ibuprofen for pain and fever, offering cool, bland foods, and watching for dehydration, since painful oral ulcers can reduce intake. Oral acyclovir treats herpesvirus infections, not enterovirus, and is not indicated. Oral amoxicillin targets bacteria and has no role in this viral illness; it could cause unnecessary side effects. Topical corticosteroids are not indicated and do not speed resolution of viral acral vesicles. Parents should be counseled on hand hygiene (fecal-oral and respiratory spread) and that the rash resolves over about a week, sometimes with later nail changes (onychomadesis).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most warrant closer monitoring or return to care?
options: A few new vesicles appearing on the buttocks the next day
Signs of dehydration, lethargy, or refusal of all fluids
Mild peeling of the palms after a week
Low-grade fever persisting for one to two days
stages.explanation: The main risk in HFMD is dehydration from poor oral intake due to painful mouth ulcers; lethargy, decreased urine output, no tears, or refusal of all fluids should prompt re-evaluation, and rare neurologic signs (with enterovirus A71) also warrant urgent care. New buttock vesicles are part of the typical evolution and are expected, not alarming. Palmar peeling and even later nail shedding (onychomadesis) are recognized benign sequelae. Low-grade fever for one to two days is typical of the viral course. Educating caregivers to monitor hydration and watch for neurologic warning signs is the key disposition teaching point.

pearls: HFMD pairs painful oral ulcers with vesicles on the palms, soles, and buttocks after a short febrile prodrome.
Coxsackievirus A16 is the most common cause; enterovirus A71 can cause more severe neurologic disease.
Management is supportive—hydration and analgesia; the main complication is dehydration from painful oral intake.
Late nail shedding (onychomadesis) and palmar/plantar peeling are benign sequelae to anticipate.

cases.title: High Fever, Red Eyes, and a Spreading Rash in an Unvaccinated Child
cases.chiefComplaint: His parents bring him to the ED for high fever, cough, red eyes, and a rash that started on his face.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for 4 days of high fever, harsh cough, profuse runny nose, and markedly red, watery eyes; a rash appeared on his face today and is spreading downward. His parents report photophobia. He has received no childhood vaccinations because of personal belief exemptions. Two weeks ago the family returned from international travel, and a cousin abroad had a similar illness. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: attends a small co-op preschool; several families are unvaccinated. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for malaise, poor appetite, and the rash; negative for vomiting, diarrhea, stiff neck, and difficulty breathing. On arrival he is placed in a negative-pressure room with airborne precautions. His parents ask whether this is just a bad cold.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, febrile child with conjunctival injection and watery eyes, photophobic. Examination of the buccal mucosa opposite the second molars reveals tiny gray-white papules on an erythematous base (Koplik spots). There is a blanching erythematous maculopapular (morbilliform) rash beginning at the hairline and behind the ears, spreading to the face and upper neck, with early confluent areas on the forehead. Nasal mucosa is inflamed with copious discharge; pharynx is erythematous. Cervical lymph nodes are mildly enlarged. Lungs have scattered coarse breath sounds but no focal consolidation; no respiratory distress at rest. No meningismus. Abdomen soft.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which pathognomonic finding seen on this child's exam strongly supports measles before the rash fully evolves?
options: Strawberry tongue with circumoral pallor
Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa opposite the molars
Dewdrop vesicles on an erythematous base
Slapped-cheek facial erythema
stages.explanation: Koplik spots—tiny gray-white papules on an erythematous buccal mucosal base opposite the molars—are pathognomonic for measles and typically appear 1-2 days before the cutaneous rash, making them an early diagnostic clue. A strawberry tongue with circumoral pallor is seen in scarlet fever. Dewdrop vesicles on an erythematous base describe varicella. Slapped-cheek erythema describes erythema infectiosum (parvovirus B19). The classic measles prodrome is the three Cs—cough, coryza, conjunctivitis—plus high fever, followed by Koplik spots and then a cephalocaudal morbilliform rash, all of which fit this unvaccinated, recently traveled child.

stages.label: INFECTION CONTROL
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate infection-control measure in the ED?
options: Standard precautions only
Droplet precautions with a surgical mask
Airborne precautions in a negative-pressure room
Contact precautions with gown and gloves only
stages.explanation: Measles is one of the most contagious human pathogens and spreads via airborne respiratory droplets and aerosols that linger in the air; airborne precautions in a negative-pressure (airborne infection isolation) room with appropriately fitted respirators (N95) are required. Standard precautions alone are inadequate. Droplet precautions with a surgical mask are insufficient because measles is truly airborne, not merely droplet-spread. Contact precautions address direct/fomite transmission and miss the airborne route. Rapid isolation protects other patients and staff—especially infants, pregnant people, and immunocompromised individuals—and is a critical first action, alongside notifying infection control and public health.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Measles IgM serology: positive
Measles RT-PCR (nasopharyngeal/throat swab): positive
WBC: 3.6 ×10³/µL (leukopenia)
Lymphocytes: relatively decreased
Chest X-ray: mild peribronchial thickening, no focal consolidation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond supportive care and isolation, which therapy is recommended for this child with confirmed measles?
options: Oral acyclovir
Vitamin A supplementation
Empiric IV vancomycin
Oral oseltamivir
stages.explanation: Vitamin A is recommended for children with measles; it reduces morbidity and mortality, particularly the risk of severe complications, and is given as age-based dosing on two consecutive days (with a third dose later if there is clinical vitamin A deficiency). Otherwise care is supportive: antipyretics, fluids, and monitoring for complications. Acyclovir treats herpesviruses, not measles. Empiric IV vancomycin is not indicated unless a bacterial superinfection (e.g., pneumonia, otitis media) develops. Oseltamivir treats influenza, not measles. Public health notification, contact tracing, and post-exposure prophylaxis (MMR or immunoglobulin) for susceptible contacts are also essential components of management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the most common cause of measles-associated death in young children?
options: Pneumonia
Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE)
Acute glomerulonephritis
Myocarditis
stages.explanation: Pneumonia—either from the measles virus itself or from secondary bacterial superinfection—is the most common cause of measles-related death in children. SSPE is a rare, fatal, late degenerative neurologic complication occurring years after infection, but it is not the leading cause of acute death. Acute glomerulonephritis is associated with streptococcal infections, not measles. Myocarditis is not a typical measles complication. Other recognized complications include otitis media (the most common overall complication), acute encephalitis, and diarrhea. Counseling families that vaccination prevents these serious complications—and recognizing pneumonia as the principal lethal complication—is high-yield.

pearls: Measles prodrome is the three Cs—cough, coryza, conjunctivitis—plus high fever; Koplik spots are pathognomonic and precede the rash.
The morbilliform rash spreads cephalocaudally from the hairline/behind the ears downward.
Measles is airborne—use a negative-pressure room and N95 respirators, and notify public health.
Give vitamin A to reduce morbidity/mortality; pneumonia is the most common cause of measles death.

cases.title: An Itchy, Blistering Rash in a School-Aged Girl
cases.chiefComplaint: Her mother brings her in for a very itchy rash with blisters that started on the trunk yesterday.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl presents with a 1-day history of an intensely itchy rash that began on the trunk and is now spreading to the face and scalp. She had low-grade fever and malaise for a day before the rash appeared. New spots continue to erupt while older ones blister and crust. She is uncomfortable from itching but otherwise drinking and active. She has not received the varicella vaccine because the family fell behind on the schedule. A classmate had a similar illness about two weeks ago. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: lives with parents and a 6-month-old sibling who is too young to be vaccinated; her grandmother, who is on chemotherapy, visits frequently. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for cough, difficulty breathing, severe headache, neck stiffness, vomiting, or unsteady gait. Her mother asks how to keep the baby and grandmother from catching it.
cases.exam: Well-appearing but visibly itchy child. Skin shows lesions in multiple simultaneous stages: erythematous macules, papules, clear thin-walled vesicles surrounded by a red halo ('dewdrop on a rose petal'), and crusted lesions—coexisting on the trunk, face, and scalp. A few lesions are present on the oral mucosa. Excoriations are noted; no purulent crusting, no surrounding cellulitis, no fluctuance. Lungs are clear without distress. No meningismus, normal mental status, normal gait and coordination. No focal neurologic deficits. Mucous membranes moist; no significant lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature is most characteristic of varicella and helps distinguish it from smallpox or other vesicular eruptions?
options: All lesions in the same stage of development progressing synchronously
Lesions in multiple different stages simultaneously (macules, vesicles, and crusts together)
Vesicles confined to a single dermatome
Acral vesicles on the palms and soles with oral ulcers
stages.explanation: Varicella characteristically shows lesions in multiple stages at the same time—macules, papules, vesicles ('dewdrop on a rose petal'), and crusts coexisting—reflecting successive crops. Synchronous, same-stage progression is classically described for smallpox, an important historical distinction. Vesicles confined to a single dermatome describe herpes zoster (reactivation), not primary varicella. Acral vesicles on the palms and soles with oral ulcers describe hand-foot-and-mouth disease. The combination of intense pruritus, centripetal (trunk-predominant) spread, and lesions in multiple stages is the hallmark of chickenpox caused by primary varicella-zoster virus infection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this otherwise healthy 6-year-old, what is the most appropriate management of her varicella?
options: Supportive care with antipruritics/antipyretics and counseling to avoid scratching
Routine oral acyclovir for all healthy children with chickenpox
Oral amoxicillin to prevent superinfection
Aspirin for fever control
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated varicella in a healthy young child is managed supportively: antipyretics (acetaminophen), antihistamines or soothing baths for itch, trimmed nails to prevent scratching, and watching for bacterial superinfection. Routine antiviral therapy is not recommended for otherwise healthy young children; acyclovir is reserved for those at higher risk of complications (e.g., adolescents/adults, those on chronic salicylates or steroids, immunocompromised hosts). Prophylactic amoxicillin is not indicated and would not prevent superinfection. Aspirin must be avoided in children with varicella because of the risk of Reye syndrome. Counseling about contagion and protecting vulnerable contacts is also important.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Her 6-month-old sibling and immunocompromised grandmother were exposed. What is the most appropriate post-exposure step for these high-risk contacts?
options: Give them the varicella vaccine immediately regardless of age or immune status
Provide varicella-zoster immune globulin (VariZIG) to the susceptible high-risk contacts
Start prophylactic oral antibiotics
No intervention is needed because varicella is mild
stages.explanation: Susceptible high-risk contacts—including infants under 12 months and immunocompromised persons such as the grandmother on chemotherapy—should receive varicella-zoster immune globulin (VariZIG) for post-exposure prophylaxis to attenuate or prevent disease. The live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in immunocompromised individuals and is not given to infants under 12 months, so it is not the right choice for these particular contacts. Prophylactic antibiotics do not prevent a viral infection. Doing nothing is unsafe because varicella can be severe or life-threatening in neonates and immunocompromised hosts. Isolating the index child and limiting contact until all lesions crust over also reduces transmission.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which is the most common complication of varicella in otherwise healthy children?
options: Cerebellar ataxia
Secondary bacterial skin infection from scratching (e.g., S. aureus or group A strep)
Varicella pneumonia
Reye syndrome
stages.explanation: Secondary bacterial infection of skin lesions—often Staphylococcus aureus or group A Streptococcus introduced by scratching—is the most common complication of varicella in healthy children, ranging from impetiginization to cellulitis and, rarely, invasive disease. Acute cerebellar ataxia is a recognized but less common neurologic complication. Varicella pneumonia is more typical of adults and immunocompromised hosts and is less common in healthy children. Reye syndrome is associated with aspirin use during varicella and is preventable by avoiding salicylates. Keeping nails short, good hygiene, and antipruritics help reduce the risk of bacterial superinfection.

pearls: Varicella shows lesions in multiple stages at once ('dewdrop on a rose petal'); smallpox lesions are synchronous and same-stage.
Healthy young children get supportive care—avoid aspirin (Reye syndrome); reserve acyclovir for higher-risk patients.
Give VariZIG to susceptible high-risk contacts (neonates, immunocompromised); live vaccine is contraindicated in these groups.
Secondary bacterial skin infection from scratching is the most common complication in healthy children.

cases.title: A Sore Throat Followed by a Sandpaper Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother brings him in for a sore throat, fever, and a fine red rash that appeared two days later.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy presents with 3 days of sore throat, fever, and headache, and a 1-day history of a diffuse red rash that started on the neck and chest. He also reports tender swelling under his jaw and decreased appetite due to throat pain. He denies cough or runny nose. Several classmates have had strep throat recently. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: acetaminophen as needed for fever. No known drug allergies. Social history: third grade; lives with parents and a younger sister. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for fever, sore throat, and rash; negative for conjunctivitis, cough, coryza, joint pain, abdominal pain, and dark urine. His mother says the rash feels rough 'like sandpaper' and is most noticeable in the skin folds. She asks whether he needs antibiotics and when he can return to school.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly ill-appearing child. Oropharynx is erythematous with tonsillar exudates and palatal petechiae; the tongue shows a white coating with hyperemic papillae evolving toward a red 'strawberry' tongue. There are tender, enlarged anterior cervical lymph nodes. Skin shows a diffuse, blanching, finely papular erythema with a coarse sandpaper texture, accentuated in the antecubital and axillary folds (linear petechiae forming Pastia lines). The perioral area is spared, producing circumoral pallor. No conjunctival injection, no cough, no rhinorrhea. No edema of the hands/feet, no mucosal ulcers, no desquamation yet. Lungs clear; abdomen soft.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which cluster of findings best supports a diagnosis of scarlet fever?
options: Bilateral conjunctivitis, cracked lips, and swollen hands with prolonged high fever
Sandpaper rash with circumoral pallor, strawberry tongue, and Pastia lines after streptococcal pharyngitis
Slapped-cheek facial erythema with a lacy reticular rash on the arms
Morbilliform rash spreading from the hairline with Koplik spots
stages.explanation: Scarlet fever follows group A streptococcal pharyngitis and features a diffuse, finely papular 'sandpaper' rash, circumoral pallor, a strawberry tongue, and accentuation in skin folds with Pastia lines (linear petechiae). Bilateral conjunctivitis, cracked lips, and swollen hands with prolonged fever suggest Kawasaki disease, an important mimic. Slapped-cheek erythema with a lacy reticular rash describes erythema infectiosum (parvovirus B19). A morbilliform rash from the hairline with Koplik spots describes measles. The combination of an exudative pharyngitis, sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, and circumoral pallor is classic for scarlet fever caused by erythrogenic-toxin-producing GAS.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to confirm the underlying infection?
options: Rapid antigen detection test for group A Streptococcus, with throat culture backup if negative
Heterophile (monospot) antibody test
Blood culture
Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer for acute diagnosis
stages.explanation: A rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for group A Streptococcus, with a confirmatory throat culture if the RADT is negative in a child (because of lower sensitivity), is the standard way to confirm GAS pharyngitis underlying scarlet fever. A heterophile/monospot test screens for infectious mononucleosis, a different cause of exudative pharyngitis but not the sandpaper rash. Blood cultures are not needed for uncomplicated GAS pharyngitis/scarlet fever in a stable child. ASO titers rise later and reflect recent (not acute) infection, so they are useful for diagnosing post-streptococcal sequelae like rheumatic fever, not for acute diagnosis. Confirming GAS guides appropriate antibiotic therapy.

stages.label: RAPID TEST
stages.content:
Rapid antigen detection test (GAS): positive
Throat culture: confirmatory culture not required given positive RADT
CBC: WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL with neutrophil predominance
No eosinophilia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no penicillin allergy. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Azithromycin for 5 days
Oral penicillin V (or amoxicillin) for 10 days
Topical mupirocin to the rash
Supportive care alone, since the illness is self-limited
stages.explanation: Oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for a full 10-day course is the first-line treatment for GAS pharyngitis/scarlet fever in a child without penicillin allergy; completing the course is important to prevent acute rheumatic fever and reduce transmission. Azithromycin is reserved for true penicillin allergy because of higher macrolide resistance among GAS. Topical mupirocin treats localized skin infections like impetigo, not pharyngitis. Supportive care alone is inadequate: although scarlet fever often resolves, untreated GAS carries a risk of suppurative complications and rheumatic fever, so antibiotics are indicated. Antibiotics also allow return to school 24 hours after starting effective therapy if afebrile.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which serious complication is prevented by timely antibiotic treatment of this infection?
options: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Acute rheumatic fever
Reye syndrome
Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis
stages.explanation: Timely antibiotic treatment of GAS pharyngitis prevents acute rheumatic fever, a nonsuppurative immune-mediated complication that can damage heart valves. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is also a sequela of GAS infection, but—importantly—antibiotic treatment of pharyngitis does NOT reliably prevent it, making rheumatic fever the classic answer for what treatment prevents. Reye syndrome is linked to aspirin use during viral illnesses (e.g., varicella, influenza), not to GAS treatment. Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis is a late complication of measles, unrelated to streptococcal infection. Eradicating GAS within about 9 days of symptom onset is what confers the protective benefit against rheumatic fever.

pearls: Scarlet fever = GAS pharyngitis plus a sandpaper rash, circumoral pallor, strawberry tongue, and Pastia lines in the skin folds.
Confirm with a rapid strep test, backed by throat culture if negative in children.
Treat with a 10-day course of penicillin V or amoxicillin (azithromycin only for true penicillin allergy).
Antibiotics prevent acute rheumatic fever but do NOT reliably prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

cases.title: Bright Red Cheeks and a Lacy Rash on the Arms
cases.chiefComplaint: Her mother brings her in for very red cheeks and a lacy rash on the arms that appeared over the past two days.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl presents with 2 days of striking redness of both cheeks, followed by a pink, lacy, net-like rash on the upper arms and thighs. About a week ago she had a mild cold with low-grade fever, runny nose, and headache that resolved; she now feels well and is playful. Her mother notes the arm rash seems to come and go and looks more prominent after a warm bath and outdoor play. Several children at school have had similar 'red cheeks.' Past medical history is unremarkable; she is fully immunized. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: kindergarten; lives with parents. Her mother is pregnant (about 18 weeks). Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for high fever, joint pain or swelling, pallor, fatigue out of proportion, mouth sores, or cough today. Her mother asks whether the rash is contagious and whether she should be worried for her pregnancy.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful child in no distress. Face shows confluent, warm erythema over both cheeks with sparing of the perioral region and nasal bridge, giving a 'slapped-cheek' appearance. The extensor surfaces of the upper arms and thighs show a pink, reticular (lacy, net-like) macular rash that blanches and is more pronounced over warm skin. No vesicles, no oral lesions, no conjunctival injection. No joint swelling, warmth, or tenderness; full painless range of motion. No pallor of conjunctivae or palms. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Lungs clear; remainder of exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which description best fits the rash of erythema infectiosum?
options: Sandpaper rash with circumoral pallor and strawberry tongue
Slapped-cheek facial erythema followed by a lacy, reticular rash on the extremities
Vesicles in multiple stages on the trunk and scalp
Morbilliform rash spreading downward from the hairline with Koplik spots
stages.explanation: Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease) classically begins with bright 'slapped-cheek' facial erythema and is followed by a pink, lacy/reticular rash on the extremities that often waxes and wanes with warmth or sun. A sandpaper rash with circumoral pallor and strawberry tongue describes scarlet fever. Vesicles in multiple stages on the trunk and scalp describe varicella. A morbilliform rash spreading downward from the hairline with Koplik spots describes measles. The well-appearing child with a preceding mild viral prodrome, slapped cheeks, and a reticular extremity rash is the classic picture of parvovirus B19 infection.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the causative agent of this illness?
options: Parvovirus B19
Human herpesvirus 6
Measles virus
Coxsackievirus A16
stages.explanation: Erythema infectiosum is caused by parvovirus B19, which infects erythroid precursor cells and is transmitted via respiratory droplets. Human herpesvirus 6 causes roseola infantum (high fever followed by a rose-pink rash as fever breaks), a different exanthem. Measles virus causes measles, with the three Cs and Koplik spots, not slapped cheeks. Coxsackievirus A16 causes hand-foot-and-mouth disease, with oral ulcers and acral vesicles. Recognizing parvovirus B19 is important because it has special implications: it can cause aplastic crisis in patients with hemolytic anemia and can affect the fetus during pregnancy.

stages.label: CONTAGION
stages.question: Her mother asks if the child is still contagious now that the rash has appeared. What is the best response?
options: She is most contagious now that the rash is visible
She is generally no longer contagious once the rash appears, because the rash is post-viremic and immune-mediated
She remains contagious until the lacy rash completely fades, often weeks
Contagiousness cannot be predicted and lifelong isolation is required
stages.explanation: In erythema infectiosum, the patient is most contagious during the prodromal (viremic) phase before the rash appears; once the characteristic slapped-cheek and lacy rash develop, the illness is immune-mediated and the child is generally no longer infectious. Therefore she does not need to be excluded from school once the rash is present. Saying she is most contagious now is incorrect. The waxing-and-waning reticular rash can persist or recur for weeks with heat or sun but does not indicate ongoing contagiousness. Lifelong isolation is never appropriate. This timing explains why outbreaks are hard to control—spread occurs before the diagnostic rash emerges.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given that the child's mother is 18 weeks pregnant and was exposed, what is the most important fetal risk and next step?
options: Congenital cataracts; reassure and take no action
Hydrops fetalis from fetal anemia; the mother should contact her obstetrician for evaluation and possible serologic testing/monitoring
Limb reduction defects; recommend immediate termination
Neonatal varicella; administer VariZIG to the mother
stages.explanation: Parvovirus B19 infection in pregnancy can cause fetal anemia (the virus targets erythroid precursors), which may lead to nonimmune hydrops fetalis and, in some cases, fetal loss, with greatest risk earlier in pregnancy. The mother should notify her obstetrician for serologic testing (IgM/IgG) to determine susceptibility and, if recent infection is confirmed, monitoring such as serial ultrasounds and middle cerebral artery Doppler to detect fetal anemia, with intrauterine transfusion available if needed. Congenital cataracts are associated with congenital rubella, not parvovirus. Limb reduction and termination are not appropriate—most exposed pregnancies have good outcomes. VariZIG is for varicella exposure, not parvovirus.

pearls: Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease, parvovirus B19) starts with slapped-cheek erythema, then a lacy reticular rash that waxes/wanes with heat.
Patients are most contagious before the rash; once the rash appears it is immune-mediated and generally non-infectious—no school exclusion needed.
Parvovirus B19 can cause aplastic crisis in patients with hemolytic anemias by infecting erythroid precursors.
In pregnancy, parvovirus B19 risks fetal anemia and hydrops fetalis—exposed pregnant patients need obstetric serologic testing and monitoring.

cases.title: Worsening, painful blistering rash in a toddler with eczema
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly spreading painful blistered rash with fever over the past two days.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother for a worsening rash and fever. He has a history of moderate atopic dermatitis since infancy, managed with daily emollients and intermittent topical hydrocortisone 1%. Three days ago his usual eczema on the cheeks and antecubital fossae began to flare; over the next 48 hours it transformed into clusters of small painful blisters that crusted and spread to the neck and forehead. His mother notes he has become irritable, is feeding poorly, and felt warm. He had a cold sore contact at daycare last week. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. Medications: emollients, topical hydrocortisone, and a single dose of acetaminophen this morning. No drug allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; the sibling recently had a 'fever blister' on the lip. Family history is notable for atopy in the mother (asthma, hay fever). He has had no prior similar episodes and no recent new foods or detergents.
cases.exam: The child is fussy but consolable. On the cheeks, forehead, and neck there are numerous discrete, monomorphic, punched-out erosions 2-4 mm in diameter, many with central umbilication and hemorrhagic crusting, superimposed on lichenified erythematous atopic skin. Several intact vesicles are present at the advancing edge. The lesions are tender and some coalesce. There is no honey-colored crusting typical of impetigo and no target lesions. Mild periorbital edema is noted but the eyes themselves are not involved on penlight exam, and there is no photophobia. Cervical lymph nodes are mildly enlarged and tender. The remainder of the skin shows chronic eczematous changes. No mucosal ulceration; lungs clear; abdomen soft.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of the rash most strongly points toward the correct diagnosis rather than an impetiginized eczema flare?
options: Honey-colored crusting over the eczematous plaques
Monomorphic punched-out erosions with central umbilication and clustered vesicles
Annular plaques with central clearing and a scaly border
Wheals that migrate and resolve within 24 hours
stages.explanation: Monomorphic, punched-out erosions with umbilication arising from clustered vesicles on atopic skin are the hallmark of eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV in atopic dermatitis), making option C correct. Honey-colored crusting (option B) describes impetigo, a bacterial superinfection that can coexist but is not specific for HSV. Annular scaly plaques with central clearing (option D) suggest tinea. Migratory wheals resolving within a day (option E) describe urticaria. The recent cold-sore contact, fever, and clustered vesicles evolving into uniform erosions in a child with eczema strongly favor HSV dissemination, which is a dermatologic emergency requiring prompt antiviral therapy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to rapidly confirm the suspected etiology?
options: HSV PCR (or DFA) from a deroofed vesicle base
Skin biopsy sent for routine H&E only
KOH preparation of a skin scraping
Patch testing for contact allergens
stages.explanation: HSV PCR from a fresh deroofed vesicle is the most sensitive and rapid confirmatory test for eczema herpeticum, so option A is correct; direct fluorescent antibody and viral culture are alternatives but PCR has the highest yield. Routine H&E biopsy (option B) can show viral cytopathic changes but is slow and unnecessary when PCR is available, and would delay care. KOH prep (option C) detects dermatophytes/yeast, not HSV. Patch testing (option D) evaluates allergic contact dermatitis and is irrelevant in an acute infectious emergency. Treatment should not wait for confirmation; empiric antivirals are started on clinical suspicion.

stages.label: LABS & TESTS
stages.content:
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hgb 11.8 g/dL
Platelets 320 ×10³/µL
Na 137 mEq/L
CRP 62 mg/L
HSV-1 PCR from vesicle base: POSITIVE
Bacterial wound culture: pending
Blood culture: pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the extent of disease, fever, and poor oral intake, what is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Outpatient oral acyclovir with next-day follow-up
Topical acyclovir cream to affected areas only
High-potency topical corticosteroid to calm the eczema flare
Hospital admission with intravenous acyclovir
stages.explanation: This child has widespread eczema herpeticum with systemic signs (high fever, tachycardia, poor intake) and risk of dissemination, so admission for IV acyclovir is correct (option D). Oral acyclovir (option A) is reasonable only for mild, localized disease in a well-appearing, hydrated child who can tolerate oral intake—not this presentation. Topical acyclovir (option B) is ineffective for disseminated cutaneous HSV. Escalating topical corticosteroids (option C) is contraindicated during active HSV because immunosuppression of the skin can worsen viral spread; steroids are cautiously resumed only after the infection is controlled. Empiric coverage for bacterial superinfection is also added if present.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication should prompt urgent ophthalmology consultation in this patient?
options: Periorbital eczematous scaling
Vesicles or erosions involving the eyelid margin with eye pain or photophobia
Mild reactive cervical lymphadenopathy
Transient fever above 39 °C
stages.explanation: HSV near the eye can cause herpetic keratitis, which threatens vision; vesicles/erosions at the lid margin with eye pain or photophobia (option B) mandate urgent ophthalmology evaluation and possibly topical/systemic antivirals tailored to ocular involvement. Periorbital eczematous scaling (option A) is chronic atopic change, not an emergency. Reactive lymphadenopathy (option C) is expected with viral infection and is not sight-threatening. Fever (option D), while important systemically, does not by itself indicate ocular disease. Because this child has periorbital edema, careful serial eye exams are warranted, and any corneal symptoms should trigger immediate specialist involvement to prevent corneal scarring.

cases.diagnosis: Eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV-1 in atopic dermatitis)
pearls: Eczema herpeticum presents as monomorphic, punched-out, umbilicated erosions from clustered vesicles superimposed on atopic skin—an emergency, not a routine eczema flare.
Confirm with HSV PCR/DFA from a deroofed vesicle, but start antivirals empirically on clinical suspicion.
Widespread disease or systemic toxicity warrants admission and IV acyclovir; avoid escalating topical steroids during active infection.
Periocular involvement risks herpetic keratitis—obtain urgent ophthalmology evaluation for any ocular pain, photophobia, or lid-margin lesions.

cases.title: Fever, sore wrists, and scattered pustules in a young woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of migratory joint pains, painful wrists, and a few scattered skin bumps.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with four days of migratory joint pain and fever. The pain started in the right knee, moved to the left wrist, and now involves both wrists and the right ankle. She reports difficulty moving her wrists due to pain along the back of the hand. She also noticed a few small painful bumps on her fingers and the back of one hand. She has had intermittent low-grade fevers and chills. She is sexually active with a new male partner over the past two months and uses condoms inconsistently. Her last menstrual period was three weeks ago; she reports mild vaginal discharge but no dysuria. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill. No drug allergies. She drinks socially, does not smoke, and denies IV drug use. No recent travel or tick exposure. Family history is noncontributory. She denies oral ulcers, photosensitivity, or prior similar episodes, and has had no recent new medications.
cases.exam: She appears uncomfortable. Skin shows fewer than ten lesions scattered on the distal extremities: small erythematous macules, a few evolving into pustules with a hemorrhagic or gray necrotic center on the dorsal hands and fingers. There is tenderness and swelling along the extensor tendon sheaths of both wrists, with pain on resisted finger extension consistent with tenosynovitis. The right ankle is mildly swollen and tender. There is no large warm effusion of a single joint. Pelvic exam reveals scant mucopurulent cervical discharge without cervical motion tenderness severe enough to suggest frank PID. No oral ulcers, no murmurs, no nuchal rigidity, and no photosensitive facial rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which triad best characterizes the classic presentation suggested by this patient's findings?
options: Urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis
Dermatitis, tenosynovitis, and migratory polyarthralgia
Malar rash, oral ulcers, and serositis
Erythema migrans, facial palsy, and heart block
stages.explanation: The combination of scattered pustular/necrotic skin lesions, inflammation of the tendon sheaths (tenosynovitis), and migratory polyarthralgia is the classic arthritis-dermatitis syndrome of disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI), making option B correct. Reactive arthritis (option A) features urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis but typically lacks pustular acral lesions and tenosynovitis after gonococcal dissemination. Malar rash, oral ulcers, and serositis (option C) describe systemic lupus erythematosus. Erythema migrans with facial palsy and heart block (option D) describes disseminated Lyme disease. In a sexually active young adult with these features, DGI must be strongly considered and worked up promptly.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate set of diagnostic tests to confirm the etiology?
options: ANA, anti-dsDNA, and complement levels
Blood cultures plus nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) of cervical/urogenital, pharyngeal, and rectal samples
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
Serum uric acid and joint aspiration for crystals
stages.explanation: DGI is confirmed by isolating Neisseria gonorrhoeae; blood cultures plus NAAT of mucosal sites (urogenital, pharyngeal, rectal) maximize yield, so option B is correct. Skin lesions and synovial fluid can also be cultured/tested, but mucosal NAAT is the highest-yield. ANA/anti-dsDNA/complement (option A) evaluate lupus, which is less likely without malar rash or oral ulcers. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP (option C) assess rheumatoid arthritis, which is chronic and symmetric rather than acutely migratory with pustules. Uric acid and crystal analysis (option D) target gout/pseudogout, unlikely in a young woman with multisite migratory symptoms and pustular lesions. Co-testing for chlamydia and HIV is also indicated.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.1 ×10³/µL
ESR 48 mm/hr
CRP 70 mg/L
Cervical NAAT: POSITIVE for Neisseria gonorrhoeae
Chlamydia NAAT: negative
HIV 4th-gen Ag/Ab: negative
Blood cultures: 1 of 2 sets positive for gram-negative diplococci
Right wrist ultrasound: extensor tenosynovitis, no large effusion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial antimicrobial therapy?
options: Oral azithromycin single dose only
Oral doxycycline for 14 days only
Intravenous/IM ceftriaxone, with hospitalization and treatment for possible co-infection
Oral ciprofloxacin for 7 days
stages.explanation: DGI requires parenteral ceftriaxone (typically 1 g IV/IM daily) with hospitalization for initial management, making option C correct; therapy is continued and can be transitioned to oral agents based on susceptibility and clinical improvement. Single-dose azithromycin alone (option A) is inadequate for disseminated disease and is no longer recommended as monotherapy. Doxycycline alone (option B) treats chlamydia, not gonococcal dissemination, though doxycycline is added if chlamydia co-infection is documented. Ciprofloxacin (option D) is avoided because of widespread fluoroquinolone resistance in N. gonorrhoeae. Partner notification/treatment and counseling on other STIs are essential components of management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond antibiotics and partner treatment, which additional step is most important for this patient?
options: Screening for terminal complement (C5-C9) deficiency given recurrent or disseminated Neisseria infection risk
Immediate splenectomy
Starting lifelong suppressive valacyclovir
High-dose systemic corticosteroids to reduce arthralgia
stages.explanation: Recurrent or unusually disseminated Neisseria infections should prompt evaluation for terminal complement (membrane attack complex, C5-C9) deficiency, so option A is correct; this is screened with a total hemolytic complement (CH50) assay. Splenectomy (option B) is not indicated and would increase risk of encapsulated-organism infection. Suppressive valacyclovir (option C) treats HSV, not gonococcus. Systemic corticosteroids (option D) are not standard therapy for DGI arthralgia and could worsen an active infection. Although a single episode does not always mandate complement testing, dissemination is a recognized trigger to consider it, alongside completing the STI workup and ensuring follow-up.

pearls: The DGI arthritis-dermatitis syndrome = tenosynovitis + migratory polyarthralgia + scattered pustular/necrotic acral skin lesions.
Maximize yield with blood cultures plus NAAT of urogenital, pharyngeal, and rectal sites; also test synovial fluid and skin lesions.
Treat with parenteral ceftriaxone and hospitalization; add doxycycline if chlamydia co-infection is present and notify partners.
Disseminated or recurrent Neisseria infection should prompt screening for terminal complement (C5-C9) deficiency with CH50.

cases.title: Non-itchy rash on the palms and soles in a young man
cases.chiefComplaint: A non-itchy coppery rash on the trunk, palms, and soles for about two weeks.
cases.history: A 29-year-old man presents to the sexual health clinic with a diffuse, non-pruritic rash that began on his trunk about two weeks ago and spread to involve his palms and soles. He also reports low-grade fevers, sore throat, malaise, and mild generalized aches over the past few weeks, plus some patchy hair loss. He recalls a painless sore on his penis roughly two months ago that healed on its own without treatment. He has sex with men and women and reports inconsistent condom use with several partners over the past year. He has noticed moist, flat growths in the perianal region. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: none. No drug allergies. He drinks socially and does not use injection drugs. He has never been tested for HIV. Family history is noncontributory. He denies new soaps or detergents, recent medications, and prior similar rashes, and notes the rash does not itch despite its extent.
cases.exam: Generalized exam reveals a symmetric, coppery-to-pink papulosquamous eruption with scattered scaly papules over the trunk and extremities, notably including the palms and soles, where discrete copper-colored macules and papules with a fine collarette of scale are seen. There are moist, flat-topped, gray-white verrucous plaques in the perianal area consistent with condylomata lata. Patchy, non-scarring 'moth-eaten' alopecia is present on the scalp. Mucous patches are noted on the oral mucosa. Generalized nontender lymphadenopathy is palpable, including epitrochlear nodes. There is no genital ulcer currently. No conjunctivitis, no target lesions, and no vesicles. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single physical finding most specifically narrows the differential toward the correct diagnosis?
options: Generalized nontender lymphadenopathy
A papulosquamous rash involving the palms and soles with moist perianal condylomata lata
Low-grade fever and sore throat
Patchy non-scarring alopecia
stages.explanation: A papulosquamous eruption that involves the palms and soles together with condylomata lata is highly specific for secondary syphilis, so option B is correct. Generalized lymphadenopathy (option A), fever/sore throat (option C), and 'moth-eaten' alopecia (option D) are all supportive features of secondary syphilis but are nonspecific and seen in many systemic illnesses. The palm/sole distribution narrows the differential to a short list (syphilis, certain viral exanthems, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, hand-foot-mouth), and the addition of painless, moist, broad perianal plaques (condylomata lata, not the cauliflower-like condylomata acuminata of HPV) points strongly to secondary syphilis, especially with a preceding painless genital ulcer.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate testing strategy to confirm the diagnosis?
options: A nontreponemal test (RPR/VDRL) confirmed by a treponemal test (FTA-ABS/TP-PA)
KOH preparation of scale from the palms
Skin patch testing
Tzanck smear from a perianal plaque
stages.explanation: Syphilis serology uses a nontreponemal screen (RPR or VDRL) confirmed by a treponemal test (FTA-ABS or TP-PA), or the reverse-sequence algorithm, so option A is correct; nontreponemal titers also track treatment response. KOH prep (option B) detects fungi, not Treponema pallidum. Patch testing (option C) evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, irrelevant here. A Tzanck smear (option D) looks for multinucleated giant cells of herpesvirus infection, not syphilis. Dark-field microscopy of a moist lesion can directly visualize spirochetes but is rarely available; serology is the practical confirmatory approach. HIV and other STI testing should be performed concurrently given shared risk factors.

stages.label: LABS & TESTS
stages.content:
RPR: reactive, titer 1:128
TP-PA: reactive (confirmatory)
HIV 4th-gen Ag/Ab: negative (repeat recommended in window period)
Gonorrhea/Chlamydia NAAT: negative
Hepatitis B/C serologies: negative
CBC and basic metabolic panel: within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for this stage of disease in a non-pregnant adult without penicillin allergy?
options: A single intramuscular dose of benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units
Oral azithromycin 1 g once
Three weekly intramuscular doses of benzathine penicillin G
Topical permethrin to affected areas
stages.explanation: Early syphilis (primary, secondary, or early latent) is treated with a single IM dose of benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units, making option A correct. Azithromycin (option B) is not recommended due to resistance and treatment failures. Three weekly doses (option C) are reserved for late latent syphilis, latent of unknown duration, or tertiary disease—not secondary syphilis. Topical permethrin (option D) treats scabies and has no role here. Penicillin-allergic non-pregnant patients may receive doxycycline as an alternative, but penicillin is preferred and pregnant patients must be desensitized. Counseling on partner notification and follow-up titers is essential.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Within hours of the first antibiotic dose, the patient develops fever, chills, headache, and myalgias. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Anaphylaxis to penicillin
Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
Treatment failure requiring broader antibiotics
New superimposed bacterial sepsis
stages.explanation: A self-limited febrile reaction with chills, headache, and myalgias occurring within hours of effective treatment is the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction, caused by release of endotoxin-like substances as spirochetes are killed, so option B is correct. It is managed supportively with antipyretics and reassurance and resolves within 24 hours. Anaphylaxis (option A) presents with urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, and hypotension—not isolated fever/myalgia—and would occur immediately. Treatment failure (option C) does not present acutely within hours of the first dose. New bacterial sepsis (option D) is unlikely given the classic timing and self-limited course. Patients should be warned about this reaction before treatment.

pearls: Secondary syphilis classically causes a non-pruritic, coppery papulosquamous rash that includes the palms and soles, often with condylomata lata and 'moth-eaten' alopecia.
Diagnose with combined nontreponemal (RPR/VDRL) and treponemal (TP-PA/FTA-ABS) testing; follow titers to assess cure.
First-line treatment for early syphilis is a single IM dose of benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units; always test for HIV and other STIs.
Warn patients about the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction—a self-limited febrile reaction within hours of treatment.

cases.title: Fever, headache, and a spreading spotted rash after a camping trip
cases.chiefComplaint: Five days of high fever, severe headache, and a rash that started on the wrists and ankles.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with five days of high fevers, severe frontal headache, myalgias, and nausea, now accompanied by a rash. The rash first appeared two days ago as small pink-red spots on his wrists and ankles and has since spread centrally to involve his forearms, trunk, palms, and soles; some spots have become darker and do not blanch. He returned a week ago from a camping trip in rural North Carolina, where he hiked through tall grass and removed at least one attached tick from his leg. He has had decreasing appetite and felt confused this morning per his wife. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: none. No drug allergies. He works as a landscaper, drinks socially, and does not use drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He denies sick contacts, recent new medications, and prior similar illness, and reports the headache is the worst of his life with associated photophobia but no neck stiffness reported initially.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing and mildly confused. Skin shows numerous 2-5 mm pink-red macules and petechiae distributed over the wrists, ankles, forearms, and now the trunk, with involvement of the palms and soles; several lesions are nonblanching and a few are early petechiae. There is mild periorbital and dorsal hand edema. Conjunctivae are injected. There is no eschar identified. Mild nuchal discomfort is present but Kernig and Brudzinski signs are negative. Mucous membranes are dry. Abdomen is mildly tender without peritoneal signs; hepatosplenomegaly is not appreciated. Neurologic exam shows mild inattention without focal deficits. No tick is currently attached.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features most strongly suggests the correct diagnosis?
options: Annular expanding erythema at the bite site with facial palsy
Fever and headache with a petechial rash beginning at the wrists/ankles and spreading to palms and soles after a tick bite
Migratory pustular lesions with tenosynovitis
Targetoid plaques with mucosal erosions after a new drug
stages.explanation: A febrile, headache-prominent illness with a centripetally spreading petechial rash that begins on the wrists and ankles and involves the palms and soles after tick exposure in an endemic area is the classic picture of Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF), making option B correct. Expanding annular erythema with facial palsy (option A) describes Lyme disease, whose rash spreads outward from the bite, not from the extremities inward. Migratory pustules with tenosynovitis (option C) describe disseminated gonococcal infection. Targetoid plaques with mucosal erosions after a drug (option D) suggest erythema multiforme/Stevens-Johnson syndrome. RMSF is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate empiric treatment because mortality rises sharply with delay.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management while awaiting confirmatory testing?
options: Withhold antibiotics until serology confirms the diagnosis
Start empiric doxycycline immediately
Start empiric IV ceftriaxone and ampicillin for meningitis only
Begin oral acyclovir
stages.explanation: RMSF must be treated empirically with doxycycline as soon as it is suspected, because delaying therapy beyond the fifth day of illness markedly increases mortality, so option B is correct; doxycycline is first-line even in children. Waiting for serology (option A) is dangerous because antibody titers are often negative early in the disease. Ceftriaxone/ampicillin for meningitis (option C) does not cover Rickettsia rickettsii and would not treat RMSF, though meningococcemia should be considered and would not preclude starting doxycycline. Acyclovir (option D) treats herpesviruses and is irrelevant. Diagnosis is confirmed retrospectively by acute and convalescent serology or skin biopsy with immunohistochemistry, but treatment never waits.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL (normal count, left shift)
Platelets 88 ×10³/µL (thrombocytopenia)
Na 129 mEq/L (hyponatremia)
AST 96 U/L / ALT 88 U/L (transaminitis)
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL
RMSF IgM/IgG (acute): negative — convalescent titer pending
CSF (LP): mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern is most characteristic of this disease and supports continuing therapy despite the negative acute serology?
options: Marked leukocytosis with eosinophilia
Thrombocytopenia, hyponatremia, and transaminitis with a normal or low WBC
Hypercalcemia and elevated alkaline phosphatase
Isolated macrocytic anemia
stages.explanation: The classic laboratory triad of RMSF is thrombocytopenia, hyponatremia, and elevated transaminases, often with a normal or only mildly elevated WBC, so option B is correct; these reflect the systemic small-vessel vasculitis caused by Rickettsia rickettsii. Marked leukocytosis with eosinophilia (option A) is not typical and would suggest other processes. Hypercalcemia with high alkaline phosphatase (option C) suggests malignancy or bone disease, unrelated here. Isolated macrocytic anemia (option D) points to B12/folate deficiency. Importantly, early serology is frequently negative, so a compatible clinical picture plus this lab pattern justifies continued empiric doxycycline while convalescent titers are obtained.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If untreated or treated late, which life-threatening complication is most characteristic of severe disease?
options: Diffuse small-vessel vasculitis causing noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, encephalitis, and skin necrosis/gangrene
Chronic destructive arthritis of the knees
Ascending flaccid paralysis from demyelination
Progressive scarring alopecia
stages.explanation: RMSF causes a systemic small-vessel vasculitis from endothelial infection, which in severe cases leads to increased vascular permeability with noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, encephalitis/cerebral edema, acute kidney injury, and digital or limb necrosis/gangrene, so option A is correct. Chronic destructive knee arthritis (option B) is characteristic of untreated late Lyme disease. Ascending flaccid paralysis from demyelination (option C) describes Guillain-Barré syndrome. Scarring alopecia (option D) is unrelated to rickettsial vasculitis. The vasculitic, multiorgan nature of severe RMSF underscores why immediate doxycycline and supportive intensive care are critical, with skin necrosis reflecting microvascular thrombosis in the most severe presentations.

pearls: Suspect RMSF with fever, severe headache, and a petechial rash that starts at the wrists/ankles and spreads centrally to the palms and soles after tick exposure.
Start doxycycline empirically and immediately—do not wait for serology, which is often negative in the first week.
The classic lab triad is thrombocytopenia, hyponatremia, and transaminitis, frequently with a normal WBC.
Severe disease is a small-vessel vasculitis causing pulmonary edema, encephalitis, AKI, and digital necrosis—mortality rises sharply with treatment delay.

cases.title: Expanding red ring on the thigh after a hike
cases.chiefComplaint: An enlarging red ring on the thigh with mild flu-like symptoms over the past week.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a slowly enlarging red rash on her right thigh that she first noticed about a week ago. It started as a small red spot and has expanded to a large oval ring with some central clearing; it is warm but only mildly itchy and not painful. Over the past few days she has had low-grade fever, fatigue, headache, and diffuse muscle and joint aches. She went hiking in wooded areas of southern Connecticut about ten days ago and recalls brushing through tall grass; she does not specifically remember a tick bite but did not perform a thorough skin check. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No drug allergies. She is married, works as a teacher, does not smoke, and drinks occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. She denies new medications, recent travel outside the northeastern United States, and any prior similar rash, and reports no shortness of breath, palpitations, or facial weakness at this time.
cases.exam: On the right proximal thigh there is a single 14 cm, well-demarcated, erythematous, annular patch with partial central clearing, giving a targetoid appearance; the lesion is warm and minimally tender, without significant scale, vesicles, or pustules. No additional similar lesions are seen elsewhere on a full skin survey. There is no regional lymphangitic streaking and only mild inguinal lymphadenopathy. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm without bradycardia or new murmurs. Cranial nerve exam, including facial nerve, is intact. There is no joint effusion, and the neurologic exam is otherwise normal. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes this rash from a bacterial cellulitis or a fungal (tinea) infection?
options: Marked tenderness, purulence, and rapidly advancing warmth
A slowly expanding annular patch with central clearing, minimal scale, and only mild symptoms after outdoor exposure in an endemic area
A scaly, well-defined plaque with a raised active border that responds to topical antifungals
Honey-colored crusting with regional lymphangitis
stages.explanation: A slowly expanding, large annular erythematous patch with central clearing and minimal scale, accompanied by mild flu-like symptoms after outdoor exposure in a Lyme-endemic region, is characteristic of erythema migrans, so option B is correct. Marked tenderness, purulence, and rapid advance (option A) suggest bacterial cellulitis, which is typically painful and progresses faster. A scaly plaque with a raised active border responding to antifungals (option C) describes tinea corporis, which usually has more scale and is smaller. Honey-colored crusting with lymphangitis (option D) describes impetigo/streptococcal spread. Erythema migrans is a clinical diagnosis; in an endemic area it warrants treatment without waiting for serology, which is often negative early.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: In this patient with a classic skin finding in an endemic area, what is the most appropriate diagnostic approach?
options: Diagnose clinically and treat; serology is not required for classic erythema migrans
Order two-tier serology (ELISA then Western blot) and withhold treatment until results return
Perform a punch biopsy for culture before any treatment
Obtain a KOH preparation of the lesion edge
stages.explanation: Classic erythema migrans in a patient from an endemic area is a clinical diagnosis that should be treated empirically without serologic confirmation, so option A is correct; early antibody testing is frequently falsely negative and delays care. Ordering two-tier serology and withholding treatment (option B) is incorrect because treatment should not wait, and early serology may miss the diagnosis. Punch biopsy for culture (option C) is low-yield and unnecessary for a classic lesion. KOH prep (option D) evaluates for dermatophytes, which is not the concern here. Serology is reserved for later disseminated manifestations (e.g., arthritis, carditis, neurologic disease) where antibodies have had time to develop.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line oral treatment for this non-pregnant adult with early localized disease?
options: Doxycycline for 10 days (amoxicillin or cefuroxime as alternatives)
Topical mupirocin to the rash
A single intramuscular dose of benzathine penicillin
Oral fluconazole for 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Early localized Lyme disease (erythema migrans) is treated with oral doxycycline, with amoxicillin or cefuroxime as alternatives (e.g., in pregnancy or doxycycline intolerance), so option A is correct; doxycycline also covers possible Anaplasma co-infection. Topical mupirocin (option B) treats superficial bacterial skin infection and does not eradicate Borrelia. A single IM dose of benzathine penicillin (option C) is the treatment for early syphilis, not Lyme disease. Oral fluconazole (option D) is an antifungal and has no role. Counseling on tick-bite prevention and prompt tick removal is also appropriate, and patients should be advised that the rash may take days to fade after starting antibiotics.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If left untreated, which manifestation is a recognized feature of early disseminated or late disease caused by this organism?
options: Cranial neuropathy (e.g., facial palsy), AV block/carditis, or migratory arthritis (often of the knee)
Permanent scarring alopecia of the scalp
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
Recurrent painless genital ulcers
stages.explanation: Untreated Lyme disease can disseminate, causing cranial neuropathies such as facial nerve palsy (sometimes bilateral), Lyme carditis with variable-degree AV block, and later a migratory mono- or oligoarthritis classically affecting the knee, so option A is correct. Permanent scarring alopecia (option B) is not a Lyme manifestation. COPD (option C) is unrelated and caused by smoking or chronic airway disease. Recurrent painless genital ulcers (option D) suggest syphilis or HSV, not Lyme. Recognizing these later manifestations is important because they may be the presenting feature when an early erythema migrans rash was missed; serology becomes useful at these stages.

pearls: Erythema migrans is a slowly expanding annular patch with central clearing and minimal scale—diagnose clinically in endemic areas and treat without serology.
Early two-tier serology is often falsely negative; reserve it for disseminated manifestations (neurologic, cardiac, arthritic).
First-line treatment is oral doxycycline (amoxicillin or cefuroxime as alternatives), which also covers possible Anaplasma co-infection.
Untreated disease can disseminate to facial palsy, AV-block carditis, and a migratory arthritis classically involving the knee.

cases.title: Rapidly spreading dark blotches and collapse in a college student
cases.chiefComplaint: A few hours of high fever, severe headache, and rapidly spreading dark skin blotches with light-headedness.
cases.history: A 19-year-old male college student is brought to the emergency department by roommates for several hours of high fever, severe headache, vomiting, and a rapidly spreading rash.
